DEPARTMENT OF THE NAVY
  FISCAL YEAR (FY) 2026
   BUDGET ESTIMATES




JUSTIFICATION OF ESTIMATES
         June 2025

Operation and Maintenance, Navy
The estimated cost of this report for the Department of the Navy
(DON) is $480,698. The estimated total cost for supporting the
DON budget justification material is approximately $3,113,561
during the 2025 fiscal year. This includes $202,708 in supplies
and $2,910,853 in labor.

The Operation and Maintenance, Navy (O&M,N) appropriation finances the day-to-day costs of training and operating naval forces,
including fuel, supplies, and maintenance of ships, Navy and Marine Corps aircraft, related equipment and weapon systems, and the
support establishment ashore. The primary focus of the Department's FY 2026 budget request is to revive the warrior ethos and
restore trust in our military, rebuild our military by matching threats to capabilities and reestablish deterrence by defending our
homeland.

The FY 2026 estimate of $74,080 million includes a price increase of $1,106 million. This price change primarily results from
purchases from revolving funds, and operation and maintenance of equipment. The budget request reflects the Nation’s priorities,
guided by a hierarchy of strategic doctrines. The Navy and Marine Corps team integrate resources across disparate domains and
elements of national power to deter adversaries and campaign forward. This approach will deliver the integrated deterrence at sea
required by the Interim National Defense Strategic Guidance (INDSG). The budget reflects the consistent priorities of Columbia
Nuclear strategic deterrence, readiness, modernization/capabilities, and capable capacity in order to implement the INDSG and builds
upon force design initiatives. People are the foundational strength of this Department, and the budget invests in quality-of-life
initiatives for Sailors, Marines, and Civilians.

The FY 2026 request delivers the resources necessary to operate, build, train, and equip the naval forces necessary to preserve the
peace, respond in crisis, and win decisively in war. Secretary Phelan has a clear strategic vision centered on three priorities:
strengthen maritime industrial base, fostering a warfighter culture, and improving sailor and marine well-being. The Chief of Naval
Operations priorities focus on enhancing readiness, strengthening maritime industrial base and improving quality of service for
sailors. The Commandant of the Marine Corps’ Force Design is a modernization effort meeting the demands of the INSG, with the
Marine Corps remaining the Nation’s expeditionary crisis response force.

Overseas Operations costs accounted for in the Budget: $779 million. Overseas Operations Costs were financed with former
Overseas Contingency Operations (OCO) funding including Operation Atlantic Resolve, Combined Joint Task Force - Horn of
Africa, Operation INHERENT RESOLVE, Operation ENDURING SENTINEL, Counterterrorism, and Security Cooperation.

           Operating Forces

The Operating Forces budget activity funds Navy and Marine Corps air operations and maintenance, ship operations and depot
maintenance, combat operations/support, weapons support, and shore infrastructure programs. Included in this budget activity are the
costs associated to support U.S. Indo-Pacific Command headquarters. Also included are the funds required to maintain combat ready
forces necessary to respond to national objectives in joint, naval and combined operations. It supports the forward presence and crisis
response capabilities of the INDSG.

The FY 2026 budget estimate of $63,985 million includes a price increase of $819 million and a net program decrease of $221 million.

Major program changes include:

                                                                                        Air Operations: FY 2026 funds the
                                                                                        maximum affordable flight hours and flight
                                                                                        training to enable Carrier and Expeditionary
                                                                                        Strike Group power projections and
                                                                                        dynamic employment. The FY 2026 PB
                                                                                        submission provides 944K flying hours,
                                                                                        providing operations, maintenance, and
                                                                                        training for 9 active Navy Carrier Air Wings
                                                                                        and 3 Marine Corps Air Wings to enable
                                                                                        carrier and expeditionary strike group power
                                                                                        projection. Total flying hours increase from
                                                                                        FY 2025 due to higher planned operational
                                                                                        tempo and aircraft sustainment and
                                                                                        availability efforts throughout the Navy and
Marine Corps. Cost per flight hour (CPH) increases slightly, from $11,530 per hour in FY 2025 to $12,083 per hour in FY 2026, with
cost growth in repairable parts, consumables, and maintenance contracts. The entire naval aviation enterprise (NAE) is acutely aware



                                                                                                                            PBA-19 Appropriation Highlights
8
of the burgeoning costs required to sustain air operations and remains focused on increasing lethality and readiness through process
improvement and driving down cost at all levels of the organization. Mission capable rates have decreased in FY 2025, and the NAE is
committed to reversing this trend in close coordination with partners across the DON and industry.

Ship Operations: In FY 2026, Ship Operations funds 25,748 steaming days across a fleet of 288 ships. The Operating Tempo
(OPTEMPO) supports 58 underway days per quarter for deployed ships and 24 days per quarter for non-deployed ships. With the
inclusion of $1.9 billion in Reconciliation funds, the account is funded to 100% of the modeled requirement. This includes full
funding for the critical readiness basket—OPTAR—which covers repair parts, consumable maintenance items, and travel for training.
FY 2026 also reflects the transfer of Military Sealift Command (MSC) Maintenance and Repair (M&R) to Ship Maintenance (1B4B).
Additionally, Ship Operations now includes support for Landing Craft, Air Cushion (LCAC), Landing Craft, Utility (LCU), and
hospital ship operations (USNS Mercy and USNS Comfort). Without Reconciliation funding, the Navy would face limited
operational flexibility—reducing the fleet’s ability to respond across all theaters, particularly in support of great power competition in
Europe and the Pacific.




                                                                                                                                                                                              PBA-19 Appropriation Highlights
9
Ship Maintenance: The FY 2026 program increases by $1.9 billion over the PB 2025
request, funding 97% of the total requirement and supporting 57 Chief Naval Operations
(CNO) availabilities across public and private sectors. This increase is primarily driven by the
transfer of Military Sealift Command (MSC) Maintenance and Repair (M&R) into the
account. FY 2026 budget requests funding for workload planning and contracting strategies
that provide industry with consistent, reliably funded work. The budget also sustains investments in
naval shipyard capacity, increasing full-time equivalent (FTE) levels to 36,488 and continuing
targeted wage increases in critical trades to address high attrition and retain skilled industrial talent.
Additionally, the Navy continues to use Other Procurement, Navy (OPN) funds for private-
sector ship maintenance, supporting 27 CNO availabilities across U.S. Pacific Fleet and U.S.
Fleet Forces Command. This OPN investment builds on previous success, offering greater stability for industry partners
and reinforcing the Navy’s commitment to financial discipline and efficient program execution.


Facilities Sustainment Restoration and Modernization: Reduced FY
2026 funding levels barely sustain the Navy’s shore infrastructure. If
enacted, the FY 2026 Reconciliation Bill will fund Unaccompanied
Housing Sustainment at 100% of the modeled requirement and overall
Sustainment at 85%. To support the Shipyard Infrastructure Optimization
Program (SIOP), the Navy has earmarked $159 million to recapitalize
public shipyards, and the Reconciliation Bill proposes an additional $830
million to support SIOP initiatives. The Navy continues to face a facilities
maintenance backlog against a plant replacement value of $475 billion.

FY2025 funding includes $1.5B in disaster recovery supplemental
funding. $1.7B in FY2026 has been requested via Reconciliation Bill.

Combat Operations/Support: Funding in Combat Operations/ Support sustains a vast array of programs that support and maintain
combat ready forces necessary to respond to national objectives in Joint, Naval, and combined operations. Combat Support accounts
include increases in Ship Self Defense Systems, Longhaul, Fixed Surveillance Systems (FSS), Tactical Training Ranges and Tactical
Training Groups, and USINDOPACOM, specifically in Pacific Multi-Domain Training & Experimentation Capability (PMTEC)
support to war gaming analysis.




                                                                                                                         PBA-19 Appropriation Highlights
10
Naval Expeditionary Combat Command increases include funding to support Naval Facilities Engineering & Expeditionary Warfare
Center supporting Mobile Diving and Salvage Units, Naval Construction Forces, Navy Expeditionary Combat Forces, and other
Mission Support programs specifically in the INDOPACIFIC area of responsibility.

Medical Readiness increases in Combat Operations/Support are primarily due to the realignment and consolidation of Navy Medical
Readiness resources to ensure consistency with other Services. This includes support for Expeditionary Health Services Systems
(EHSS), medical readiness education and training, medical operational programs, medical facilities maintenance, medical equipment,
and treatment facility support.

The Navy continues to invest in cyberspace capabilities, including training, manning, and equipping cyber mission forces and
activities to strengthen our ability to defend the network through information assurance activities. The Navy increased funding for
cyber operations supporting critical assets and naval defense operations, Cyber Mission Forces Individual Training, Cyber Protection
Team Deployable Mission Sustainment Systems, Information Systems Security and Naval Identity Services. Additionally, the
Department of the Navy transferred mission, funding and civilian manpower to U.S. Cyber Command beginning in FY2024 to
consolidate cyber mission force capabilities, funding, and personnel under USCYBERCOM in order for USCYBERCOM to have
control and authority of the Department’s joint cyber mission force.


Weapons Support: funding supports a comprehensive package of functions required to field, maintain, and ensure the operational
readiness and capability of weapon systems. This includes everything from initial design and development to long-term sustainment,
encompassing not just the physical weapon systems themselves but also all related equipment, materials, services, personnel, and
means of delivery.

Fleet Ballistic Missiles: The Navy continues to support the throughput for receipt, disassembly, test, alteration, test, storage, and
reissue of missiles and missile guidance components. Increases for critical infrastructure to support testing, civilian personnel to
support the D5 Life extension and W93/MK7 programs, Missile Processing program, Conventional Prompt Strike program and the
Transit Protection program.

Weapons Maintenance: The Navy continues to support a wide range of weapons systems. Increases are associated with the Ordnance
maintenance/rework, Littoral and Mine Warfare, specifically support funding for the deployment of additional Mine Countermeasures
Unmanned Surface Vehicles (MCMUSVs), engineering support, and sustainment funding for the MQ-9 Reaper program




                                                                                                                            PBA-19 Appropriation Highlights
11
Enterprise Information Technology: The Navy continues to invest in to support Enterprise Information Technology. Next
Generation Enterprise Network (NGEN) supports Microsoft Office, Advanced Threat Protection (zero-day threat and malware) and
Advanced Security Management. Funding for Navy Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) increases due to requirements for
modernization, audit compliance, and continued command migrations. Additional increases support growth the Navy
Jupiter/ADVANA user base and capabilities, and electronic Procurement System (ePS) sustainment.

           Budget Activity 2:
                                    1,670         -1         770          2,439          151         -811          1,779
             Mobilization

The Mobilization budget activity maintains assets that will support forces that rapidly respond to contingencies throughout the world.
Also funded are the maintenance, overhaul, and calibration requirements of Navy-Type Navy-Owned (NTNO) equipment installed on
Coast Guard ships and aircraft. Additionally, this program funds the inactivation of ships, submarines, and aircraft and includes the
maintenance of selected inactive ships and aircraft as well as material disposal costs.

The FY 2026 budget estimate of $1,779 million includes a price increase of $151 million and program decrease of $811 million.

Major program changes include:

Ready Reserve and Prepositioning Forces: The Prepositioning Forces Activity Group funds the operating costs of 13 (down from
16 in FY25) Prepositioning ships and funds changes in Reduced Operating Status (ROS)/Full operating Status (FOS) changes within
the Prepositioning Fleet. The Ready Reserve Force funding line funds support the additional maintenance and modernization of one
New Used Vessels in FY 2026, as well as five Large Medium Speed Roll-on-Roll-off vessels from the Army bringing the Ready
Reserve Sealift Program to 54 at the end of FY 2026.

Activations/Inactivations: The current plan is to inactivate 14 ships in FY26, 5 of which are being inactivated prior to End of
Service Life (ESL).

Additionally, in FY25 the Inactivation budget included a program increase of $645M in Nuclear Surface Ship Inactivation/Disposal
Program, mainly due to the inactivation and disposal contract for the USS Enterprise (CVN-65). This one-time increase specific to
the Enterprise was not required in FY26 resulting in an overall program decrease.

Reduction in Mobilization resources is partially attributed to the realignment and consolidation of Navy Medical Readiness resources
under Combat Operations/Support to ensure consistency with other Services.




                                                                                                                           PBA-19 Appropriation Highlights

                                                            ($ in Millions)
                                  FY 2024      Price      Program       FY 2025       Price      Program       FY 2026
                                   Actual     Growth      Growth        Enacted      Growth      Growth        Estimate1
           Budget Activity 3:
             Training and          2,806         65          -21         2,851          47          -356          2,542
              Recruiting

The Training and Recruiting budget activity funds all centrally managed or directed individual training required to meet Navy training
standards. This includes accession training including the United States Naval Academy (USNA) and Navy Reserve Officer Training
Corps (ROTC), basic skills training, and professional military education provided at the Naval War College, and the Naval
Postgraduate School. It also includes Navy recruiting and advertising, centralized civilian training programs, and the Junior Navy
Reserve Officer Training Corps.

The FY 2026 budget estimate of $2,542 million includes a price increase of $47 million and program decrease of $365 million.

Accessions Training: refers to the initial military training provided to new recruits entering the Department of Defense (DOD)
services, as well as the training for officers entering service through various programs like the Officer Training School (OTS) and
Reserve Officers' Training Corps (ROTC). This training encompasses basic training for enlisted personnel and specialized training for
officers, aiming to instill military culture, essential skills, and leadership development.

The Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps (NROTC) program produces unrestricted line Navy and Marine Corps officers. It
provides college instruction to highly qualified baccalaureate degree students who, upon graduation, receive a commission in the
Navy, Marine Corps or associated reserve program. Resources increased by $3.7 million due to a simultaneous increase for
scholarships and reductions to civilian personnel and support, in order to align with President's Directives to enhance government
efficiency.

Basic Skill and Advanced Training: The Navy offers a variety of training programs for service members, both basic and advanced,
to prepare them for their military careers and for civilian life after their service.

The Specialized Skill Training reduction of $187.9M is primarily due to a reduction of fielding and contract supports for the Ready
Relevant Learning (RRL) program, a transformational Navy training initiative that accelerates the learning of every Sailor for faster
response to our rapidly changing warfighting requirements.




                                                                                                                           PBA-19 Appropriation Highlights
14
Professional Development Education prepares career officers for more demanding assignments, particularly command and staff
positions. A $75.4 million program decrease is highly attributed to the realignment of medical training and education resources to BA
1, Operating Forces funding. The reduction due to the transfer is partially offset by the realignment of the Naval Community College
to the Professional Development Education budget activity and increases to the Naval War College to support war gaming expansion,
modeling, and simulation system integration.

Training Support provides the Battle Force Tactical Training (BFTT) Program, which contains the following Force Structure
Elements: Training Systems (BFTT), Fleet Synthetic Training (FST), Surface Training Advance Virtual Environment (STAVE), and
Total Ship Training Capability (TSTC) Live Virtual & Constructive (LVC) Sustainment. A program increase of $40.6 million is a
result of realignments to support the development of training curriculum, STAVE, Aegis Ashore Team Trainer, and other training
system modernization and sustainment efforts.

Recruiting and Other Training and Education: The Navy has comprehensive programs for recruiting, training, and education of
its personnel, both military and civilian and is committed to recruiting a qualified workforce and providing them with essential
training and education opportunities, addressing challenges in a constantly evolving environment.

Recruiting and Other Training and Education is reduced by approximately $65.2M in FY 2026. This reduction is primarily due to the
elimination of the Tuition Assistance (TA) program and other Off-Duty and Voluntary Education programs, approximate 50%
reduction to the Navy Junior Reserve Officer Corps (NJROTC) program, and reductions to civilian personnel and support, in order to
align with Presidential Directive’s to enhance government efficiency. These reductions are partially offset by the increases to
Advertising and Recruiting efforts.

Reduction in Training and Recruiting resources is partially attributed to the realignment and consolidation of Navy Medical Readiness
resources under Combat Operations/Support to ensure consistency with other Services.




                                                                                                                         PBA-19 Appropriation Highlights

               Budget Activity 4:
               Administrative and
                                          6,680        227          -822        6,085          89          -400        5,775
                 Service-wide
                  Activities

The Administration and Servicewide Support budget activity funds shore-based activities required for the effective operation of the
Department of the Navy. The general services provided include administration and personnel support costs, engineering and
acquisition support, security and investigative support, humanitarian and civic support, and centralized transportation and
communications costs.

The FY 2026 budget estimate of $5,775 million includes a price increase of $89 million and program decreases of $400 million.

Major program changes include:

Administration: Funding in Administration sustains a vast array of programs that support the direct staff offices of the Secretary of
the Navy and the Chief of Naval Operations. It also resources the headquarters staffs of Navy operational and systems commands, and
centrally managed programs on behalf of the DON. Administration accounts include increases in the Defense Finance and Accounting
Services (DFAS) support, civilian personnel and support funding for the United States Naval Community College (USNCC), and the
DON Chief Information Officer (CIO) Information Superiority Vision (ISV) Implementation.

Military Manpower and Personnel Management: The Department continues efforts in Personnel and Pay transformation in
foundational data integration, access, and transactional elements for HR management and support activities through five lines of
effort, leading to Full Operational Capability by FY 2028. The FY 2026 decrease is mainly due to the reductions in contractor support
for the MyNavy Career Centers and continued transition from development to sustainment of the Enterprise Customer Relationship
Management system (an HR support system), the consolidation of the legacy systems into the new Authoritative Data Environment
and increased functional support across the My Navy HR portfolio.

Servicewide Transportation: Service wide Transportation (SWT) provides centralized funding for transportation and cargo
movement. Funding increase to sustain the operational volume and demand of transportation services to meet national security



                                                                                                                          PBA-19 Appropriation Highlights
16
objectives in peace and war. Increase also supports mandatory high-priority quality of life support for overseas mail and NEXCOM.
The program's volume is driven by a variety of factors, the most significant ones being operating tempo and warfighting readiness.

Planning, Engineering, and Program Support: Planning, Engineering, and Program Support provides funding for acquisition,
planning, engineering, design, environmental protection, and physical security programs. Decrease is largely attributed to the
reductions in Civilian personnel and Advisory & Assistance Services implementing the President’s Department of Government
Efficiency Workforce Optimization Initiative.

Investigative and Security Services: The Department will relentlessly pursue a force where no one ever feels unsafe and our
consecutive request for increased funding in our SAPR programs is proof of that. The growth in this budget will continue to
implement the Independent Review Commission’s (IRC) recommendations related to accountability, prevention, climate, culture, and
victim care and support. Additional funds are added to further focus on strengthening Sexual Harassment response efforts.

Reduction in Administration and Service-Wide Activities resources is partially attributed to the realignment and consolidation of Navy
Medical Readiness resources under Combat Operations/Support to ensure consistency with other Services.

                                                                             Department of the Navy
                                                                    FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                       Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                       Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                         Activity Group: Air Operations
                                                        Detail by Subactivity Group: Mission and Other Flight Operations
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Mission and Other Flight Operations include all Navy and Marine Corps Tactical Air (TACAIR) and Anti-Submarine Warfare forces, shore-based Fleet Air Support, and irregular
warfare. Funding provides flying hours to maintain required levels of readiness, enabling Navy and Marine Corps aviation forces to perform their primary missions as required in
support of national objectives. In addition, the Flying Hour Support program provides funding for transportation for equipment, travel for personnel, aircrew training systems,
commercial air services, and various information technology systems. These support accounts enable training for and execution of primary missions.

The Navy measures aviation readiness using the Defense Readiness Reporting System. Carrier Air Wings (CVWs) maintain varied training and readiness (T&R) levels in
accordance with the Optimized Fleet Response Training Plan (OFRTP), providing adequately trained aircrews across a 36-month deployment cycle.

Marine Corps TACAIR readiness differs in approach and requires maintaining a steady readiness profile for rapid and effective deployment on short notice in support of
operational plans or contingency operations. The Marine Corps Aviation Plan (AVPLAN) directs the T&R requirements and resources to attain readiness levels over a 12-month
snapshot of a USMC 36-month squadron training cycle. The AVPLAN aligns with Department requirements by implementing a comprehensive, capabilities-based training system
that provides mission skill-proficient crews and combat readiness to the Combatant Commanders.


II. Force Structure Summary:

In FY 2024, there are 9 active carrier air wings, 2,396 crews, and 1,887 tactical primary mission aircraft (PMAI).
In FY 2025, there are 9 active carrier air wings, 2,310 crews, and 2,129 tactical primary mission aircraft (PMAI).
In FY 2026, there are 9 active carrier air wings, 2,370 crews, and 2,123 tactical primary mission aircraft (PMAI).

                                                                             Department of the Navy
                                                                     FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                         Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                          Activity Group: Air Operations
                                                                  Detail by Subactivity Group: Fleet Air Training
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Fleet Air Training includes Fleet Replacement Squadrons (FRS) located throughout the country which train replacement aircrews for each Navy and Marine Corps
type/model/series in weapons tactics, weapons delivery, and carrier landing qualifications. Tactical Air/Anti-Submarine Warfare force level requirements establish student levels
by authorized, aircrew personnel rotation rates, and student output from the Undergraduate Pilot/Navy Flight Officer Training Program. Flight hours also facilitate specialized
schools such as the Naval Aviation Warfighting Development Center (NAWDC) for adversary support. Fleet Air Training includes the material costs to support simulator
maintenance.
Fleet Air Training also includes funding for Chief of Naval Air Training (CNATRA) flight operations for undergraduate pilot and flight officer training, and test pilot-transition
training. Undergraduate pilot training spans multiple fiscal years. The longest training pipeline is T-45 Goshawk pilot training at 87 weeks in duration. Flight operations consist
of fuel, flight gear, parts and material support for organizational and intermediate maintenance, aviation depot level repairables, and contractor maintenance support. Flight hours
for each fiscal year are calculated based on phase output requirements for undergraduate pilot and flight officer production rates prescribed by the Chief of Naval Operations.
Funds for flight support include academic training, contractor maintenance of training simulators, contract flight simulator instructors, consumable supplies, civilian salaries and
operating costs for CNATRA headquarters and Training Air Wings.
Also included in Fleet Air Training is funding for the Navy's Flight Demonstration Squadron (NFDS). The NFDS trains for and conducts aerial flight demonstrations across the
United States to enhance recruiting and increase public awareness of the U.S. Navy and other military departments.


II. Force Structure Summary:
There are 18 Fleet Replacement Squadrons (7 USMC, 11 USN), the Navy's Flight Demonstration Squadron (NFDS), and the Naval Flight Training Program conducted by five
training air wings (TW): (1) TW-1 at NAS Meridian, MS, (2) TW-2 at NAS Kingsville, TX, (3) TW-4 at NAS Corpus Christi, TX, (4) TW-5 at NAS Whiting Field, FL, and (5)
TW-6 at NAS Pensacola, FL.
In addition, Fleet Air Training supports seven major programs:

1.       United States Naval Test Pilot School (USNTPS) – USNTPS trains fixed wing, rotary wing, and airborne & unmanned systems students to become qualified Developmental
         Flight Test Pilots and Flight Officers.

2.       Fleet Electronic Warfare Systems Group (FEWSG) - FEWSG provides Electronic Warfare (EW) training aircraft and associated contracting and engineering services to
         supply unique commercial turbo fan EW training assets. Specifically, the FEWSG program utilizes the High Endurance Electronic Warfare Jet (HEEWJ), Electronic
         Warfare (EW) Jets, and Stand-Off Jamming (SOJ) contracts to emulate evolving threats via modified fixed wing aircraft. Gulfstream G-IV aircraft and Learjets are
         configured as required to simulate hostile EW threats for air-to-air and air-to-surface training events in support of Fleet training and battle group exercises.

3.       Range Support (RS) - Provides logistics for training range systems, maintenance and operating costs of scoring systems, depot repair of equipment for Fleet Training Ranges,
         and configuration management support of range systems and for software sustainment of fielded range systems. Major systems include Tactical Aircrew Combat Training
         Systems (TACTS), Large Area Tracking Range (LATR), Ocean Systems, Electronic Warfare (EW) Threat, Weapons Scoring Systems, Laser Training Systems, Navy
         Tactical Data Systems and Tactical Combat Training System (TCTS). This program also supports Fleet Support Team initiatives to ensure part obsolescence issue
         identification and resolution.



                                                                                                                                                              Exhibit OP-5, 1A2A
         49                                                                                                                                                         (Page 1 of 13)
                                                                          Department of the Navy
                                                                  FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                      Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                      Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                       Activity Group: Air Operations
                                                               Detail by Subactivity Group: Fleet Air Training
4.   Training Equipment Operations and Maintenance (TE) – Supports operation and maintenance of training equipment prior to training agent acceptance, in-service
     maintenance of training devices, training course revision and maintenance, and other support related to training devices and courseware.

5.   Aerial Targets – Provides support for Aerial Targets by maintaining target support equipment, transporting targets, creating and updating operator and maintenance technical
     publications, creating and updating training material for aerial target maintenance, building targets which include rocket assist installation and target engine/rocket testing.
     This also includes launching, flying, and recovering targets. The mission of the target maintenance program is to improve, maintain and operate threat representative
     equipment to ensure maximum combat readiness to US Naval Surface Combatants.

6.   DoN Terminal Attack Controller Trainer (TACT) – Provides contracted trainer aircraft services for close air and offensive air support to Expeditionary Warfare Training
     Group Pacific and Atlantic, USMC Marine Expeditionary Force Ground and Air Major Subordinate Commands, and Marine Aviation Weapons and Tactics Squadron One
     as well as Navy Joint Terminal Attack Controller (JTAC) generation requirements at Naval Aviation Warfighting Development Center (NAWDC). It also provides
     management, contracting and engineering oversight for contracted air trainers to geographically dispersed training sites to augment live fleet training asset support. The
     TACT contract provides around 1,520 sorties capable of employing ordnance and targeting pods in day and night flight conditions in support of Naval JTAC, Forward Air
     Controller (FAC), and Forward Air Controller (Airborne) (FAC(A)) certification and qualification Training and Readiness requirements.

7.   Fleet Replacement Squadron Contract Maintenance Support - Provides Contract Maintenance Support (CMS) personnel to augment organizational level (O-level)
     maintenance. It provides on-site support for aircraft maintenance, scheduled and unscheduled phase maintenance, flight schedule requirements, pilot production and all
     related Naval Aviation Maintenance Program (NAMP) support at Fleet Replacement Squadrons/Detachments (FRSs/FRDs).

                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Aviation Technical Data and Engineering Services
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Engineering Technical Services (ETS) - This program provides on-site technical information, instruction and training (formal and on the job) to aviation maintenance personnel at
the organizational and intermediate levels of maintenance for a network of 25 detachments dispersed worldwide. The purpose o f the training is to elevate the technical knowledge
and skills of Navy and Marine aviation maintenance technicians in the installation, maintenance, repair, and operation of all types of aviation equipment and associated support
equipment. Engineering Technical Services (ETS) are performed by Contractor Engineering Technical Services (CETS) and Navy Engineering Technical Services ( NETS)
personnel. Naval.Air Technical Data and Engineering Services Command (NATEC) administrative functions include: technical man agement, data distnbution, digitization and
conversion, Fleet liaison, customer service, ETS management/administration and corporate operations. NATEC is Naval aviation's authoritative source for technical manuals,
technical directives, technical data packages (TDP), and integrated logistics documentation. NATEC provides fleet maintainers, Naval Supply Systems Command (NAVSUP) ,
Defense Logistics Agency (DLA), F oreign Military Sales (FMS), intemational partners, and other customers 24/7 access to the technical data needed to maintain and operate their
weapons systems, perform manufacturing, and order repair parts.

In FY25 the Aviation Technical Data and Engineering Services line item was disestablished. The operations financed in this line are consolidated with other Aviation Support
efforts and reflected in the 1A4N budget exhibit.




II. Force Structure Summary:
Engineering Technical Services (ETS) tasks are in support ofsustaining the current readiness and mobilization capability of the Naval Active Force. The Naval Air Technical Data
and Engineering Services Command (NATEC) facility responds to customer demands and ensures that Fleet users oftechnical documentation are working with accurate and current
information to maintain fleet readiness. NATEC responds to ensure that requirements for Fleet Engineering Technical Services are satisfied.

                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                          Activity Group: Air Operations
                                                          Detail by Subactivity Group: Air Operations and Safety Support
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Air Operations and Safety Support consists of seven major programs.

The Expeditionary Airfields (EAF) program supports airfield matting refurbishment, in-service engineering, life-cycle management, logistical and technical efforts, and fleet direct
and technical support for expeditionary airfields.

The Aviation Facilities and Landing Aids program supports improvements in shore-based landing aids, installation of the Naval Air Systems Command (NAVAIR) provided
equipment, development/revision of aviation facilities planning and design criteria, and NAVAIR facilities and management fun ctions.

The Aviation Mobile Facilities program supports aviation mobile facility configurations for the Navy and Marine Corps.

The Aviation Life Support Systems program provides in-service basic design engineering and logistics management support for over 1,100 Aircrew Systems and Flight Deck
products for the total life cycle. The Aircrew Systems products are essential to aircrew performance, safety and survival. These products include clothing and equipment for all
aircrew, maintainers, and passengers involved in and supported by Naval Aviation. These products are designed to support per formance in all phases of flight for all aviation
missions, including vision protection from LASERs, night vision, and aviation Chemical Biological protection. The escape sys terns provide safe egress from disabled aircraft
(ejection seats, parachutes, helicopter emergency escape devices), survival on land and water (water flotation/life vests, se at survival kits, medical items), and equipment to effect a
successful rescue (survival radios/electronics, rescue slings, hoisting rings).

The Air Traffic Control (ATC) program provides logistics, engineering, and maintenance support for identification and landing systems for facilities ashore and afloat.

The Marine Air Traffic Control and Landing Systems (MATCALS) provides life-cycle support of electronic and other systems used by the Marine Air Traffic Control
Detachments in support of Marine expeditionary forces. Funds the Fleet Support Team (FST), the In-service Engineering Activity, and other supporting activities in maintaining
this equipment in operational condition. Included in the FST tasking is technical and training assists to Fleet units (on-site and telephonic/email), technical manual development
and updates, maintenance tracking programs, engineering investigations, resolution of obsolescence issues, and other tasks re quired to maintain and support the electronic and
other systems used by the Marine Air Traffic Control Detachments. These systems include Airport Surveillance Radars, Precisi on Approach Radars, control and communications
systems, air navigation aids, towers, and the ancillary equipment associated with the systems. The high use and harsh environment encountered during expeditionary operations
necessitate frequent and thorough overhaul of these systems. Funded activities include supply support, procurement agencies, and other life-cycle support requirements.

The Aircraft Launch and Recovery Equipment (ALRE) program provides life-cycle management including launchers, recovery, visual landing aids, information systems, fleet
technical support, and policy management.

The Aviation Foreign Object Damage Mitigation program supports aviation foreign object damage prevention for the Navy and Marine Corps.

Also includes funding for environmental protection, and other miscellaneous operations and services.



In FY25, Air Operations line item was disestablished. The operations financed in this line are consolidated with other Aviation Support efforts and reflected in the 1A4N budget
exhibit. The Aviation Mobile Facilities program operations financed are consolidated in and reflected in both the 1A4N and lASA budget exhibits.
The Aviation Foreign Object Damage Mitigation program operations financed are consolidated and reflected in the 1A9A budget exhibit.

I. Description of Operations Financed:

Aviation Technical Data & Engineering Services

Engineering Technical Services (ETS) - This program provides on-site technical information, instruction and training (formal and on the job) to aviation maintenance personnel at
the organizational and intermediate levels of maintenance for a network of 25 detachments dispersed worldwide. The purpose of the training is to elevate the technical knowledge
and skills of Navy and Marine aviation maintenance technicians in the installation, maintenance, repair and operation of all types of aviation equipment and associated support
equipment. Engineering Technical Services (ETS) are performed by Contractor Engineering Technical Service (CETS) and Navy Engineering Technical Service (NETS)
personnel. Naval Air Technical Data and Engineering Services Center (NATEC) Administrative functions include: technical data management, data distribution, digitization and
conversion, Fleet liaison, Customer Service, ETS management/administration and corporate operations. NATEC is Naval aviation's authoritative source for technical manuals,
technical directives, technical data packages (TDP), and integrated logistics documentation. NATEC provides fleet maintainers, Naval Supply Systems Command (NAVSUP),
Defense Logistics Agency (DLA), Foreign Military Sales (FMS), international partners, and other customers 24/7 access to the technical data needed to maintain and operate their
weapons systems, perform manufacturing, and order repair parts.

Air Operations and Safety Support

The Expeditionary Airfields (EAF) program supports airfield matting refurbishment, in-service engineering, life-cycle management, logistical and technical efforts, and fleet direct
and technical support for expeditionary airfields.

The Aviation Facilities and Landing Aids program supports improvements in shore-based landing aids, installation of the Naval Air Systems Command (NAVAIR) provided
equipment, development/revision of aviation facilities planning and design criteria, and NAVAIR facilities and management functions.

The Aviation Mobile Facilities program supports aviation mobile facility configurations for the Navy and Marine Corps.

The Aviation Life Support Systems program provides in-service basic design engineering and logistics management support for Aircrew Systems and Flight Deck products for the
total life cycle. Examples of Aircrew Systems products that are essential to aircrew safety and survival include clothing and equipment that Navy and Marine Corps aircrew and
passengers need to function within all flight envelopes (helmets and hearing protection, oxygen masks, chemical biological protective equipment, flight suites, gloves, in-flight
personal communications), escape safely from disabled aircraft (ejection seats, parachutes, helicopter emergency escape devices), survive on land and water (water flotation/life
vests, seat survival kits, medical items), and effect a successful rescue (survival radios/electronics, rescue slings, hoisting rings).

The Air Traffic Control (ATC) program provides logistics, engineering, and maintenance support for identification and landing systems for facilities ashore and afloat.

Marine Air Traffic Control and Landing Systems (MATCALS) provides life-cycle maintenance support of electronic and other systems used by the Marine Air Traffic Control
Detachments in support of Marine expeditionary forces. This funding provides for support of the Fleet Support Team (FST), the In-service Engineering Activity, and other
supporting activities in maintaining this equipment in operational condition. Included in the FST mission is technical and training assists to Fleet units (on-site and
telephonic/email), technical manual development and updates, maintenance tracking programs, engineering investigations, resolution of obsolescence issues, and other tasks
required to maintain and support the electronic and other systems used by the Marine Air Traffic Control Detachments. Provides depot maintenance and restoration of electronic
and other systems used by the Marine Air Traffic Control Detachments in support of Marine expeditionary forces. The high use and harsh environment encountered during

                                                                                                                                                             Exhibit OP-5, 1A4N
         79                                                                                                                                                         (Page 1 of 19
                                                                             Department of the Navy
                                                                     FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                          Activity Group: Air Operations
                                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Air Systems Support
expeditionary operations necessitate the frequent and thorough overhaul of these systems. These systems include Airport Surveillance Radars, Precision Approach Radars, control
and communications systems, air navigation aids, towers, and the ancillary equipment associated with the systems. Funded activities include supply support, procurement
agencies, and other life-cycle support requirements.



The Aircraft Launch and Recovery Equipment (ALRE) program provides life-cycle management including launchers, recovery, visual landing aids, information systems, fleet
technical support, and policy management.

This budget includes funding for environmental protection, and other miscellaneous operations and services.

Air Systems Support

Air Systems Support provides funding for engineering and logistics analysis necessary to sustain aircraft systems and equipment across four multi-faceted programs areas. 1)
Product Support - Provides the technical support of over 4,000 in-service aircraft, aircraft systems and related ground support. The technical support consists primarily of
engineers and logisticians responsible for maintaining the safety, readiness and supportability of in-service systems. Products and services include but are not limited to Technical
Directives, Grounding Bulletins (RED STRIPES), Safety Engineering Investigations, Routine Investigations, Technical Publications updates (Interim Rapid Action Changes
(IRACS), Maintenance Requirements Cards (MRC cards), and Manual Change Requests), Engineering Change Proposals/Rapid Action Maintenance Engineering Changes, and
Type/Model/Series (T/M/S) Reliability Centered Maintenance (RCM) analyses. 2) Software Support - Provides for the life cycle sustainment of Operational Flight Program
software to support operational effectiveness and reliability of software intensive weapons systems by sustaining the software, threat databases, and associated engineering support
resources. 3) Multi-Platform Support (Logistics) - Provides support for programs benefiting across the aviation enterprise for Logistics IT systems, support equipment database
management, maintenance planning support, and fleet readiness and reliability data analysis. 4) Multi-Platform Support (Engineering) – Provides support for programs benefiting
across the aviation enterprise for common systems/ structural/basic design engineering services and fatigue life management.

Aircraft Depot Operations Support

Support Services Program provides unscheduled services to the Fleet and is budgeted on the basis of historical level of effort and projected emergent requirements. The program
enhances Fleet readiness by providing expeditious solutions for the correction of unplanned maintenance problems incurred during Fleet operations. Services include Depot
Support Items, Customer Services, Customer Fleet Support, Ferry Flight and Maintenance Support. Depot Support Items include the costs of Salvage to support an immediate
depot requirement, Preservation and De-preservation during a period of idleness prior to induction to rework. Customer Services includes the costs of nonscheduled work to
provide D-level industrial services directly to operating force activities for immediate needs both in hardware rework and technical/engineering services. This service is provided
for the accomplishment of emergency check/test and minor repair of aeronautical material. This service also provides special industrial processes, such as plating, heat treating,
nondestructive testing, and machine shop services. Customer Fleet support includes the costs associated with Customer /Operating Forces Training and Navy Oil Analysis
sampling conducted at the Fleet Readiness Centers (FRC's). A comprehensive aviation maintenance training program is required to provide skilled technicians in the maintenance
and support of the Navy's aircraft weapon systems. Ferry Flights includes the costs of travel, per diem, and fuel associated with bringing aircraft to a rework facility prior to
maintenance (i.e. Standard/Scheduled Depot Level Maintenance (SDLM) / Product Data Management (PDM) / Planned Maintenance Interval (PMI) or retrofit/modernization
efforts or returning aircraft to the Fleet following maintenance. The Ferry Flight program was established because the aircraft may not be returned to the same squadron and are
not being picked up by Fleet pilots. Maintenance Support provides certification criteria for performance of NAVAIR configuration management logistics functions. It provides

                                                                                                                                                              Exhibit OP-5, 1A4N
         80                                                                                                                                                         (Page 2 of 19)
                                                                              Department of the Navy
                                                                       FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                         Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                           Activity Group: Air Operations
                                                                 Detail by Subactivity Group: Air Systems Support
technical directive management and fleet assistance on all aspects of configuration management including Technical Directive Status Accounting (TDSA), Configuration Status
Account (CSA), and updates to Fleet guidance.

Fleet Readiness Center Western Pacific (FRCWP) is tasked with providing quality depot level aviation maintenance for all current and future Navy and Marine Corps Forces
outside the Continental United States (OCONUS). FRCWP provides scheduled maintenance (Integrated Maintenance Plan or IMP) with International Commercial Partners for
eleven Type, Model, Series (TMS) Aircraft to include F-18 models A-G, H-60, H-1, H-53, KC-130 and V-22 airframes; unscheduled maintenance through In-Service Repair (ISR)
for all TMS; and provides Support Equipment (SE) overhaul. This account supports salaries for civilian personnel, PCS costs, tools, equipment, travel, training, transportation,
communication, IT (non-NMCI network), supplies, utilities and operation and administration of facilities. All of FRCWP's activities are based OCONUS with HQ residing at NAF
Atsugi, Japan and Detachments located in Iwakuni, Japan; Okinawa; Busan, Korea; Guam; Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia; USCENTCOM; and aboard deployed Aircraft Carrier
Groups.

Fleet Readiness Center (FRC) provides for the recurring costs associated with Commander, Fleet Readiness Centers (COMFRC) oversight, management, and direct support of the
Fleet Readiness Centers. COMFRC provides governance functions associated with the FRC commands which integrates Intermediate and Depot Level maintenance organizations.
COMFRC is staffed by military and civilian workforce to support FRC operations, ensure current Intermediate and Depot operations receive proper oversight, identify areas for
process improvement to increase readiness and cost efficiency and to communicate status to leadership. In addition to the functions performed on behalf of COMFRC, the
business unit provides direct support to FRC Mid-Atlantic, Patuxent River; FRC West, China Lake; and FRC Aviation Support Equipment management and financial operations in
Navy ERP.

Depot Industrial Support Services funds Naval Air Systems Command civilian workforce labor and minimal support for supplies, travel and other governmental administrative
support.

Fleet Readiness Centers Infrastructure Optimization and Modernization Plan (FIOP) is a concerted effort to modernize the naval aviation depot's aging infrastructure to be prepared
for next generation aircraft and weapon systems to meet the requirements set forth in the 2019 National Defense Authorization Act. FIOP activities include improving readiness of
current systems, replacing equipment, transitioning to new technology, removing old equipment, consolidating equipment/facilities, and outfitting new facilities.



Equipment Maintenance

METCAL sustains Metrology Automated System for Uniform Recall and Reporting (MEASURE), Calibration Standards Asset Management System (CSAMS), and Team Work
Plan Automated Module (TWPAM) applications. Each has a web enabled front-end portal that accesses an ORACLE schema database through NMCI, CANES-21, OneNet,
USMC and other networks. MEASURE provides 80 NAVAIR calibration activities the capability of managing their cognizant inventory, calibration recall, recording calibration
event transactions, printing calibration documents or METCAL Forms (METER Cards), generating standard reports, and Ad- Hoc Reports. CSAMS is used to manage NAVAIR
Calibration Standards (CALSTDs) inventory, including repair, allowance, issue and receipt for NAVAIR calibration activities throughout the life cycle of each CALSTD.
TWPAM provides a comprehensive plan for utilization and execution of METCAL program resources.

Program Executive Office, Aviation Common Systems and Commercial Services – PEO (CS)

Program Executive Office, Aviation Common Systems and Commercial Services (PEO (CS)) achieves acquisition excellence by delivering the aviation common systems, services,
and support to provide our warfighters and partners a decisive edge in defense of our nation. Leveraging common systems and services decreases investment and sustainment costs
and reduces the numbers of configurations in the Fleet.

Program Executive Office Air Anti-Submarine Warfare, Assault & Special Mission – PEO (A)

Program Executive Office, Air, ASW, Assault, and Special Mission Programs (PEO (A)) provides fleet capability and capacity, supporting development and sustainment of Navy
and Marine Corps helicopters, special mission aircraft and aviation anti-submarine warfare equipment and aircraft.

Program Executive Office, Tactical Aircraft Programs – PEO (T)

The Program Executive Office for Tactical Aircraft Programs (PEO (T)) provides full life cycle support of naval aviation aircraft, weapons and systems operated by Sailors and
Marines.

Program Executive Office, Unmanned and Weapons – PEO (U&W)

PEO U&W is the central management authority responsible for the development, production, fielding and sustainment of the Navy's unmanned aircraft, weapons and target
systems.



II. Force Structure Summary:
Aviation Technical Data & Engineering Services
Engineering Technical Services (ETS) tasks are in support of sustaining the current readiness and mobilization capability of the Naval Active Force. The Naval Air Technical Data
and Engineering Services Center (NATEC) facility responds to customer demands and ensures that Fleet users of technical documentation are working with accurate and current
information to maintain fleet readiness. NATEC responds to ensure that requirements for Fleet Engineering Technical Services are satisfied.

Air Systems Support
Air Systems Support provides engineering and logistics support for the Navy and Marine Corps operational inventory of aircraft, associated support equipment, automatic test
equipment, and aircraft systems. These services are performed at Fleet Readiness Centers, Naval Air Warfare Centers, and through private contractors.

Air Operations and Safety Support
Expeditionary Airfields (EAF) support the First, Second, Third, and Fourth Marine Aircraft Wings (MAW).

Aviation Facilities and Landing Aids support all Naval and Marine Corps aviation facilities and shore-based landing aids.
In FY25, 1A4A and 1A6A budget exhibits is consolidated with other Aviation Support efforts and reflected in the 1A4N budget exhibits.
1A6A line item for the Aviation Mobile Facilities program operations financed is consolidated and reflected in the 1A4N budget exhibits.                   Exhibit OP-5, 1A4N
        82                                                                                                                                                       (Page 4 of 19)
                                                                           Department of the Navy
                                                                   FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                      Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                      Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                        Activity Group: Air Operations
                                                              Detail by Subactivity Group: Air Systems Support
Aviation Mobile Facilities support Naval and Marine Corps aviation with over 3,000 configured mobile facilities.

Aviation Life Support Systems provides in-service support functions for over 1,100 aircrew system products that are essential to aircrew safety and survival. Many of these
aircrew system products are essential to maintain life in-flight (e.g., oxygen masks and equipment) and directly impact mission capability status and mission readiness of aircraft.

Air Traffic Control (ATC) supports over 100 Naval and Marine Corps aviation shore facilities worldwide and all aviation/combat ships afloat.

Marine Air Traffic Control and Landing Systems (MATCALS) provide support to ten (10) active and one (1) reserve Marine Air Traffic Control Detachments (MATCD),
components of the Marine Air Control Squadron, Marine Air Control Group, and Marine Aircraft Wing.

Aircraft Launch and Recovery Equipment (ALRE) supports approximately 2,000 systems.

Aircraft Depot Operations Support
Fleet Readiness Center Western Pacific (FRCWP) force structure consist of the civilian and military workforce and Foreign National Indirect Hires who perform the above
functions at FRCWP, NAF Atsugi, Japan, and FRCWP detachments. Production and Operations personnel are assigned to directly support maintenance efforts along with the
support of personnel in areas such as Engineering, Business Operations, Supply, Administrative, IT, Safety, Quality Assurance, and Finance. COMFRC provides governance
functions associated with the FRC commands which integrates Intermediate and Depot level maintenance organizations. COMFRC is staffed by military and civilian workforce to
support FRC operations, and provide proper oversight of current Depot and Intermediate operations. In addition to the functions on behalf of COMFRC, the business unit also
provides direct support to FRC Mid-Atlantic, Patuxent River; FRC West, China Lake; and FRC Aviation Support Equipment (FRC ASE) management and financial operations.
The force structure also includes the civilian workforce of the NAVAIR Sustainment Group Industrial Operations Salary and Support. Aircraft Depot Operations Support is
performed at Fleet Readiness Centers (East, Southeast, and Southwest) in support of the Primary Aircraft Authorized.

                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                          Activity Group: Air Operations
                                                             Detail by Subactivity Group: Aircraft Depot Maintenance
I. Description of Operations Financed:
A. Airframe Rework: This program provides Fleet aircraft inspection, rework and emergent repairs. Through periodic depot level maintenance activities, aircraft major structures
and airframe systems are maintained in a safe flyable condition. Depot maintenance is currently being performed under the Integrated Maintenance Concept (IMC), Phase Depot
Maintenance (PDM) and other scheduled and unscheduled depot maintenance programs. The IMC concept uses Planned Maintenance Intervals (PMI), performing depot
maintenance, with smaller work packages, reducing out of service time. The program's goal is to improve readiness and availability while reducing operating and support costs.
Currently, the AV-8B, C-130, C-2, E-2, EA-18G, FA-18, H-1, H-53, H-60, MV-22, and P-3 and P-8 are supported under IMC. The PDM concept is used with the E-6B, F-16 and
Executive Helicopter aircraft. MQ-4, MQ-8, other cargo, test pilot and trainer aircraft programs are supported under other scheduled rework programs.

B. Engine Rework: The engine rework program accomplishes aircraft engine, engine modules, gearboxes and torque meters repair and overall. The program objective is to return
depot-repairable engines to ready-for-issue (RFI) status to support Fleet aircraft and Fleet engine pool requirements. Under the Reliability Centered Maintenance (RCM) Program
engines are repaired at the lowest level of maintenance possible. Engines beyond the intermediate maintenance activities repair capability are scheduled for depot induction.
Engine field team is included in this budget submission to provide on-site depot level maintenance on an as-needed basis.

C. Components: The component repair program supports depot level aircraft mission systems and equipment component repair under programs and projects that do not have a
Material Support Date (MSD) established. Additionally, Contractor Logistics Support (CLS) support is provided for closed-loop weapon systems and equipment for Presidential
Helicopter and trainer aircraft propeller repair that will be commercially supported for its life cycle.

D. Depot Readiness Initiative – AVDLR: The Depot Readiness Initiative (DRI) utilizes depot capacity (i.e. skilled depot level artisans) to accomplish select Organizational level
(O-level) tasks (repairs, inspections, technical directive incorporation, etc.) into Phased Depot Maintenance (PDM) and Planned Maintenance Interval (PMI) events. This initiative
expedites the delivery of a Mission Capable (MC) aircraft to the squadron following a depot event. O-level DRI maintenance events may include special inspections, phase/hourly
inspections, Technical Directives (TDs), ordering the non-RFI component replacements and unscheduled maintenance (over and aboves.)

E. Calibration: The Calibration program is the sole method utilized to sustain required in-service measurement of Test, Measurement and Diagnostic Equipment (TMDE) and
Aviation Support Equipment (SE) accuracy. This equipment is used to test, repair, and maintain aircraft, Aviation Weapon Systems, and other measurement-based performance
verifications. This program establishes and maintains Calibration Laboratory Reference Standards (CALSTDs) and supporting technical products/services to ensure aviation
measurement parameters are traceable and accurate to US National standards held at the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). Program funds sustain aviation 'I'
and 'D' level cal lab capability, and 'D' level man-hours for asset cal beyond Fleet capability. Calibration is an on-cycle maintenance action for all quantitative equipment, which
must be removed from service if left un-calibrated.

F. Ground Support Equipment Rework: The SE Rework Program provides the management, facilities, equipment, material, and manpower to perform Depot Level
Maintenance for Aviation Support Equipment.

G. Aviation Mobile Facilities: The Aviation Mobile Facilities program supports Aviation Mobile Facility configurations for the Navy and Marine Corps.

A Mobile Facility (MF) is a tactical shelter that meets structural and dimensional requirements of the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), the American National
Standards Institute (ANSI), and the American Society for Testing and Material (ASTM). Designed for a 20-year service life. MFs are heated and cooled by Environmental Control
Units. All configuration of units is completed at Maintenance Depot Marine Core Base in Albany, Georgia. Occasional MF and equipment rework is performed at Blount Island
Command for the USMC Maritime Prepositioning Ships (MPS) program.

                                                                                                                                                              Exhibit OP-5, 1A5A
         98                                                                                                                                                         (Page 1 of 10)
                                                                           Department of the Navy
                                                                    FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                      Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                      Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                        Activity Group: Air Operations
                                                           Detail by Subactivity Group: Aircraft Depot Maintenance
The Mobile Facilities Program (MFP) purchases six different MF types and constructs them into over 150 different configurations. The six MF types are Type A (MFA),
Integration Unit (MFI's), Side Opening Type A (SOMFA), Side Opening Type B (SOMFB), Side Opening Type B Modified (SOMFBM), and Side Opening Type C (SOMFC).


II. Force Structure Summary:
Airframe & Engine Rework: Aircraft Depot Maintenance - Aircraft Rework and Maintenance is performed at Fleet Readiness Centers (East, Southeast, Southwest), commercially
(many vendors including Boeing, DRS Technical Services, L-3 Communications, Sikorsky, Raytheon, Rolls Royce, Standard Aero, etc.), and via interservice agreements with the
Army and Air Force in support of the following Primary Aircraft Authorized (PAA) and Total Aircraft Inventory (TAI):

                                            FY2024           FY2025           FY2026
TAI                                          3,366           3,317             3,344
PAA                                          3,322           3,364             3,395


The Metrology and Calibration program maintains aviation core technical capability at 61 Intermediate (I Level) and 20 Depot (D Level) servicing activities worldwide. The
program provides uniform units of measurements necessary to support over 447,000 Aviation Test and Measurement Systems to meet OPNAV and Commander Fleet Forces
Command goals for calibration.

The Ground Support Equipment and Aviation Support Equipment overhaul program supports approximately 1,992 different support equipment end items.

Aviation Mobile Facilities support Naval and Marine Corps aviation with over 3,000 configured mobile facilities.




In FY25, 1A4A budget exhibits for the Aviation Mobile Facilities program operations financed is consolidated and reflected in the 1A5A budget
exhibits.

                                                         Detail by Subactivity Group: Aircraft Depot Operations Support
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Support Services Program provides unscheduled services to the Fleet and is budgeted on the basis of historical level of effort and projected emergent requirements. The program
enhances Fleet readiness by providing expeditious solutions for the correction of unplanned maintenance problems incurred dur ing Fleet operations. Services include Depot
Support Items, Customer Services, Customer Fleet Support, Ferry Flight and Maintenance Support. Depot Support Items include the costs of Salvage to support an immediate
depot requirement, Preservation and De-preservation during a period of idleness prior to induction to rework. Customer Services includes the costs of nonscheduled work to
provide D-level industrial services directly to operating force activities for immediate needs both in hardware rework and technical/en gineering services. This service is provided
for the accomplishment of emergency check/test and minor repair of aeronautical material. This service also provides special industrial processes, such as plating, heat treating,
nondestructive testing, and machine shop services. Customer Fleet support includes the costs associated with Customer /Operating Forces Training and Navy Oil Analysis
sampling conducted at the Fleet Readiness Centers (FRC's). A comprehensive aviation maintenance training program is required to provide skilled technicians in the maintenance
and support of the Navy's aircraft weapon systems. Ferry Flights includes the costs of travel, per diem, and fuel associated with bringing aircraft to a rework facility prior to
maintenance (i.e. Standard/Scheduled Depot Level Maintenance (SDLM) / Product Data Management (PDM) / Planned Maintenance Int erval (PMI) or retrofit/modernization
efforts or returning aircraft to the Fleet following maintenance. The Ferry Flight program was established because the aircraft may not be returned to the same squadron and are
not being picked up by Fleet pilots. Maintenance Support provides certification criteria for performance of NAVAIR configuration management logistics functions. It provides
technical directive management and fleet assistance on all aspects of configuration management including Technical Directive Status Accounting (TDSA), Configuration Status
Account (CSA), and updates to Fleet guidance.

Fleet Readiness Center Western Pacific (FRCWP) is tasked with providing quality depot level aviation maintenance for all current and future Navy and Marine Cotps Forces
outside the Contiguous United States (OCONUS). FRCWP provides scheduled maintenance (Integrated Maintenance Plan or IMP) with International Commercial Partners for
eleven Type, Model, Series (TMS) Aircraft to include F-18 models A-G, H-60, H-1, H-53, KC-130, and V-22 airframes; unscheduled maintenance through In-Service Repair
(ISR) for all TMS; and Support Equipment (SE) overhaul. The 1A6A account supports United States Civil Service (USCS) salaries , PCS costs, tools, equipment, travel, training,
transportation, communication, IT (non-NMCI network), supplies, utilities and operation and administration of facilities. All of FRCWP's activities are based OCONUS with HQ
residing at NAF Atsugi, Japan and Detachments located in Iwakunt Japan; Okinawa; Busan, Korea; Guam; Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia; USCENTCOM; and aboard deployed
Aircraft Carrier Groups.

Fleet Readiness Center (FRC) provides for the recurring costs associated with Commander, Fleet Readiness Centers (COMFRC) oversight, management, and dir ect support of
the Fleet Readiness Centers. COMFRC provides governance functions associated with the FRC commands which integrates Intermed iate and Depot Level maintenance
organizations. COMFRC is staffed by military and civilian workforce to support FRC operations, ensure current Intermediate a nd Depot operations receive proper oversight,
identify areas for process improvement to increase readiness and cost efficiency and to communicate status to leadership. In addition to the functions performed on behalf of
COMFRC, the business unit provides direct support to FRC Mid-Atlantic, Patuxent River; FRC West, China Lake; and FRC Aviation Support Equipment management and
fmancial operations in Navy ERP.

Depot Industrial Support Services funds Naval Air Systems Command civilian workforce labor and minimal support for supplies, travel and other governmental admi nistrative
support.

Fleet Readiness Centers Infrastructure Optimization and Modernization Plan (FIOP) is a concerted effort to modernize the naval aviation depot's aging infrastructure to be
prepared for next generation aircraft and weapon systems to meet the requirements set forth in the 2019 National Defense Auth orization Act. FIOP activities include improving
readiness of current systems, replacing equipment, transitioning to new technology, removing old equipment, consolidating equ ipment/facilities, and outfitting new facilities.


In FY25, the Aircraft Depot Operations Support line item was disestablished. The operations fmanced in this line are consolidated with other Aviation Support efforts and
reflected in the 1A4N budget exhibit.
