I. Description of Operations Financed:
The Aviation Logistics program provides Navy and Marine Corps aviation programs a budget line item for Contractor Logistics Support (CLS) and Performance Based Logistics
(PBL). CLS is the maintenance performance and/or materiel management functions for a DoD system by a commercial activity. PBL is purchase support as an integrated
performance package designed to optimize system readiness and meet performance goals for a weapon system through long-term support arrangements with clear lines of authority
and responsibility. Below are the current platforms:

The KC-130J – The airframe and Harvest Hawk (HH) variants contractor provides engineering and logistics support services, and Field Service Representatives (FSRs) for the
KC-130J weapon system aircraft. The propulsion support contractor provides propulsion system (engine and propeller) support, which includes all labor and material support for
depot level repair; material for Organizational level scheduled and unscheduled maintenance (repairable and consumable) logistics, engineering, and Field Services
Representatives (FSR) support.

Fat Albert - The propulsion support contractor provides propulsion system (engine and propeller) support, which includes all labor and material support for depot level repair;
material for Organizational level scheduled and unscheduled maintenance (repairable and consumable) logistics, and engineering support.

C/MV-22 – The Joint Performance Based Logistics (PBL) contractor provides services and products supporting the C/MV-22 weapon system aircraft including obsolescence and
configuration management, sustainment of maintenance planning and supportability analysis, technical data support, in-service engineering and logistics support, training and
trainer support, and peculiar support equipment sustainment. The propulsion support contractor provides engine sustainment on a flight hour basis, Power By the Hour (PBtH),
that includes depot level engine and component repair and O-level material. Services are provided in the form of engine related obsolescence and configuration management,
technical data support, in-service engineering and logistics support, training and trainer support, and sustainment of peculiar support equipment. On-site field service
representatives support all permanent V-22 operating locations.

F-35 – Management Initiative Decision 917 directed Joint Strike Fighter (JSF), one of six pilot programs, to test revised contracting, programming, budgeting, and financing
processes for PBL agreements. The JSF approach is a total PBL government and industry partnership for maintenance and airframe and propulsion systems engineering support
including depot level repairables and consumables, support equipment, Autonomic Logistics Information System (ALIS), simulators and maintenance trainers, aircraft and engine
components, software maintenance, and ferry flight services through separate Performance Based Agreements (PBA). The Flying Hour Program funds fuel and common
consumables for the aircraft.

E-6B – Supports Chairman of Joint Chiefs of Staff (CJCS) and United States Strategic Command (USSTRATCOM), the E-6B mission is to provide survivable, endurable and
reliable Airborne Command, Control, and Communications between the President, Secretary of Defense and US Strategic and Non-Strategic Forces. Designated with the highest
possible Force Activity Designator (FAD) readiness level set by the Chief of Naval Operations (CNO), the E-6B requires 24/7/365 day a year support, with Commander, Naval Air
Forces (CNAF) stated Ready For Tasking (RFT) requirement of 12 aircraft out of a fleet of 16 aircraft. The E-6B is the only survivable nuclear hardened weapon system aircraft
capable of performing the CJCS's critical nuclear deterrence through mobile command, control, and communications. There is no back-up platform. The E-6 aircraft is a CLS life-
of-type contractor supported platform. The E-6B Integrated Logistics Support provides logistics support for the 16 aircraft flying ~11,000 flight hours a year in support of national
tasking. In FY24, one trainer aircraft (TE-6B) will be introduced, flying ~600 flight hours a year to maintain pilot proficiency. This trainer will alleviate the structural stress of
repeated touch and go landing cycles on the E-6B airframe. The Repair of Repairables (RoR) program provides CLS managed repairable repairs, consumable replenishment
including surveyed assets, and support equipment replacement as well as inventory adjustments needed to support 16 operational E-6B aircraft and one trainer (TE-6B).

FOD - The Aviation Foreign Object Damage (FOD) Mitigation Program supports aviation foreign object damage prevention for the Navy and Marine Corps.
In FY25, 1A4A budget exhibits for the Aviation Mobile Facilities program operations financed is consolidated and reflected in the 1A9A
                                                                                                                                                               Exhibit OP-5, 1A9A
budget exhibits.

II. Force Structure Summary:
The Aviation Logistics program is performed commercially (Lockheed Martin, Rolls-Royce, Pratt & Whitney, IAP (previously DRS), and Bell-Boeing) as well as organically at
the Naval Aviation Warfare Centers (NAWC), Fleet Readiness Centers (FRC), and Naval Supply Systems Command Weapon Systems Support (NAVSUP WSS) Philadelphia and
via an Inter Service Agreement (ISA) with the Air Force and Army in support of the following Primary Aircraft Authorization (PAA) and Total Aircraft Inventory (TAI):

                             PAA                                            TAI
               FY 2024     FY 2025    FY 2026                 FY 2024    FY 2025     FY 2026

 KC-130J          54          57          60                     64          64         67
 C/MV-22          226        229         236                    334         329         334
 E-6B             16          16          16                     16          16         16



Aviation Foreign Object Damage Mitigation supports Navy and Marine Corps aviation with a multi-tiered systems approach to preventing damage to aircraft and aircraft engines.

                                                                          Activity Group: Ship Operations
                                                           Detail by Subactivity Group: Mission and Other Ship Operations
I. Description of Operations Financed:
This sub-activity group represents the core capability of the US Navy. It provides funding for all aspects of ship operations required to continuously deploy combat ready warships
which support national objectives. Programs financed include operating tempo (OPTEMPO), fleet and unit training, operational support such as command and control, pier side
support, port services, organizational maintenance, and associated administrative and other support. Costs consist of distillate fuel to support the OPTEMPO which has historically
been 58 underway days per quarter for deployed Fleet forces and 24 underway days per quarter for non-deployed forces. Other items funded include amphibious support craft,
temporary assigned duty (TAD) for shipboard and afloat staff personnel, organizational level repairs, supplies and equipage (S&E) Operating Target (OPTAR); utilities costs; and
charter of units through the Military Sealift Command (MSC).

                                                      Detail by Subactivity Group: Ship Operational Support and Training


I. Description of Operations Financed:
Ship Operational Support and Training provides for detailed pre-planning, engineering, training and range operations necessary to ensure all operating force ships and
nuclear attack submarines and their crews are operating at high levels of readiness. Specific programs funded include submarine support, surface ship support, and Receipt,
Segregation, Storage, and Issue (RSSI).

Submarine Support includes the Submarine Operations and Safety Program, which provides technical direction and engineering support to safely extend the operating interval
between shipyard overhauls for operational submarines. It also manages advanced planning and engineering for overhauls and other submarine availabilities. The Deep
Submergence Systems Program (DSSP) provides program and technical management to support the Fleets' capabilities in the areas of submarine rescue, deep ocean search,
inspection, object recovery, and scientific research and data gathering for a variety of manned and unmanned deep submergence vehicle programs. This program supports Virginia
Class (VACL) submarine obsolescence materials public shipyard outsourcing work in an effort to support Navy's 15-year maintenance plan thru the FYDP.

Surface Support includes the AEGIS Program, which supports the Navy's AEGIS cruiser and destroyer fleet. The AEGIS Program funds a variety of requirements for AEGIS
cruisers and destroyers from training officers and crew members to operate the AEGIS weapons system critical equipment testing after depot maintenance availabilities.

The Fleet Ordnance Receipt, Segregation, Storage, and Issue (RSSI) program provides for ordnance movement, handling, storage, and disposal as required by Fleet operations and
for inventory management. The RSSI program supports personnel and material to manage the Navy's worldwide disposable ordnance inventory and accomplishes required reuse
and declassification in the most effective and economical manner consistent with safety, security, and environmental regulations and constraints.



II. Force Structure Summary:
Not applicable.

                                                                              Department of the Navy
                                                                     FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                         Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                          Activity Group: Ship Operations
                                                                  Detail by Subactivity Group: Ship Maintenance
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Financing within this program supports maintenance ranging from overhauls (OH) to restricted and technical availabilities (RA/TA) performed at naval shipyards (public) or
private shipyards. Ship overhauls restore the ship including all operating systems that affect safety or combat capability to established performance standards. This includes the
correction of all discrepancies found during pre-overhaul tests and inspections or developed from maintenance history analysis. RA/TA repairs include selected restricted
availabilities (SRA), phased maintenance availabilities (PMA), emergent repairs, service craft overhauls (SCO), repairs during post-shakedown of new units, interim dry docking,
battery renewals and various miscellaneous type repairs.

Program supports Virginia Class (VACL) submarine materials for Chief of Naval Operations (CNO) maintenance availabilities in an effort to improve material availability and
increase operational availability (Ao) for the fleet. Availability of these materials would enable the shipyards to start work on time, conduct required repairs, on-time work
completion and optimize supply chain capability.

Program supports Maintenance and Repair (M&R) for Military Sealift Command (MSC) in an effort to improve readiness across the logistics force. This includes the correction of
all discrepancies found during the Mid-Term Availability (MTA) and Regular Overhaul (ROH) to be repaired or corrected in accordance with the United States Coast Guard
(USCG) / American Bureau of Shipping.

Non-depot/Intermediate Maintenance supports Fleet Maintenance performed by Navy personnel and civilians on repair ships, aircraft carriers, Intermediate Maintenance Facilities
(IMFs), Navy Regional Maintenance Centers (RMCs), Trident Refit Facilities (TRFs) and Regional Support Group (RSG). The RMCs provide in-service engineering & technical
support and oversight of private sector depot-level maintenance and modernization in fleet concentration areas. The TRFs provide industrial support for incremental overhaul and
repair of Trident submarines and for the overhaul of equipment in the Trident Planned Equipment Replacement (TRIPER) Program. RSG Groton, CT provides intermediate-level
maintenance, ordnance, and supply support to nuclear attack submarines, support vessels and service craft.

Funding includes the necessary FY 2025 and FY 2026 advanced planning (i.e. availability execution planning), ship checks, and material required to support FY 2025 and FY
2026 inductions.

II. Force Structure Summary:
The total Ship Maintenance program supports 8 Overhauls & 60 maintenance availabilities in FY 2024. In FY 2025, the Navy plans to award 2 Overhauls & 69 maintenance
availabilities. In FY 2026, the Navy plans to award 5 Overhauls & 52 maintenance availabilities.
                                                    FY          FY          FY
     Availability Type (OMN+OPN)                   2024        2025        2026
 Overhauls (OH)                                      8           2           5
 Selected Restricted Availabilities (SRA)           38          47          37
 Surface Incremental Availabilities (SIA)           16          15          10

IV. Performance Criteria and Evaluation Summary Table II :

Table II - Activity: Non-depot / Intermediate-level Maintenance

Activity Goal: The Intermediate-level (I-level) maintenance program supports ship maintenance performed by Navy personnel and civilians on tenders, repair ships, aircraft
carriers, at Regional Maintenance Centers (RMCs), Trident Refit Facilities (TRFs), and at the Regional Support Group (RSG), Groton.


Description of Activity: The I-level maintenance program funds the pay of civilian personnel, materials and day-to-day operations at the RMCs, TRFs, and RSG. The RMCs
perform I-level maintenance on ships and submarines assigned to their respective port. The TRFs provide industrial support for incremental overhaul and repair of Trident
submarines and for the overhaul of equipment in the Trident Planned Equipment Replacement (TRIPER) program. RSG Groton, CT provides I-level maintenance, ordnance,
and supply support to nuclear attack submarines, support vessels and service craft.

Table VI – Deferred Maintenance

                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                         Activity Group: Ship Operations
                                                           Detail by Subactivity Group: Ship Depot Operations Support
I. Description of Operations Financed:
This sub-activity group funds a variety of depot maintenance programs. The planning and technical support function provides management for availabilities, life cycle
maintenance, and class maintenance support due to alterations, repair material management, and special projects for ship logistics managers. This program provides for the
continued safe and reliable operation of naval nuclear propulsion plants by funding essential routine maintenance and engineering support, inspection and refurbishment of reactor
components, as well as routine support of reactor refueling/defueling and reactor servicing equipment.

The Supervisors of Shipbuilding, Conversion, and Repair (SUPSHIPs) are designated contract administrators and on-site technical and business agents for the Navy Department
and other Department of Defense shipbuilding, design and conversion and facility contracts at assigned shipyards. They are also responsible for planning, procuring, and providing
field program management of overhauls, repairs, alterations, and inactivation performed on Naval and other DoD ships at private shipyards.

The Navy Modernization Process (NMP) funds all design services in support of Operation and Maintenance Navy (O&M, N) alterations. The NMP is designed to upgrade ships of
the fleet to be mission capable in countering current and projected threats and improve operational capabilities. Modernization of aircraft carriers, surface combatants and
submarines improves war-fighting capability, reliability, safety and quality of life.

Ship Life Cycle Support provides technical oversight in the diagnosis, planning and execution of modernization and repair work. In addition, management and technical expertise
are provided to ensure that documentation, support, spare parts and personnel are available to support the Fleet.

Naval shipyards' centrally managed Quality and Safety Management program develops policy, technical guidance, training, and oversight to ensure that industrial work performed
in Naval Shipyards and SUPSHIPs accomplished in accordance with technical specifications, regulations and customer requirements.

The Berthing and Messing program funds off-ship berthing costs for a ship's crew when the ship is uninhabitable during an availability or overhaul. This program also supports
berthing barge operation and maintenance.

The Shipyard Infrastructure Optimization Program (SIOP) supports modernizing the Navy's four public shipyards at Norfolk, Portsmouth, Puget Sound and Pearl Harbor. The
SIOP program will deliver efficient and modernized shipyards through upgrading existing dry docks and building new ones, reimaging the physical layout of the shipyards and
replacing antiquated capital equipment with modern machines.

The Submarine Industrial Base (SIB) program supports critical efforts to recapitalize the Submarine Industrial Base by investing in shipbuilder infrastructure, supply chain
capability/capacity, scaling new technologies, addressing workforce trade skill gaps and constraints, expanding capacity via strategic outsourcing, and government oversight of
expanded industrial base efforts.

II. Force Structure Summary:
Not applicable.

      vii) DDG-1000 Maintenance and Support - Program decrease is due to DDG 1000 completing the modernization availability in            -6,941
        FY25. (Baseline: $197,600)
      viii) Total Berthing and Messing Program - Program decrease from 11 availabilities in FY 2025 (1 Drydock Restricted                 -6,990
        Overhaul (DROH), 4 Restricted Overhaul (ROH), 1 Restricted Availability (RAV) and 5 Intermediate Maintenance
        Availabilities (IMAV)) to 8 availabilities in FY 2026 (1 DROH, 6 RAV and 1 IMAV) with reduced scope and complexity.
        (Baseline: $121,924)
      ix) Facilities and Supply Support Operations - Program decrease is due to a reduction in support to the Painting Center of          -7,820
        Excellence Program in the area of corrosion control technology transition and implementation. This includes reduced support to
        the implementation of corrosion resistance ship components. (Baseline: $87,573)
      x) Surface & Amphibious Ship Support - The decrease is due to the reduced funding for Bridge Lighting Design Requirements           -8,466
        and Video Data Recorder (VDR) sustainment. (Baseline: $463,167)
      xi) Total Berthing and Messing Program - Program decrease due to CVN 79 delivering in FY 2025 and the Pre-                         -10,122
        Commissioning Unit (PCU) crew no longer requiring off-ship housing. (Baseline: $13,115)
      xii) Smart Work/TOC Initiatives - Program decrease due to reduction of LOG IT Modernization requirements that support              -10,924
        digital data transformation (Baseline: $35,283)
      xiii) Surface & Amphibious Ship Support - Program decrease due to USS Blue Ridge (LCC-19) reduced support services for             -24,343
        Docking Selected Restricted Availability (DSRA). Decrease accounts for a reduced need for Engineering, Ship Checks, Ship
        Installation Drawings, and Material, required to support future LPD class ship availabilities. (Baseline: $463,167)
      xiv) Navy Modernization Process (NMP) - Carriers - Program decrease due to the Dual Band Radar obsolescence and RADAR               -25,292
        Suite Drawings on CVN 78. Program changes from year-to-year reflect the approved OPNAV 4700 which depict CVN
        schedule driven availabilities. Each availability has numerous Ship Change Documents (SCD) where the cost per unit vary
        year-to-year by carrier. (Baseline: $69,476)
      xv) Efficiency – Contract Services: Reduces contracts for Advisory and Assistance Services to promote efficiencies and             -32,892
        advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222, "Implementing the President's
        Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative." (Baseline: $138,044)
      xvi) Reduces civilian personnel full-time equivalents and funding to optimize the workforce in compliance with Executive           -50,684

                                                                                Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                                        Detail by Subactivity Group: Combat Communications and
                                                                                            Electronic Warfare
I. Description of Operations Financed:

This subactivity group includes funding for communications systems that directly support fleet operations.

Navy Information Dominance Force (NAVIFOR) mission is to organize, man, train, equip and maintain Cyber/Command, Control, Communications, Computers, Collaboration,
Intelligence (C5I) forces and activities to generate required levels of current and future readiness. NAVIFOR operates and maintains space system ground based components,
performs satellite monitoring and long–term system performance analysis.

Additional programs supported are Electronic Command and Control Systems including Global Command and Control System (GCCS), Defense Information Services Network
(DISN) Subscription Service (DSS) Long Haul communications, Clarinet Merlin, Mobile User Objective System (MUOS), Navy Circuit Management (NCMO), Take Charge and
Move Out (TACAMO), Commercial Satellite Communications, Fixed Submarine Broadcast System (FSBS), Navy Multiband Terminal (NMT), Global Broadcast Service (GBS),
Cooperative Engagement Capability (CEC), Navigation System Management, Navy Tactical Mobile (TacMobile), Trusted Information System, Fleet Readiness Directorate
(FRD), Joint Mobile Ashore Support Terminal (JMAST) and Ring Laser Gyro Navigator (RLGN). The Follow-On Satellite Program supports the Fleet satellite constellation and
provides reliable communication links among Navy forces with EHF, UHF and SHF capabilities.

Funding also provides for In-Service Engineering Activity (ISEA) support of Shipboard Information Warfare Exploitation systems, Common Data Link System (CDLS), and
Common High Bandwidth Data Link (CHBDL) programs onboard a multitude of platforms in the Fleet. ISEA efforts include hardware and software maintenance, technical
assistance to deployed systems, Casualty Report (CASREP) repairs, in-service system support, pre-deployment system grooms, on-site system training, documentation updates and
configuration management of deployed systems.

II. Force Structure Summary:

Combat Communications supports the Navy Center for Tactical System Interoperability, Joint Mobile Ashore Support Terminal, the Operational Test and Evaluation Force, the
Signals Warfare Support Center and various satellite communications sites.

        business services and management operations in support of PEO C4I, PEO MLB, and PEO Digital Acquisition Programs.
        (Baseline: $26,628)
      xxxv)CIVPERS: decrease resulting from updated personnel pricing based on planned workforce reshaping. (Baseline:                  -5,238
        $345,577; -8 civilian FTE)
      xxxvi) Ship Self Defense System (SSDS)/Advanced Combat Direction System (ACDS); SSDS In-Service Engineering:                      -6,397
        Decrease in requirements for pre-installation inspections and check-out for all Ship Self Defense System (SSDS) ships
        entering an availability where modernization or repair of any integrated combat system element is scheduled. Additionally, a
        decrease in post-delivery activities, light-off of the integrated combat system will continue, as well as crew operator and
        maintenance support to exit the availability. (Baseline: $33,101)
      xxxvii) Command, Control, Communications, Computers and Intelligence (C4I); Commercial Satellite (COMERSAT),                     -10,319
        INMARSAT-B, TV-DTS & C-Band Leases: decrease in requirements for licenses for traditional commercial broadband
        satellite capabilities. (Baseline: $76,141)

I. Description of Operations Financed:

Expeditionary Health Services Systems (EHSS) provide expeditionary medical systems (EXMEDS) to U.S. and allied forces for combat and contingency operations. These
systems include large Role 3 Expeditionary Medical Facilities (EMF), Hospital Ships (T-AH), smaller Role 2 Enhanced Expeditionary Medical Units (EMU), and specialized units
like Expeditionary Resuscitative Surgery Systems (ERSS) and En-Route Care Systems (ERCS). EMFs and EMUs are strategically pre-positioned to complement Fleet medical
capabilities and support Marine Corps operations. T-AH Hospital Ships, USNS MERCY and USNS COMFORT, offer extensive medical treatment with 12 operating rooms and
1,000 beds. They maintain a Reduced Operation Status and undergo regular maintenance. EMFs and EMUs are modular and adaptable to mission requirements, providing essential
stabilization and treatment for wounded troops. Funding supports modernization through the Service Life Extension Program (SLEP) and replenishment of medical supplies.
FDPMUs rapidly address health threats in any operational theater. ERSS provides life-saving damage control surgery in austere environments, while ERCS ensures patient
stabilization during transport within deployed theaters.

Naval Medical Forces Support Command (NMFSC) oversees medical readiness training, offering over 124 courses to prepare officers and enlisted personnel for operational
medical roles. NMFDC manages the Medical Modeling and Simulation program, ensuring standardized training tools and emerging technologies. It certifies the readiness of all
deployable medical platforms, including EMFs, EMUs, ERSS, and Role 2 Platforms.

Education and Training funding supports specialized medical education for Navy personnel, including the Armed Forces Health Professions Scholarship Program (AFHPSP) and
Financial Assistance Program (FAP), covering tuition and related expenses. The Health Professions Loan Repayment Program (HPLRP) aids active-duty providers in repaying
education loans. NMFDC also delivers specialized training for aviation, undersea medicine, and special operations forces.

Enterprise Operations funds support Navy Medicine's operational programs, including Advanced Analytics, Information Technology, and Logistics. It also covers compliance
programs like Financial Improvement and Audit Readiness (FIAR) and Base Operations Support, ensuring continued administrative and operational efficiency.

Facilities Operations funding maintains Navy Medicine facilities, ensuring compliance with environmental laws and extending the service life of infrastructure through
restoration and modernization efforts.

Medical Readiness programs ensure Navy and Marine Corps personnel meet medical standards, including readiness programs like Family Readiness, Medical Evaluation Boards,
and Emergency Management. This includes support for critical medical specialties like Aerospace Medicine, Undersea Medicine, and Independent Duty Corpsmen.

Mental Health (MH) programs focus on maintaining wartime readiness and reducing mental health issues such as PTSD and suicide, which impact operational deployments.

Operational Medicine (O2) develops policies for Navy Medicine Clinical Operations, including Women's Health and Dental Care, ensuring comprehensive care for operational
forces.

Sexual Assault Prevention & Response (SAPR) continues to enhance reporting and prevention efforts, aligned with the Department of Defense's roadmap for implementing
Independent Review Commission recommendations.


                                                                                                                                                          Exhibit OP-5, 1C1M
        215                                                                                                                                                     (Page 1 of 11)
                                                                                 Department of the Navy
                                                                        FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                            Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                            Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                      Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                                     Detail by Subactivity Group: Medical Readiness

USNS COMFORT: Medical Treatment Facility (MTF), which includes medical equipment support for USNS COMFORT (T-AH 20) to maintain the ship's material and
maintenance requirements. The T-AH Hospital Ships afloat have the capability to conduct casualty medical treatment providing services to 12 operating rooms and 1,000 beds.
The services provide for intensive care/medical-surgical units, operating rooms, laboratory and radiology for site support. The primary mission of the Medical Treatment Facility
(MTF) on the USNS COMFORT is to provide a mobile, flexible, and rapidly responsive afloat medical capability for acute medical and surgical care in support of amphibious task
forces, Marine Corps, Army and Air Force elements, and forward deployed Navy elements of the fleet where fleet activities are in any area which may be hostile and imminent. As
a secondary mission, Medical Treatment Facility (MTF) USNS COMFORT is capable of providing a full hospital service asset for use by other government agencies involved in
the support of relief and humanitarian operations worldwide.

USNS MERCY: The USNS MERCY (T-AH 19) along with a Medical Treatment Facility (MTF) staff embarked, provides rapid, flexible and mobile acute medical care to
support designated naval/joint/multinational forces engaged in or potentially engaged in combat. USNS MERCY (T-AH 19) provides mobile surgical and tertiary care hospital
service in disaster relief or humanitarian missions.

The Expeditionary Medical Facility (EMF) in Camp Lemonnier, Djibouti provides support for expanded medical care to personnel supporting Combined Joint Task Force.
The Fleet Hospital Program is comprised of scalable, modular, rapidly erectable Expeditionary Medical Facility (EMF) that provide comprehensive medical support to U.S. and
allied forces in the event of combat or contingency operations. These medical capability packages play a critical role in the Navy's evolving war fighting strategies of forward
deployed theater operations. Funding supports, capability-based EMFs when and where tasked to stand up a medical facility in the Horn of Africa Area of Responsibility (AOR).
The Horn of Africa region, EMF Djibouti provides acute medical and dental services in support of approximately 4,000 U.S. joint and allied military forces, civilian personnel and
U.S. Department of Defense contractors as well as personnel from ships, aircraft, and other units conducting operations.

The Newport News Clinic is part of larger efforts directed by the Vice Chief of Naval Operations (VCNO) to improve Quality of Service (QoS) to Sailors assigned to vessels in
both New Construction and Maintenance Availability periods under the cognizance of Supervisor of Shipbuilding Newport News and executed by Huntington Ingalls Industries at
the Newport News Shipyard. Funding supports this temporary location while pending construction of the planned permanent facility which will provide medical ancillary services
as different vessels, and their organic medical departments, transition into and out of the Newport News Shipyard.

Naval Expeditionary Health Services Support (NEHSS) focuses on integrating fleet-wide Roles 1-3 medical capabilities, to increase patient survivability rates, recovery and
patient movement, while also providing care chain capabilities in a distributed environment.

Expeditionary Medicine Family of Systems: Expeditionary Health Services Systems (EHSS) provide comprehensive deployable medical systems (DEPMEDS) to U.S. and
allied forces in the event of combat or contingency operations. DEPMEDS includes, land-based Role 3 Expeditionary Medical Facilities (EMF), land-based Role 2 Enhanced
Expeditionary Medical Units (EMU), land or sea-based Role 2 Light Maneuver (R2LM) Expeditionary Resuscitative Surgery Systems (ERSS), En Route Care Systems (ERCS),
Certified Diagnostic Mobile Laboratories (CDML), and land-based Forward Deployable Preventive Medicine Units (FDPMU). EMFs and EMUs are pre-positioned ashore and
afloat throughout the world and are designed to complement and expand the organic medical capabilities of the Fleet and play a critical role in the Marine Corps' evolving war
fighting strategies of forward deployed theater operations. EMFs and EMUs are composed of distinct capability based modules/packages which can be tailored to meet whatever
mission is required. These medical and surgical facilities provide the ability to stabilize, treat, and rehabilitate wounded warfighters. Funding supports the transformational
modernization/upgrading of these units through the Service Life Extension Program (SLEP) and periodic replacement of perishable or shelf-life limited medical supplies. The
Certified Diagnostic Mobile Lab (CDML) is a diagnostic mobile lab capability that is able to support pandemic and outbreak response testing. The ERCS is the core capability to
patient movement ensuring degradation of patient movement does not occur during transport to the next level of care.

II. Force Structure Summary:
The AFHPSP, established by 10 U.S.C. 2121, is an Individual Ready Reserve Program for students accepted to, or enrolled in, an accredited training program leading to a health
professions degree in medicine, dentistry, optometry, clinical psychology, and master's degrees as physician assistants. The FAP, established by 10 U.S.C. 2121, is an Individual
Ready Reserve Program for physicians or dentist currently accepted to, or enrolled in, an accredited residency or fellowship program progressing toward a medical or dental
specialty which has been designated as critical to the Department of Defense. The HPLRP is a direct accession and retention program to maintain adequate numbers of
commissioned officers of the armed forces on active duty who are qualified health professionals in specific specialties.

The EXMEDS program provides for the management, transformation, modernization, and reconstitution of platforms that will deliver more agile capabilities across the spectrum
of Navy, Marine Corps, and Joint operations. Navy's EHSS funding provides equipment and supplies for EMFs, T-AHs, EMUs, ERSSs, ERCS's and FDPMUs, as well as program
staff at BUMED and Navy Expeditionary Medical Support Command (NEMSCOM).

The Navy and Marine Corps Force Health Protection Command (formerly Navy and Marine Corps Public Health Center) establishes command and control of the two Navy Drug
Testing Laboratories, conducts tri-annual quality assurance inspections, and administrates the Tri-service reagent, consumable, maintenance contracts and equipment purchase with
the assistance of the Naval Medical Readiness Logistics Command and Washington Headquarters Services. The Chief of Naval Personnel establishes Navy urinalysis testing
requirements, ensures annual quality assurance inspections at the Navy drug labs, and manages the military education and training programs.

The Fleet Hospital Program consists of two Afloat Fleet Hospital Ship, USNS COMFORT (T-AH 20) and USNS MERCY (T-AH 19). Each hospital ship includes an onboard
Medical Treatment Facility (MTF).

One Expeditionary Medical Facility (EMF) in Camp Lemonnier, Djibouti

One Clinic in Newport News, Virginia as directed by the Vice Chief of Naval Operations (VCNO) to improve Quality of Service (QoS) to Sailors assigned to vessels in both New
Construction and Maintenance Availability periods under the cognizance of Supervisor of Shipbuilding Newport News and executed by Huntington Ingalls Industries at the
Newport News Shipyard.

                                                               Detail by Subactivity Group: Space Systems and Surveillance
I. Description of Operations Financed:

This subactivity group includes funding for the Integrated Undersea Surveillance System, Commander, Undersea Surveillance (CUS and the Naval Ocean Processing Facilities
(NOPF)) at Whidbey Island and Dam Neck. Programs include the Sound Surveillance System (SOSUS), Fixed Distributive System (FDS), Fixed Surveillance System (FSS),
International Programs and the Surveillance Towed Array Sensor System (SURTASS).

Tug Auxiliary General Ocean Surveillance (T-AGOS)

          Ocean surveillance ships gather underwater acoustical data. The T-AGOS ships are operated by Military Sealift Command to support the anti-submarine warfare mission
of the commanders of the Atlantic and Pacific Fleets. The two classes of surveillance ships use surveillance towed-array sensor system (SURTASS) equipment to gather undersea
acoustic data. The ships also carry electronic equipment to process and transmit that data via satellite to shore stations for evaluation. Victorious-class ocean surveillance ships are
built on a small-waterplane, twin-hull, or SWATH, design for greater stability at slow speeds in high latitudes under adverse weather conditions. Impeccable has a hull form based
on that of Victorious, but has a more powerful propulsion plant and is designed specifically for deploying an additional active towed-array system.

Fixed Surveillance System (SS) Classified Program

         Additional details held at a higher classification.

Integrated Undersea Surveillance Systems (IUSS)

          Funding in this line supports the Maritime Surveillance Systems (MSS) mission to detect, classify, localize, track and report nuclear and diesel submarines in deep-ocean
and littoral regions. Specifically financed is the Surveillance Towed Array Sensor System (SURTASS) which provides funding for the operation and maintenance of mission
equipment on T-AGOS (Auxiliary General Ocean Surveillance) Class ships (including array depot repairs for passive systems, as well as Low Frequency Active (LFA)/Compact
LFA (CLFA) equipment maintenance), operations and maintenance planning and execution, technician support services (including training), maintenance of Integrated Common
Processor (ICP), and program support. This line funds all support and sustainment efforts for the Expeditionary SURTASS (SURTASS-E) project. OMN funding for both
SURTASS and SURTASS-E supports ship operations, including logistic support facility, array maintenance facility, software maintenance, and depot level spares. Funding will
also continue to provide technical support by US technicians to SURTASS ships and shore facilities in Japan. Funding in this line supports Deployable Surveillance Systems (DSS)
which provides on-demand, deployable maritime surveillance capability in ocean areas of national interest in support of Joint and Naval Task Force Commanders.

Drug Interdiction and Counter Drug Activities

        Counter Narcotics funding is provided during the year of execution. Funds support Relocatable Over-the-Horizon Radar (ROTHR) systems. A unique radar system
designed to provide tactical warning to battle group commanders of air and surface threats at an extended range to allow time for responsive engagement.

I. Description of Operations Financed:

Anti-Submarine Range and Test Program
SSRNM, FSSN, US FORACS (N96) - The Surface Ship Radiated Noise Measurement (SSRNM), Fleet Sonar Self Noise (FSSN), and United States (U.S.) Fleet Operational
Readiness Accuracy Check Sites (FORACS). The SSRNM program provides acoustic trials, acoustic trial management, acoustic data for fleet tactical plans and vulnerability
assessments of surface combatants that have been constructed with reduced acoustic signature and silencing systems. Noise reduction is necessary to achieve sonar performance,
reduce vulnerability to acoustic influence mines and torpedoes. These funds also provide life cycle support, Technical Direction Agent (TDA) and In-service-Engineering Agent
(ISEA) support for SSRNM in-service systems. The FSSN program provides expert analysis of sonar self-noise data and is part of the ship's Planned Maintenance System (PMS).
The analysis provides data about the ship's sonar operational status and its interaction with other assets. The U.S. FORACS program consists of two fixed test ranges (Atlantic
Undersea Test and Evaluation Center (AUTEC) & San Clemente Island (SCI) in Southern California and deployable capabilities. Mission is to measure the bearing, range,
position, and heading accuracy of sensors in surface ships, submarines, and maritime aircraft to satisfy Sensory Accuracy Test (SAT) requirements material readiness standards
through dynamic combat system performance evaluation. Funding for SATs, Technical Direction Agent (TDA), and In-Service Engineering Agent (ISEA) support for fixed and
portable SAT capabilities for both government and contractor support.

NATO FORACS & US FORACS (N94)- North Atlantic Treaty Organization Naval Forces Sensor and Weapon Readiness Accuracy Check Sites (NATO FORACS) program is a
multi-national NATO project available to all Alliance nations and consists of three fixed test ranges and deployable capabilities. Mission is to measure the bearing, range, position,
and heading accuracy of sensors in surface ships, submarines, and maritime aircraft to satisfy national requirements and meet NATO material readiness standards through dynamic
combat system performance evaluation. U.S. participation in the NATO FORACS program provides the U.S. cost share for the operation of three test ranges (Norway, Greece,
Atlantic Undersea Test and Evaluation Center (AUTEC)). The U.S. FORACS program has a fixed range off the coast of San Clemente Island (SCI) in Southern California and
provides deployed portable test capabilities to satisfy requirements. Funding for SATs, Technical Direction Agent (TDA), and In-Service Engineering Agent (ISEA) for both
government and contractor support for fixed and portable Sensor Accuracy Test (SAT) capabilities to both U.S. and NATO FORACS.

UTRE, MK28 (N94)- The Underwater Tracking Range Equipment (UTRE) Program provides pinger systems for tracking on the Navy's Undersea Fleet Training and Test &
Evaluation Ranges. The program provides equipment maintenance, installation, and repair, plus data reporting and support of fleet exercises at the Atlantic Undersea Test &
Evaluation Center (AUTEC), Southern California Offshore Range Extension (SCORE) and Barking Sands Tactical Underwater Range (BARSTUR) training ranges. In addition,
this budget supports product improvement efforts for the tracking instrumentation in support of the Navy's underwater readiness training. This program also provides Technical
Direction Agent (TDA) and In-Service-Engineering Agent (ISEA) support for underwater tracking range equipment. The MK28 Surface Weapon Test Target (SWTT) provides the
Navy the capability to test the explosive train of torpedoes and the reliability of the torpedoes warhead. The funding supports government oversight and product improvement
efforts.

ICS CAP (N97)- The Integrated Combat Systems Certification and Assessment Program (ICS CAP) is a program that determines and certifies the technical, material, and logistical
readiness of the submarine ICS to support operational submarine warfare mission areas. ICS CAP supports certification following initial installation and subsequent modernization
of the ICS for all new construction and in-service submarines. ICS certification for in-service submarines is based on data gathered by the ICS CAP test team from dockside testing
and at-sea testing, as well as the review of other technical documentation. Data to support ICS certification of new construction submarines is provided by the acquisition Program
Office (PMS 450 for VA Class submarines) from Program-sponsored testing, as well as the review of other technical documentation.




                                                                                                                                                               Exhibit OP-5, 1C4C
         236                                                                                                                                                        (Page 1 of 20)
                                                                                Department of the Navy
                                                                      FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                          Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                         Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                    Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                                    Detail by Subactivity Group: Warfare Tactics
ASW Range Support- Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW) Range support program provides training range equipment and Fleet support equipment for use on the Navy's underwater
ranges. This equipment is used for Fleet Training, ASW exercises and torpedoes firings and ASW readiness assessment testing. The Fleet training ranges supported are Southern
California Tactical Training Range (SCTTR), Barking Sands Tactical Underwater Range/Barking Sands Underwater Range Extension (BARSTUR/BSURE), Atlantic Underwater
Test and Evaluation Center (AUTEC) and Naval Undersea Warfare Center, Division Keyport Dabob Bay ASW Range and supports USN training at the Joint Canadian and US
ASW range at Nanoose. Product Improvement funds enhancement tasks to Range Support of the MK30. The MK 30 ASW Training Target provides essential Fleet ASW training
on the Navy's underwater tracking ranges by emulating threat submarines. The MK30 Mod 1 is currently used at BARSTUR - Hawaii, AUTEC -Bahamas, SCTTR and the
Northwest ASW ranges. ASW range support funds are used to procure MK30 Mod 1 components (i.e. replacement silver-zinc batteries, towed array buoys and towed arrays,
strobe batteries, inflation bags, as well as consumable hardware such as o-rings and seals, fasteners, etc.) that are consumed/expended during fleet in-water runs. These funds are
used to replace obsolete components and improve maintenance and reliability of the targets. This funding also provides support of Helicopter and ship launch and recovery
equipment. Engineering funds support Configuration Management, Manual Change Requests, Engineering Change Proposals (ECPs), Software changes, test equipment changes,
and obsolescence engineering efforts as performed by the field activities for the MK30 program.

Seaborne Targets
Seaborne Targets program provides in-service engineering, maintenance, logistics, and depot support for the Navy's total fleet of seaborne targets.

SOCAL Mine Training Range
The Mine Countermeasures (MCM) Target Maintenance program provides in-service engineering support, targets maintenance and engineering logistics support for the Navy's
stockpile of mine countermeasures targets.

Training

Tactical Training Group, Pacific (TTGP): Trains and evaluates Carrier and Expeditionary Strike Group staff's tactical and operational proficiency in doctrine, tactics techniques
and procedures. TTGP executes the Fleet Synthetic Training program for COMPACFLT, as well as, support to Commander Third Fleet and Commander Seventh Fleets' training
programs at the tactical and operational level to include integrated Navy, Joint, Coalition, and/or combined environments.

Expeditionary Warfare Training Group Pacific (EWTGP): Conducts Navy and Marine Corps training and instruction in the doctrine, tactics, and techniques of Naval
Expeditionary Warfare with a focus on amphibious operations.

Afloat Training Group Pacific (ATGPAC): Conducts unit level training, assessment, and certification for surface units in the Pacific. Includes development of critical tasks and
standards for ships related to mission area training, as well as maintaining all Commander, U. S. Surface Forces Pacific certification and training data. They conduct unit level
qualifications and Ready For Sea (RFS) tasking.

Readiness Assistance Training Teams (RATT): Provides relevant shipboard training tailored to an individual ships while informing schoolhouse and waterfront trainers and
assessors of fleet-validated learning needs, in order to improve Sailor performance and enhance mission readiness.

Diesel Electric Submarine Initiative (DESI): This program funds the participation of South American diesel electric submarines as opposition forces during Fleet exercises and
training events.


                                                                                                                                                             Exhibit OP-5, 1C4C
         237                                                                                                                                                      (Page 2 of 20)
                                                                              Department of the Navy
                                                                     FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                   Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                                   Detail by Subactivity Group: Warfare Tactics

This sub-activity group has the responsibility to plan, execute, request and report expenditures of Navy and Joint funding required to certify and support pre-deployment and
sustainment training of combat-ready Navy forces. The USFF N7 Directorate is responsible for conducting financial management and fiscal oversight for Fleet training in direct
support of the Optimized Fleet Response Plan (O-FRP).
Fleet training efforts include:
         • The Fleet Synthetic Training (FST) Program; which is a key readiness, tool encompassing a multitude of synthetic exercises;
         • Academic training of individuals and staffs in preparation for deployment;
         • Live training event/exercise support that involve unit and integrated training exercises using land, sea and underwater training/tracking ranges, electronic warfare threat
         emitters, aerial, seaborne, land and underwater targets.

Fleet Training facilitated by:
         • Commander, Strike Group Four (CSG-4);
         • Tactical Training Group Atlantic (TTGL);
         • Expeditionary Warfare Training Group Atlantic (EWTGL);
         • Commander, Naval Air Forces Atlantic (CNAL);
         • Commander, Naval Surface Forces Atlantic (CNSL);
         • Afloat Training Group Atlantic/Pacific (ATGL)
         • Commander, Submarine Forces Atlantic (CSL);

Major Fleet Training Programs / Efforts:
        Directs and executes all funding for Fleet Training efforts, responsible for the pre-deployment certification and sustainment of US Navy forces and after-action
        assessments.
        Fleet Synthetic Training (FST) using the Navy Continuous Training Environment (NCTE) provide unit through operational level of war proficiency training,
        interoperability training, operational training, mission rehearsal training, and joint interoperability training for ships, aviation and ship simulators through a series of
        evaluated training events. FST is conduct in conjunction with live training to create a unified Live, Virtual and Constructive (LVC) training environment.
        Engineering Readiness Assessment Team (ERAT)/Combat Systems Readiness Assessment Team (CSRAT)/INSURV Readiness Assessment Team (IRAT) assess unit's
Engineering combat and INSURV readiness.
        The Diesel-Electric Submarine Initiative (DESI) is a collaborative program between the U.S. Navy and South American navies to deploy their diesel-electric submarines
        to the east coast to provide the U.S. Navy with the opportunity to practice and improve Fleet Antisubmarine Warfare (ASW) training against current threats.
        The Quantitative Fleet Feedback (QFF) program supports training, assessment, and readiness certification decisions by providing timely reconstruction and analysis of
        synthetic and live training events using quantitative, metrics-based data;
        The Regional Security Education Program (RSEP) prepares Naval Forces for overseas operations by providing education about the regions in which they will operate.
        RSEP provides education afloat for deploying Naval Strike Group staffs to include Carrier Strike Groups (CSG) and Amphibious Readiness Groups (ARG).
        Defense Readiness Reporting System-Strategic (DRRS-S) provides resourcing data via the Global Status of Readiness and Training System (GSORTS) and the
        Organizational and Reporting System (OARS). DRRS-S supports Global Force Readiness to include: 24/7 Maritime Operations Center (MOC) support, purchasing



                                                                                                                                                               Exhibit OP-5, 1C4C
         238                                                                                                                                                        (Page 3 of 20)
                                                                           Department of the Navy
                                                                  FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                     Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                     Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Warfare Tactics
        hardware and software licenses, Program of Record (POR) acquisition management, installation, training, operations and maintenance, contract labor and non-labor
        support for Navy Readiness Reporting Enterprise (NRRE) and DRRS-S Family of Systems (FOS).
        Live, Virtual and Constructive (LVC) allows the US Navy (USN) to train in much larger context, utilizing both live assets on ranges and employing robust simulator
        capabilities across the Navy Continuous Training Environment (NCTE) training network to create a much larger exercise than normally would be fielded. USN will
        continue linking platforms together and delivering enhanced capability to the Fleet to train with those platforms across the NCTE training network in a Fleet Battle
        Problem (FBP) and then in a Large Scale Exercise (LSE).


Ranges
Fleet Training Tactical Training Range (TTR) Operations: Funding primarily supports civilian and contractor labor at Pacific Missile Range Facility (PMRF), NAS Fallon,
Southern California Tactical Training Range (SCTTR), Southwest Tactical Training Range (SWTTR), Pacific Northwest Ranges, and Western Pacific Ranges. Funds provided are
for operations and maintenance of range instrumentation, air, surface, and subsurface targets, minor range engineering projects, fleet training on test ranges, consumable costs,
Electronic Warfare (EW) threat training equipment, and underwater tracking systems supporting the Navy and Marine Corps, ensuring that both Navy and Marine Corps aviation
units are prepared for evolving challenges and diverse operational needs.

Environmental
Environmental readiness provides range environmental planning; exercise environmental planning, afloat environmental compliance and pollution prevention; range sustainability
and operational management issues; environmental quality; and mission related natural, cultural and marine resource management. Fleet Environmental Readiness Program Effort:
Developing and executing environmental programs to support fleet warfighting, basing, afloat hazardous material disposal, and training in support of operational readiness.
Developing, obtaining, and implementing environmental planning documentation and permits to ensure Fleet training and basing are fully compliant with environmental law,
regulation, and policy. Managing and coordinating compatibility, readiness sustainment, and encroachment actions for at-sea and land-based tactical ranges, Operating Areas,
Special Use Airspace, and Military Training Routes in order to preserve Navy access, training, and certification equities.

Navy Assessment Program
This sub-activity provides CNO War-games support for the Navy Assessment Program's research and evaluation of all DON warfighting, crisis response, support capabilities and
vulnerabilities in order to develop analyses. These evaluation analyses provide Navy's top leadership with the "honest broker" assessments required to ensure the Navy will have
the ability to execute its role as defined by the National Military Strategy to support Deputy Chief of Naval Operations Integrations Capability and Resource Office (N803) Navy
Analytics Studies Program (NASP) Contracts and Financial Management and Plan to Perform (P2P) initiatives. Additionally, funds support the Navy Irregular Warfare (IW)
Office (OPNAV N5), which is to deliver rapid capability to the warfighter. Further, this provides support to the CNO by developing and maintaining an integrated view of Navy
and external analyses and assessments in intelligence, strategy, policy, concepts, warfare capability requirements, along with key insights and implications to the Navy's strategy
and program. Funding also supports Warfighter Development which leads the Naval Education Enterprise, in collaboration with the Secretary of the Navy's Chief Learning Officer
and the Marine Corps Deputy Commandant for Combat Development and Integration, to reinforce individual learning as a warfare enabler through intellectual opportunity and
talent management.

Director of Operations (OPNAV N3/N5) Support Services: N3/N5 is organized for the management of current operations, global force management and continuity of operations.
Provide CNO recommendations and advice on maritime elements of joint plans and policies. These plans and policies shape the geo-strategic security environment, inform Navy
senior leader decision making and provide a global Navy posture that is ready and sustainable, at acceptable risk, to meet anticipated national security requirements.


                                                                                                                                                            Exhibit OP-5, 1C4C

                                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Warfare Tactics
Deputy Chief of Naval Operations (DCNO): for Warfighting Development (OPNAV N7) is charged with advancing Naval Warfighting advantage in order to deter, dissuade, and
deny or defeat adversaries. OPNAV N7's mission and functions follow three broad, interrelated lines of effort supported by a digital platform: warfighter development, warfare
development, and warfighting corps development. Through these lines of effort, OPNAV N7 will enhance individual learning; accelerate institutional learning; and broaden and
deepen our warfighting knowledge to help the Department of Navy overcome strategic challenges. To meet this mission, OPNAV N7 requires support services to facilitate
management and analytical processes.

Naval Warfare Development Centers
Naval Surface and Mine Warfighting Development Center (NSMWDC): NSMWDC will train Navy Surface Forces in advanced Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs)
across all combat mission areas at the individual, unit, and integrated levels; develop, validate, standardize, publish, and revise TTPs; provide operational and subject matter
expertise to Strike Group Commanders, Numbered Fleet Commanders, and Combatant Commanders; and for assigned mission areas as the supported WDC conduct training and
effectiveness assessments and identify gap solutions or mitigations across all platforms and staffs within that mission area; collaborate with other WDC's to ensure cross-platform
integration and alignment.

Naval Aviation Warfighting Development Center (NAWDC): NAWDC will train Naval Aviation Forces in advanced Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs) across all
combat mission areas at the individual, unit, integrated, and joint levels ensuring alignment of the training continuum; develop, validate, standardize, publish, and revise TTPs;
provide operational and subject matter expertise support to Strike Group Commanders, Numbered Fleet Commanders, Navy Component Commanders, and Combatant
Commanders; for assigned mission areas as the supported WDC, conduct training and warfighting effectiveness assessments, set and enforce performance standards, and identify
and mitigate gaps across all platforms and staffs within that mission area; collaborate with other WDCs to ensure cross-platform TTP integration and alignment.

Navy Warfare Development Center (NWDC) Efforts: Revising and publishing Navy Doctrine, Joint Doctrine, Allied Doctrine, Tactical Memos (TACMEMO), Concept of
Operations (CONOP), and Operational Tasking's (OPTASK) to provide the fleet the most up-to-date advanced, cross domain tactics for use in developing training/certification
standards as well as real world operations. Maintenance and administration of the Navy Doctrine Library System online resource (both unclassified and classified websites) making
doctrine available 24/7 to forces deployed globally. Maintenance and administration of the Navy portion of the Joint Lessons Learned (JLL) online resource (both unclassified and
classified websites) making lessons learned immediately available to deployed forces that may be engaged in crisis or contingency operations. Maintenance and administration of
the Navy Concept Generation and Concept Development program, which identifies future operational capability needs and guides future investment decisions. Providing readiness
to Strike Group and Fleet Commanders by implementing war games aimed at facilitating concepts, practice for infrequently used skills, and a low cost high return opportunity to
develop expertise in the emerging DMO concept.

Undersea Warfighting Development Center (UWDC) Efforts: Providing guidance to assess Theater and Strike Group Tactical Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW) warfighting
effectiveness across all communities. Providing training, performance assessments and operational support in Theater ASW to the numbered fleet commanders; developing
undersea concept of operations (CONOPS) and tactics, techniques and procedures (TTP). Principal advisor to the Submarine Force Commanders and forward Task Force
Commanders on areas pertaining to Undersea Warfare. Developing tactical guidance, doctrinal publications, performance assessments, and training solutions for advanced
undersea (submarine, Integrated Undersea Surveillance System (IUSS), and unmanned undersea vehicles (UUV) warfighting readiness and preparing deploying Carrier Strike
Groups (CSG), Expeditionary Strike Group (ESG), and independently deploying surface ships to perform integrated ASW. Providing tactical development and analysis of real
world events with the Tactical Analysis Group (TAG).

II. Force Structure Summary:

The major commands that perform the training in this program, and their facilities, are located in San Diego, CA (Tactical Training Group, Pacific (TTGP) Afloat Training Group,
Pacific (ATGPAC), Readiness Assistance Training Team (RATT), Commander, Naval Surface and Mine Warfighting Development Center (SMWDC), Naval Surface and Mine
Warfighting Development Center Detachment San Diego (SMWDC Det San Diego), Naval Surface and Mine Warfighting Development Center Detachment Point Loma
(SMWDC Det Point Loma), and Expeditionary Warfare Training Group Pacific (EWTGP)), the Pacific Northwest (TTGP Det PACNORWEST), Afloat Training Group Pacific
Northwest and West Pacific (ATG PACNORWEST/ATG WESTPAC), Dahlgren, VA (Naval Surface and Mine Warfighting Development Center Detachment Dahlgren
(SMWDC Det Dahlgren)), Virginia Beach, VA (Naval Surface and Mine Warfighting Development Center Detachment Little Creek), Fallon, NV (Naval Aviation Warfighting
Development Center (NAWDC)), Hawaii (Pacific Missile Range Facility (PMRF) and Afloat Training Group Middle Pacific (ATG MidPac)), Japan (TTGP Det Yokosuka, Pacific
Missile Range Facility Far East), in addition to other range activities in Fallon, Guam, Yuma, Point Mugu, China Lake, and Nellis Air Force Base NV. These activities provide
training to a variety of forces in the Pacific.
Funds within this sub-activity group (4C Warfare Tactics) support a variety of efforts designed to improve and enhance Naval Warfighting capabilities. The primary efforts
include:
          Fleet Synthetic Training (FST) Program
          Naval Continuous Training Environment (NCTE)
          Live, Virtual and Constructive (LVC)
          Fleet Training Wholeness (FTW)
          Quantitative Fleet Feedback (QFF) Program
          Fleet Tactical Training Ranges (TTR)
          Training Support Vessels (TSV)
          Aerial, Seaborne, Land and Underwater Targets
          Various Warfare Tactics and Fleet Training capabilities for instruction, training events, exercises, live and synthetic training and range operations
The Fleet Synthetic Training (FST) Program provides in port and at sea training for aviation and surface units, Carrier Strike Group (CSG) and Amphibious Ready Group/Marine
Expeditionary Unit (ARG/MEU) supported by the Naval Continuous Training Environment (NCTE).
Fleet Training supports development and sustainment of warfighting capabilities to meet required Fleet Readiness and Combatant Commander Forces. Fleet Training forward
capabilities for Air Craft Squadrons, Ships, Submarines, Amphibious Missions, Mine and Undersea Warfare, Intelligence, C5I and deployed staffs, Warfare Commander (Anti-Air
Warfare Commander (AAWC), Anti-Submarine Warfare Commander (ASWC), etc.), Strike Group, and Commander/Staff, Fleet/Force (CTF 80, C2F, C4F, C5F, C6F) as well as
Joint/Coalition integrated training, both live, at-sea and on range, and synthetic courses, classes, exercises and events.

USFF Fleet Training Directorate (N7) executes funding in this sub-activity group for:
       Fleet training support contracts including the Fleet Deployment Training Program Support (FDTPS) that provides staff, Fleet training school house academic and Fleet
       Synthetic Training (FST) support, Small Business Innovative Research (SBIR), Fleet Forces Atlantic Exercise Coordination Center (FFAECC) and Training Support
       Vessel Squadron (TSVRON) Contracts;
       Naval Continuous Training Environment (NCTE);
       Live, Virtual and Constructive (LVC);
       Fleet Training Wholeness (FTW);
       Command Information Technology Support;

                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                   Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                         Detail by Subactivity Group: Op Meteorology and Oceanography
I. Description of Operations Financed:

Funding within this activity group supports the performance of the Naval Meteorology and Oceanographic mission functions worldwide and provides a wide array of essential
operational meteorological and oceanographic products and services to operating forces afloat and ashore. These services include collecting and processing environmental data
using resources such as oceanographic ships, aircraft, satellites, and computing systems. These products and services enhance the performance of active and passive sensor and
weapon systems; optimize the effectiveness of the sea control mission for mine counter-measures; and identify the environmental effects that influence the performance of fixed
and mobile warfare systems and tactics. Hydrographic data from near shore areas supports the production of coastal, combat, approach, harbor and special purpose nautical charts
used to address littoral warfare requirements. General and tailored oceanographic, acoustic and meteorological forecasts provided daily to fleet commanders and individual
operating units from the Meteorology and Oceanography Command's numerical modeling and forecasting centers and from forecasting support activities located worldwide.
Funding also supports the U.S. Naval Observatory's mission to provide Precise Time and Time Interval (PTTI), Astrometry, Astrometry (Celestial Reference Frame CRF), and
Earth Orientation products to DOD and other government agencies. As DOD's designated PTTI manager, the Naval Observatory maintains the Master Clock, and provides
essential PTTI information to space surveillance, communications, and navigation systems, including the Navigation Satellite Timing & Ranging (NAVSTAR) Global Positioning
System (GPS). Astrometry and Earth Orientation products provide vital support for various strategic and tactical programs including precise targeting and positioning of ballistic
missiles, stellar navigation, and intelligence collection and mapping using satellites.

Oceanographic Ship Operations: All days at sea per year (transit, survey, rescue mission, etc.) as well as all days in port per year (VRA, diplomatic, resupply, etc.) and
Geotechnical analysis support and ISS-60 life cycle support systems.

Oceanographic Collections and Deployments: Meteorological and Oceanographic Support Systems such as Satellite Support and Oceanographic Models; Maritime Homeland
Defense Systems; Exercises; Unmanned Underwater Vehicle (UUV) Deployments; Ocean Products; Acoustic Products; Mine Warfare (MIW) Products; Builder Products; Target
Acquisition Weapon Software (TAWS) Products and METOC Support Products.

Weather & Fleet Safety (Deployable Fleet Support): All facets of weather support to at-sea and shore based naval forces, operations and facilities. This entails aviation weather
forecasting products and services for naval aviation, maritime and tropical weather forecasting and warnings, naval resource protection recommendations and operational warfare
support to Carrier Strike Groups (CSGs) and Amphibious Ready Groups (ARGs).

Operational Environmental Modeling: Supports oceanographic environmental characterization worldwide and provides an array of essential operational oceanographic products
and services to operating forces afloat and ashore and supports the worldwide production and delivery of operational meteorological products and services to Navy/DOD assets
afloat and ashore.

Operational Command, Interagency Liaison and Ed & Training: Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW) and Mine Information Warfare (MIW) direct Fleet support such as
deploying ASW and MIW teams and utilizes reach-back cells. Enables capabilities acoustic models and databases, and collection of oceanographic and geophysical data as
required by COCOM Components. Supports CNO's Undersea Dominance initiatives.

Operational Meteorology and Oceanography: Supports the performance of Naval Meteorological and Oceanographic mission functions worldwide as well as Intelligence
Preparation of the Operational Environment (IPOE) for Information Warfare (associated warfare areas and operations), Safety of Navigation (surface and submarine), Safety of
Flight, and Resource Protection. It also provides funding for In-Service Engineering Agent (ISEA) and Software Support Activity (SSA) efforts including engineering and logistics
support for all Navy and Marine Corps meteorological equipment. Funding will enable the timely introduction and support of various meteorological and oceanographic equipment
such as Naval Integrated Tactical Environmental System Next Generation (NITES-Next), Marine Corps Meteorological Mobile Facility Replacement Next Generation
                                                                                                                                                              Exhibit OP-5, 1C5C
         256                                                                                                                                                       (Page 1 of 13)
                                                                              Department of the Navy
                                                                      FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                         Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                    Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                         Detail by Subactivity Group: Op Meteorology and Oceanography
(METMF(R) NEXGEN), Hazardous Weather Detection & Display Capability (HWDDC), Littoral Battlespace Sensing and Unmanned Undersea Vehicles (LBS-UUV) program,
which are all critical components of tactical meteorology, oceanography and safety of flight.
Environmental Satellite Receiver Processor (ESRP): Comprised of ESRP AFLOAT (AN/SMQ-11) and ESRP ASHORE (AN/FMQ-17) systems that enable timely afloat and
ashore Command, Control, and Communications (C3) decision making with weather data to receive Direct Readout (DRO) of Environmental Data Records (EDR). Sources
include national and international Polar and Geostationary Meteorology and Oceanography (METOC) satellites. Funding supports the performance of critical mission functions
worldwide to include Information Warfare (associated warfare areas and operations) and provides funding for In-Service Engineering Agent (ISEA) and Software Support Activity
(SSA) efforts including engineering and logistics support for all Navy and Marine Corps meteorological equipment. Environmental Satellite Receiver Processor (ESRP) Afloat
(AN/SMQ-11) and Ashore (AN/FMQ-17) supports mission planning and execution by predicting environmental impacts to Fleet operations, including support to surface, sub-
surface, and air platforms as well as weapons and sensor systems.

Precise Time & Astrometry Program Support: Enables the U.S. Naval Observatory to develop and maintain the standards for precise time and time interval, the celestial
reference frame, and Earth orientation parameters for the DoD Components required for navigation, command and control, communications, intelligence, surveillance, and
reconnaissance, and weapons systems in accordance with CJCSI 6130.01E and DoDD 4650.05. This program funds upgrades and life-cycle replacement for the Astrometric
Observation System, Earth Orientation System, the Master Clock ensemble, and associated timing distribution systems to meet requirements specified in the GPS III CDD.

Precise Time and Astrometry Network (PTA Network): Provides essential information for navigation, orientation, targeting and weapons guidance, tracking of space-based
platforms, and identification of space objects. Additionally provides program management of the USNO IT Network to include all acquisition activities such as development,
modernization, enhancement, extensions and new capabilities. The United States Naval Observatory (USNO) was funded to remediate critical cyber security vulnerabilities on the
PTA Network. Funds are required to sustain a cyber-secure network at the three USNO sites (Washington, D.C., Colorado Springs, CO, and Flagstaff, AZ).

II. Force Structure Summary:

Operational Meteorology and Oceanography supports the performance of the Naval Meteorological and Oceanographic mission and functions, including six oceanographic survey
ships (T-AGS) operated by the Military Sealift Command, meteorology and Oceanography Centers, facilities, and detachments worldwide. It also supports the Naval Observatory
mission performed in Washington, DC, Flagstaff, AZ, and Schriever AFB, CO, which includes predicting celestial positions and providing precise time for all of the Department of
Defense.
