                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                         Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                    Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Combat Support Forces
I. Description of Operations Financed:

Funding in Combat Support Forces sustains a vast array of programs that support and maintain combat ready forces necessary to respond to national objectives in Joint, Naval, and
combined operations.

Funding supports the operations of the Navy Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC), Special Combat Support Forces (SCSF), other Mission support programs, and Fleet
management headquarters and staffs.

NECC provides Navy forces to operate in an expeditionary environment providing a secure area for forces and logistics to operate through the full continuum of situations from
blue water to ashore.

Ship Environmental Protection, Diving and Salvage operations support all Navy ship salvage and Underwater Ship Husbandry; overhauls, repairs, and maintains the Navy's
inventory of open sea pollution abatement equipment located in the Emergency Ship Salvage Material (ESSM) bases; and performs Navy Certification and In-Service Engineering
Agent (ISEA) support for all Fleet diving systems and equipment.

Funding is provided for acquisition, life cycle management, Integrated Logistic Support (ILS), and Table of Allowance (TOA) Configuration Management for the Naval
Construction Force and other Navy Special Operating Units.

The Ocean Facilities Program serves as the Navy's facilities expert for engineering, maintaining and installing ocean, littoral and underwater systems, as well as for design and
certification of shore based hyperbaric facilities.

The Chemical, Biological and Radiological Defense (CBRD) program supports systems and equipment to protect forces, facilities, ships, and equipment from the effects of
chemical, biological, and radiological contamination and toxic industrial hazards that have been disseminated through conventional, asymmetric or terrorist methods.

Africa Partnership Station (APS) East and West is part of an international initiative developed by COMNAVEUR and COMNAVAFRICA that improves maritime safety and
security in Africa. Training on the continent of Africa is designed to increase maritime domain awareness, build partnership capabilities resulting in expanded safety and security
throughout the region.

US Fleet Cyber Command provides operational support to Navy commanders worldwide, supporting information, computer, electronic warfare and space operations. In addition to
joint and service reporting, the command also serves as the Navy's cryptologic commander, reporting to the Central Security Service and has operational control over Navy
information, computer, cryptologic, and space forces.


II. Force Structure Summary:

Combat Support Forces provides support for Fleet headquarters and training staffs, two Assault Craft Units, one Amphibious Construction Battalion, three Emergency Ship
Salvage Material (ESSM) bases, 71 Landing Craft Air Cushion units, the Naval Facilities Engineering Command Expeditionary Warfare Center (NAVFAC EXWC) and the Navy
Expeditionary Combat Command (NECC).

The FY 2026 request for Combat Support Forces includes $2,291,340 thousand of discretionary and $57,517 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a total of $2,348,857
thousand. Navy Special Warfare, and Navy Expeditionary Combat Enterprise requirements. Further information for this reconciliation request is provided in Section
Reconciliation Bin 20002 of the Reconciliation Exhibit. FY 2026 funding for homeland territorial integrity and security is included in Reconciliation Exhibit Section 20011:
Homeland Border Security Initiatives.

     FY 2024 includes $296,905 in OOC Actuals. FY 2025 includes $178,826 in OOC Enacted. FY 2026 includes $197,337 for the OOC Request.
     Overseas Operations Costs (OOC) are those financed with former overseas contingency operations (OCO) funding.

                                                                             Department of the Navy

                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy

                                               Detail by Subactivity Group: Equipment Maintenance and Depot Operations Support
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Calibration

Aviation Calibration

    Aviation Equipment maintenance

        Provides maintenance and technical support for calibration equipment and standards to improve and maintain equipment ensuring maximum combat readiness.
    Calibration transferred out of the 1C7C line item in FY25.

Shipboard Calibration

    Metrological Engineering and Technical Support (METCAL) program.

         The Test Equipment Maintenance program funds the engineering and technical support for the Navy's METCAL program. The METCAL line funds NAVSEA's support
    for Test and Monitoring Systems (TAMS) Executive Board tasking and Technical Agent support. It provides NAVSEA's portion of the required annual Shared Funding for the
    common Navy METCAL processes, products and data. The program also provides multiple products such as instrument calibration procedures, laboratory certifications,
    answers to Fleet calibration program reports, participation in Joint Navy/Marine Corp efforts, setting initial calibrations for existing TMDE, training material, publication of a
    composite index listing of TMDE as well as the correct calibration procedures and intervals to assure compliance with METCAL TWH requirements and standards. The
    program supports over 400 laboratories, 25,000 plus unique models of test equipment and over 1 million calibration actions per year. This program includes maintenance of
    automated calibration reporting process to reduce Fleet labor and costs as well to improve the integrity of the data collected. The METCAL program collects data from all
    calibration activities and reports the data to the Navy's METCAL Central Data Facility foe enterprise wide analysis and use. The data is used to support the optimization of the
    TDME acquisition program and the assignment of in-service periodicities for TMDE to maximize operational availability.

    Shipboard Instrumentation and Systems Calibration (SISCAL) program.

         Shipboard Instrumentation and Systems Calibration (SISCAL) provides engineering and technical products to assure the accuracy and reliability of installed
    instrumentation used to monitor and maintain shipboard critical systems and equipment parameters. SISCAL minimizes down time and avoids needless damage that may
    occur through removal, transport and reinstalling of instrumentation and system components. In addition, the SISCAL Program reduces shipboard maintenance by eliminating
    unnecessary calibration requirements through application of a refined Reliability Centered Maintenance (RCM) Decision Logic Model (DLM) process. The typical workload
    savings per ship is a 34% reduction in instruments requiring calibration after DLM analysis is performed. The SISCAL program assures that the Ships SISCAL technical
    documentation is current in response to configuration changes associated with overhauls, mechanical alts, ship alts and supply system changes to the installed baseline for each
    hull. Inaccurate SISCAL data leads to potentially severe operational problems such as: equipment safety hazards, false alarms, machinery control system failures and
    malfunctions, failure to achieve full power, loss of propulsion r electrical power. Accurate SISCAL data supports processed that allow time for operator intervention to
    PREVENT latent system problems from going undetected resulting in personnel and ships systems casualties.

    TAMS Support.


                                                                                                                                                               Exhibit OP-5, 1C7C
        290                                                                                                                                                         (Page 1 of 11)
                                                                               Department of the Navy
                                                                       FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                          Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                          Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                     Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                 Detail by Subactivity Group: Equipment Maintenance and Depot Operations Support
         The Test and Monitoring Systems (TAMS) Program requirements are established by the Navy TAMS Executive Director and the TAMS Executive Board, a multi-
    SYSCOM oversight body. OPNAV N8 assigned TAMS Executive Director to manage the Navy requirements for common solutions to reduce cost and improve
    efficiency. Funding supports those functions necessary to operate the entire Navy TAMS Program to the benefit of all SYSCOM and Fleet activities. Products provided as
    common solutions are the Metrology Data System (MDS), the Joint Naval Audit and Certification (JNAC) Program which is used to certify all NAVY labs to a common
    standard, thus allowing the SYSCOMs to utilize each other's laboratories; and Navy participation in several Joint Service Metrology and Calibration (METCAL) Boards
    established to reduce DoD's costs associated with calibratable equipment and improve program performance. Funding supports optimization initiatives via process analysis,
    LSS and VSA initiatives for NAVY METCAL procedures, infrastructure, manning and Logistics.
         Specifically, the Metrology Data System (MDS) collects, processes and disseminates all of the Navy's calibration data. This includes calibration data from all of the
    SYSCOMs including the ground Marines. The data is used to track calibration due dates (intervals), equipment performance (reliability), lab performance (productivity), and
    technician certifications (training). The information is used by engineers to verify that the equipment calibration intervals are appropriate and modify them as needed to meet
    the OPNAV required reliability threshold. MDS also serves as the repository for all Navy calibration procedures. Due to the high volume of changes to these procedures, the
    entire data base is replaced monthly and sent to the Ships, Submarines, and Aviation Units and shore labs.

    GPETE Engineering

         Along with the Navy TAMS Program efforts and products, funding supports engineering efforts used to determine Navy-wide General Purpose Electronic Test Equipment
    (GPETE) solutions. GPETE is used to test, maintain, and diagnose every shipboard and shore system in the Fleet. Efforts include the labor associated with responding to
    emergent Fleet test equipment needs, engineering analysis, requirements documentation, allowancing, obsolescence management, multi-SYSCOM commonality solutions, and
    sustainment of GPETE in support of OPNAV and Fleet requirements to manage test equipment readiness to 85% availability.


External Fuel Tank Maintenance.

        External Fuel Tank maintenance program provides for the support of external fuel tanks during carrier operations. These fuel tanks provide the aircraft with the ability to
    carry more fuel during missions. External Fuel Tank Maintenance transferred out of the 1C7C line item in FY25.

Life of Type Service.

         Contract Maintenance Support or Life of Type Services provides contractor maintenance services in direct support of a small population of EP-3 special mission aircraft.
    Life of Type Service transferred out of the 1C7C line item in FY25.

Overhaul of Ground Support Equipment.

        Depot level maintenance of aviation support equipment used to service aircraft between flights. Overhaul of Ground Support Equipment transferred out of the 1C7C line
    item in FY25.

Airborne Mine Countermeasures Equipment (AMCM).


                                                                                                                                                             Exhibit OP-5, 1C7C
        291                                                                                                                                                       (Page 2 of 11)
                                                                                Department of the Navy
                                                                       FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                          Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                          Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                     Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Equipment Maintenance and Depot Operations Support
         This program provides the fleet with Airborne Mine Countermeasures (AMCM) equipment in support of peacetime, wartime operating and training requirements. Within
    the stated requirements, is the need to maintain sufficient inventories of equipment and materials for the scheduled Standard Depot Level Maintenance (SDLM), maintenance
    upkeep of all helicopters Interface Removable equipment used in the AMCM mission, scheduled/unscheduled calibration of both mission and support/test equipment
    replacement due to damage or wear and tear of out-of-production equipment, including the support of publications and other technical documentation, and funding of the In-
    Service Engineering Agent (ISEA) chartered to ensure adequate and timely technical and logistics support is provided to the fleet.
         In addition, this program provides support for the introduction of the Organic Airborne Mine Countermeasures (OAMCM) programs. These programs will deploy to the
    fleet as sensors integrated into the MH-60S platform in order to provide Mine-Like Object Detection, classification, localization, mine identification, mine reacquisition, mine
    neutralization, and influence clearance capabilities aboard the Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) or other ships of opportunity.


II. Force Structure Summary:
Calibration
The Aviation Metrology and Calibration program maintains aviation core technical capability at 61 Intermediate (I Level) and 20 Depot (D Level) servicing activities worldwide.
The program provides uniform units of measurements necessary to support over 447,000 Aviation Test and Measurement Systems to meet OPNAV and Commander Fleet Forces
Command goals for calibration.

Overhaul Ground Support Equipment
The Aviation Support Equipment program supports approximately 1,992 different end items of support equipment.

      i)Airborne Mine Countermeasures Equipment (AMCM): increase in repair and maintenance efforts to the Airbourne Mine                   10,132
         Counter Measures (AMCM) equipment due to a short-term relief to sunsetting of the legacy AMCM program in the outyears,
         reducing the need for equipment upgrades, replacement costs, calibration, spares, technical and logistics support. (Baseline:
         $32,395)
      ii)Metrological Engineering and Technical Support (METCAL) program: Increase expands on the effort beginning in FY25 to               1,686
         establish a coordinated repair program at Naval Sea Systems Command to maintain 26,000+ Calibration Standards
         (CALSTD) across the fleet. CALSTD are the highly accurate instruments used to calibrate equipment during maintenance.
         Repair and replacement of old and obsolete CALSTDs increases operational availability (Ao) and overall readiness of
         shipboard systems including nuclear reactor maintenances, SMs, Propulsion Diesel Engines, VLS, SPY-1, etc. Targeted to
         raise test equipment Ao from 70% to 85%. (Baseline: $9,249)
      iii)Shipboard Instrumentation and Systems Calibration (SISCAL) program: Increase expands on the effort beginning in FY25 to           1,363
         establish an efficient fleet response process to Technical Feedback Reports (TFBR) necessary to reduce a backlog of 15,356
         TBFRs. Each TFBR represents a piece of calibration equipment waiting in a calibration lab for procedures before it can be
         returned to the fleet. This effort is essential to increase the capability of sailors to perform fleet maintenance. (Baseline:
         $1,959)

I. Description of Operations Financed:

USINDOPACOM Core Operations: Funding in this sub-activity group supports operation and administration of the United States Indo-Pacific Command (USINDOPACOM).
Funding provides for staff, including civilian personnel, travel, supplies, and training.

Civilian Harm Mitigation and Response (CHMR): Supports Civilian Harm Mitigation and Response (CHMR) staffing requirements at the Department of the Navy (DON)
components. Initial staffing requirements are for FTEs. Consistent with the CHMR-AP, DON will establish a range of organizational elements across DON to more effectively
mitigate and respond to civilian harm. These include the creation of Civilian Environment Teams to identify critical aspects of the civilian environment for military commanders,
Civilian Harm Assessment Cells to assess civilian harm resulting from operations, red team personnel to support planning and operations and to avoid mistaken target
identifications, and Civilian Harm Mitigation and Response Officers at combatant commands and elsewhere across the joint force to ensure CHMR activities are appropriately
integrated into their commands' efforts.

United States Forces Japan (USFJ): Funds the operations, activities, and actions within Japan to support U.S. Indo-Pacific Command (USINDOPACOM). USFJ conducts
current and future operations, exercises, and training in concert with our alliance partners to support USINDOPACOM readiness in Japan. USFJ manages the U.S. – Japan Alliance
and is the focal point for planning, coordinating, and supporting U.S. defense issues in Japan. Program increase for USFJ Improvements, increased in funding for critical
improvement projects at United States Forces Japan (USFJ) headquarters. These projects are essential to support the ongoing expansion of USFJ and ensure its continued
effectiveness in maintaining regional stability and security.

Asia Pacific Regional Initiative (APRI): Provides Theater-targeted funding for security cooperation activities in support of the USINDOPACOM Commander's strategy. APRI
is enacted annually in the Appropriations Bill and provides a vehicle to enhance theater engagements and activities in the Indo-Pacific region.




II. Force Structure Summary:
This sub-activity supports the headquarters U.S. Indo-Pacific Command (USINDOPACOM) and U.S. Forces Japan (USFJ).

                                                                         Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                    Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                    Detail by Subactivity Group: Combatant Commander Direct Mission Support
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Funding in this Sub-Activity Group supports the Combatant Commander's direct mission support. U.S. Indo-Pacific Command (USINDOPACOM) is a pivotal component of the
Department of Defense (DoD), overseeing a vast area of responsibility that includes the Pacific Ocean and about half of the Indian Ocean. The command's funding supports a wide
range of critical operations and initiatives, ensuring the security and stability of the region.

USINDOPACOM's funding is strategically allocated to support a wide range of operations and initiatives, ensuring the security and stability of the Indo-Pacific region. From core
operations and Title 10 activities to specialized missions and peace and security initiatives, the command's funding reflects its commitment to maintaining a strong and effective
presence in the region.

U.S. Indo-Pacific Command Headquarters (USINDOPACOM HQ) Mission Operations
Funding in this sub-activity group supports the Combatant Commanders Direct Mission. U.S. INDO-Pacific Command headquarters (INDOPACOM HQ) promotes security and
peaceful development in the Asia-Pacific region by deterring aggression, advancing regional security cooperation, responding to crisis, and fighting to win. Combatant
Commanders Direct Mission funding also supports the Center for Excellence (CFE) and Counter-Drug operations, as well as United States Forces Japan (USFJ). CFE increases the
readiness of U.S. forces, civilian and military counterparts, and international partners to respond globally to foreign humanitarian assistance and disaster relief operations. Counter
Drug interagency, multinational network of partners counters the threat of drug-related transnational crime in the Asia-Pacific region by supporting major law enforcement
operational objectives and initiatives to disrupt and dismantle drug-related TCOs, thereby protecting national security and promoting regional security. USFJ conducts operations,
activities, and actions within Japan to support USINDOPACOM, including efforts to manage the U.S.–Japan Alliance and sets conditions within Japan to ensure U.S. service
components maintain a lethal posture and readiness to support regional operations in steady state, crisis, and contingency and that bilateral mechanisms between the United States
and Japan provide the ability to coordinate and synchronize actions in support of the U.S.–Japan Alliance.

         Pacific Multi-Domain Training & Experiment Capability (PMTEC): PMTEC integrates and improves elements of existing ranges, training areas, and test facilities in
         the Pacific to provide a multi-domain test, training and experimentation capability. It aims to deliver a robust theater posture; campaign with lethal, persistent forces
         forward; technologically superior capabilities to maintain our warfighting advantages in the near, mid, and long-term; and stronger relationships with our allies, partners,
         and friends. The expansion of a widespread and diverse posture gives us the ability to more easily exercise and operate with our partners, deliver, precision fires, and
         sustain force with distributed network of stores, munitions, and fuel to support our operations.

         INDOPACOM Mission Network/Mission Partner Environment (MPE): MPE provides the infrastructure to support multi-national data management requirements,
         information-sharing agreements, and interoperable communication architectures, with the requisite authorities to incentivize intelligence sharing and resilient command
         and control (C2) with Allies and Partners. INDOPACOM is also working to integrate technologies, under the THUNDERDOME project, to implement a Zero-Trust
         approach, which shifts the focus from securing a network's perimeter to securing data.

         Joint Posture Management Office (JPMO): Integrates and synchronizes the joint planning, programming and project delivery activities of the Components for a joint
         posture footprint plan to meet INDOPACOM warfighting requirements on timeline required. The JPMO acts under the authority of the INDOPACOM/J44 Engineer staff
         to action the requirements of the Combatant Command engineers.

         Joint Electromagnetic Spectrum Operations (JEMSO): The Combatant Commanders Direct Mission central source to plan and synchronize electromagnetic spectrum
         (EMS) operations across all domains in support of the commander's scheme of maneuver.
         Deployment & Distribution Operations Center (DDOC): An enduring requirement that provides logistics command and control of the Joint Force and A&P operating
         in the USINDOPACOM area of responsibility.
                                                                                                                                                 Exhibit OP-5, 1CCM
         310                                                                                                                                            (Page 1 of 10)
                                                                             Department of the Navy
                                                                     FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                   Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                   Detail by Subactivity Group: Combatant Commander Direct Mission Support

        Fusion Centers: Supports vital Fusion Centers that will coordinate the efforts of whole of government actions with our Allies and Partners.

        INDOPACOM Manpower Increase - Contractor Support: The requirement for increased contractor and civilian manpower supports INDOPACOM ability to Seize
        the Initiative, which encompasses deterrence, decision superiority, and the ability to blind, see, and if necessary kill our theater adversaries. This critical manpower
        requirement is a key priority for USINDOPACOM and is vitally necessary to deter the PRC, and if deterrence is unsuccessful, fight and win.

        Pacific Movement Coordination Center (PMCC): Supports personnel that will develop a regional distribution network essential to see and understand the availability
        of dispersed forces and distribution capabilities operating in a contested environment. It supports regional hubs in Japan, Guam, and Australia that integrate intra-theater
        movements to the point of need.

        Natural Resource Management: Supports the United States Indo-Pacific Strategy to pursue the following five objectives, each in concert with U.S. allies and partners,
        as well as with regional institutions: 1) Advance a free and open Indo-Pacific, 2) Build connections within and beyond the region, 3) Drive regional prosperity, 4) Bolster
        Indo-Pacific Security, and 5) Build regional resilience to transnational threats.




SEXUAL ASSAULT PREVENTION & RESPONSE (SAPR): The SAPR program is a critical component of USINDOPACOM's efforts to create a safe and respectful
environment for all personnel. Funding supports training, victim support services, and prevention programs aimed at reducing incidents of sexual assault and ensuring a culture of
accountability and respect.

SERVICE SUPPORT TO PACOM - Special Operation Command - Pacific (SOCPAC): As a sub-unified command, SOCPAC serves as the functional component for all
special operations missions deployed throughout the Indo-Pacific region. The Command coordinates, plans, and directs all special operations in the Pacific theater supporting
USINDOPACOM.

        Campaign Effort in the INDOPACOM (IP) Theater of Operations - Maritime Support Vessel (MSV): Provides INDOPACOM and its components the capability to
        conduct operations at the time of our choosing to influence and shape behavior throughout the theater to reassure allies and partners, keep competitors guessing, and
        achieve Integrated Deterrence. In FY 2023, SOCOM transferred funding in support of this SOCPAC program – MSV. In FY 2024, Congress provided funding to the U.S.
        Navy for SOCAPC in support of USINDOPACOM. Currently, in FY25, this SOCPAC program is unfunded.

        SOCPAC Counter Terrorism: Supports actions taken directly against terrorist networks and indirectly to influence and render global and regional environments
        inhospitable to terrorist networks in USINDOPACOM area of responsibility (AOR), which includes areas that support OPE-P in the Philippines.

SERVICE SUPPORT TO PACOM - Global Command & Control Services (GCCS): The United States' armed forces DoD joint command and control (C2) system used to
provide accurate, complete, and timely information for the operational chain of command for U.S. armed forces.


                                                                                                                                                             Exhibit OP-5, 1CCM
        311                                                                                                                                                         (Page 2 of 10)
                                                                             Department of the Navy
                                                                     FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                   Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                   Detail by Subactivity Group: Combatant Commander Direct Mission Support
COUNTERDRUG SUPPORT (Year of Execution Funding Only): USINDOPACOM also plays a crucial role in counter-drug operations, working to disrupt the flow of illegal
drugs through the Indo-Pacific region. Funding for counter-drug support includes resources for interdiction efforts, intelligence gathering, and cooperation with regional partners to
combat drug trafficking.

SERVICE SUPPORT TO OTHER NATIONS - PACOM Title 10 Activities: Supports Security Cooperative engagements. Execute low-cost/high-impact Theater Security
events in and as directed by USINDOPACOM's Allied & Partners Line of Effort and Posture Plan with T10 Grant Aid Eligible Nations.

II. Force Structure Summary:
The force structure of the United States Indo-Pacific Command (USINDOPACOM) ensures comprehensive coverage and rapid response across the Indo-Pacific region. It is
comprised of the USINDOPACOM Headquarters at Camp H.M. Smith, Hawaii; five Subordinate Service Component Commands (United States Army Pacific, United States
Marine Forces Pacific, United States Pacific Fleet, United States Pacific Air Forces, and United States Space Forces Indo-Pacific); three Subordinate Unified Commands (United
States Forces Japan, United States Forces Korea with Special Operations Command Korea, and Special Operations Command Pacific); two Direct Reporting Units (United States
Pacific Command Joint Intelligence Operations Center and the Center for Excellence in Disaster Management and Humanitarian Assistance); and two Standing Joint Task Forces
(Joint Interagency Task Force West and Joint Task Force Red Hill).

This robust structure, involving approximately 375,000 military and civilian personnel, allows USINDOPACOM to effectively coordinate and execute a wide range of operations,
from humanitarian assistance to combat operations and counter-terrorism efforts.

                                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Cyberspace Activities
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Funding in this sub-activity supports all aspects of Cyberspace Activities specifically intended to secure, defend and preserve data, networks, net-centric capabilities, and other
designated systems by ensuring security controls and measures are in place, and taking internal defense actions. This includes access to system controls, monitoring,
administration and integration of cybersecurity into all aspects of engineering and acquisition of cyberspace capabilities. In addition, these activities include implementation,
evaluation, and dispose of information technology and services, as well as, information resources management, and the management, storage, transmission, and display of data and
information.

Administration (Civilian Salaries): Supports NAVWAR (BSO39) cyber programs.

Administration (Finance, Contracts and Management): Funding provides support to Information Assurance Management, specifically Command Information Systems Security
Management and Network Security Management. These systems keep NAVWAR Headquarters (HQ) and Program Executive Offices (PEOs) in compliance with assorted
Department of Defense (DoD) Cybersecurity directives.

Computer Network Defense (CND): CND provides operational maintenance support to fielded computer information assurance defense agents and primary network boundary
protection systems (to include Host Based Security Systems (HBSS) for IT-21 Ships and Outside Continental United States (OCONUS) Navy Enterprise Network (ONE-NET).
The funding also supports conducting shipboard CND operational readiness Computer Network Defense in Depth Baseline Assessments (CNDiDBA) and supports CND program
Information Assurance Vulnerability Management (IAVM). In addition, the funding provides for Commercial Off the Shelf (COTS) CND warranty support to operational system
components, maintenance of logistics support documentation, training curricula maintenance, and ISEA 24/7 Help Desk support. Funding also provides support for cyber
remediation initiatives within the Navy's CND program in order to achieve improved network defense and security wholeness. The funding also supports Navy Cyber Situational
Awareness (NCSA) Common Operational Picture (COP) and the Nuclear Command, Control, and Communications, Navy (NC3-N) system of systems. Funding will also be used
to support SHARKCAGE, providing mechanisms to sense cyber threats across all Navy shore and afloat networks to reduce the complexities of monitoring, assessing, and
detecting adversary activities across multiple enclaves. (e.g. Nonsecure Internet Protocol (IP) Router Network (NIPRNET), Secret Internet Protocol Router Network (SIPRNET),
Command, Control, Communications, Computers, & Intelligence (C4I), Combat Systems, Hull Mechanical & Electrical (HM&E), etc.)

Counter Insider Threat Capability (CITC): Executive Order 13587 and the National Insider Threat Policy mandate all United States Government departments and agencies to
implement insider threat programs that monitor user activity on all classified networks and provide an insider threat analytical and response capability. CITC is the Department of
the Navy's (DoN) implementation of this requirement. CITC's mission is to deter, detect, and respond to the threat from witting and unwitting insiders. The Personnel Risk
Indicator Detection Enterprise (PRIDE) is the materiel solution required to support the CITC mission, and it will consist of two parts: (1) User Activity Monitoring (UAM), which
will monitor user activity on Navy networks, and (2) an Integrated Tool Suite (ITS), which will provide the Information Technology platform for the analytic and response
capabilities. The PRIDE system will provide the technology required by the DoN Insider Threat Hub to comply with the National mandates and to protect Navy data, equipment,
and personnel from insider threats.

Cyber Mission Force (CMF): Operates in support of U.S. Cyber Command's (USCYBERCOM) mission to conduct and synchronize activities to secure, operate and defend DoD
information networks, attain freedom of action in cyberspace and, when directed, conduct full spectrum military cyberspace operations in order to enable actions in all domains,
ensure US/Allied freedom of action in cyberspace, and deny the same to our adversaries.

Cyber Security - Activities Support/Inspections: NAVIFOR HQ is the CYBER/C5ISR TYCOM responsible for Information Warfare (IW) Man, Train, Equipment (MT&E) to
include resource management, IW force generation and delivery to the Joint Force, IW doctrine, policy, and governance, training requirements, and community management. The

                                                                                                                                                             Exhibit OP-5, 1CCY
         320                                                                                                                                                       (Page 1 of 12)
                                                                            Department of the Navy
                                                                    FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                       Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                       Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                 Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                              Detail by Subactivity Group: Cyberspace Activities
IW community encompasses Meteorology & Oceanography, Cryptologic Warfare, Information Professional/Information Technology, Intelligence and Space Cadre. NAVIFORES
provides maximum operational contributory support to active forces as an adjunct to readiness training.

Cybersecurity BASE Support: Information Assurance (IA)/Cyber Security Services include activities, personnel and materials peculiar to the security and integrity of
Information Technology systems and applications. Provides support to Naval Bases, Naval Air Stations, Naval Support Activities, and technical support sites both in the
continental United States and Overseas. It also includes Cyberspace operations, Risk Management Framework, Control Systems Platform Enclave, MOSAICS, Cyber Protection
and Response Capabilities, and Mission Critical Facilities Related Control Systems (MC-FRCS) cyber risk management at the Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command,
Headquarters.

Information Systems Security Program (ISSP): ISSP funding provides Fleet support of fielded systems. The ISSP program ensures the continued protection of Navy and Joint
communications and computing systems from hostile exploitation. Specific field support covers various families of Communications Security (COMSEC), Transmission Security
(TRANSEC), Crypto Data, Crypto Voice, Public Key Infrastructure - Key/Certification management, Key Management (KM) Systems (to include Key Management Infrastructure
(KMI), Electronic Key Management Systems (EKMS) Simple Key Loader (SKL) and Tactical Key Loader (TKL)), and Cybersecurity Services. Fleet support includes efforts such
as: Information Assurance Publications and Policy Evaluations, Certification & Accreditation support. The ISSP program also provides Casualty Report (CASREP) technical
support to fielded systems, In-service Engineering Agent support (ISEA), 24/7 Help Desk support, training, logistics, licensing, system maintenance and repair, and configuration
management.

Joint Deployable Intelligence Support System (JDISS): JDISS provides technology, intelligence tradecraft, and training support for the Joint warfighter. JDISS is identified as
one of the intelligence-related components of the Department of Defense Information Networks and delivers Integrated Imagery and Intelligence (I3)-enabled intelligence
capabilities for command-and-control support and the Global Command and Control System (GCCS) family of systems. JDISS bundles commercial off-the-shelf hardware and
software applications in a standard desktop environment. JDISS provides a field-deployable office automation suite built upon the system security infrastructure provided by
client-server environment system services. JDISS also allows electronic mail and chat between intelligence echelons via the site's existing communications architecture. JDISS
provides access to theater, Service, and national intelligence resources, such as databases, basic imagery analysis and dissemination capabilities, specific analytical tools, and
support functions required to execute the intelligence mission.

Naval Identity Services (NIS): Provides Identity, Credential and Access Management (ICAM) capabilities to the Department of the Navy (DON). ICAM capabilities enable a
zero-trust environment where people and things can securely and dynamically access all authorized resources where and when needed based on mission. This funding will support
services that will enable the DON to: align with DOD ICAM strategy, create foundation for Zero-Trust to significantly improve data security, achieve visibility and control of
identity across the DON for auditability and enhanced cybersecurity, provide warfighter timely access to the right information, enable single identity for life and account
automation, resolve audit material weaknesses identified by the Government Accountability Office (GAO) and automate authentication across multiple network and security
domains. The NIS funding provides support for program management, help desk, system integrator services (installation, configuration and maintaining of the NIS ICAM Service)
and cloud hosting services.

Other Cyberspace Operations is a domain within the information environment consisting of the interdependent network of information technology infrastructures, including the
internet, telecommunications networks, computer systems, and embedded processors and controllers. Other Cyberspace Operations includes resources associated with Information
Operations, Computer Network Defense, Information Assurance, Computer Network Operations, Computer Security Inspection and Compliance Program (CSICP), Command
Cyberspace Operational Readiness Inspections (CCORIs) to assess cyber operational risk to Navy's critical missions and impacts to the DoD information network (DoDIN), and
Navy Cyber Warfare Development Group. The Undersea Warfighting Development Center (UWDC) provides undersea tactical development and doctrine, training, and
certification for all U.S. Naval Forces. These efforts support tactical data information assurance and security and analyses.
                                                                                                                                                         Exhibit OP-5, 1CCY
          321                                                                                                                                                  (Page 2 of 12)
                                                                             Department of the Navy
                                                                     FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                   Activity Group: Combat Operations/Support
                                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Cyberspace Activities

Telecommunications Electronics Material Protected from Emanating Spurious Transmissions (TEMPEST): Implements Executive Order (EO) 13526, Classified National
Security Information Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 2001.51, and Committee on National Security Systems (CNSS) Policy (CNSSP) 300, National Policy on Control of
Compromising Emanation, which instruct federal agencies to protect classified information. Electronic information processing equipment can produce unintentional intelligence-
bearing emanations, which if intercepted and analyzed, disclose the information being processed and transmitted. The Navy TEMPEST Program for collateral and general service
top secret and below implements standards developed by the NSA to safeguard national security information (NSI) from eavesdropping, unintentional leakage, and/or inadvertent
transmission of crosstalk, which could lead to serious compromise of secure communications.

TEMPEST conducts technical investigations and studies of compromising emanations from electronic information processing equipment to detect or deter exploitation of
classified information through technical collection methods, and to determine and apply necessary countermeasures. Navy Certified Technical TEMPEST Authority (CTTA)
designated personnel perform TEMPEST Countermeasures Reviews (TCR) and protected Distribution Systems (PDS) design reviews and provides countermeasures guidance to
ensure facilities with equipment and/or systems processing and handling National Security Information (NSI) are compliant with national policy. TCRs and PDS reviews are
required to be completed before countermeasures are implemented or NSI is processed."

USINDOPACOM: Supports operations identified as Cyber Activity for Chief of Naval Operations (OPNAV), Field Support Activity (FSA), Navy Legal Service Command
(NLSC) and Office of Judge Advocate General Command (OJAG), US Navy Band (USNB), Navy History and Heritage Command (NHHC), US INDO-Pacific Command
(INDOPACOM), and Navy Safety Center (NSC). This includes personnel and operational cost directly associated with Cybersecurity (Information Assurance) and Cyberspace
Operations.


II. Force Structure Summary:
Cyberspace Activities supports Computer Network Defense, Information Assurance, Computer Network Operations, Cyber Mission Forces (CMF), Computer Security Inspection
and Compliance Program (CSICP), Command Cyberspace Operational Readiness Inspection (CCORI) Program, Special Access Program at Naval Information Forces Command
(NAVIFOR), Commander, Submarine Force Atlantic (COMSUBLANT), U.S. Fleet Cyber Command (FCC), and U.S. Naval Forces Central Command (NAVCENT).


Force Structure includes Naval Bases, Naval Air Stations, Naval Support Activities, and technical support sites both in the continental United States and Overseas. It also includes
Cyberspace operations, Risk Management Framework, Control Systems Platform Enclave, MOSAICS, Cyber Protection and Response Capabilities, and Mission Critical Facilities
Related Control Systems (MC-FRCS) cyber risk management at the Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command, Headquarters.

                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                        Activity Group: Weapons Support
                                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Fleet Ballistic Missile
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Funding for the Fleet Ballistic Missile program provides for the operational readiness and reliability of the Navy Strategic Weapons Systems (SWS) aboard fleet ballistic missile
submarines (SSBNs). SSBN forces currently supported are the TRIDENT I (C-4) (disposal) and TRIDENT II (D-5/D5LE) SSBNs deployed in the Pacific and Atlantic.

SWS consists of the launcher, fire control, navigation, test instrumentation, missile, missile checkout, guidance and re-entry subsystems. Funding provides support for all
subsystem equipment aboard TRIDENT II (D-5) SSBNs and at shore facilities. These operational & engineering support efforts include: maintenance for subsystem equipment
aboard SSBNs; equipment renewal and updating during overhauls; repair of failed components; logistics control procedures; operational flight testing; support of crew training;
technical engineering services required to test, analyze and maintain reliability of the weapons system; targeting support; and support to the USNS WATERS for navigation and
test range support. Shore-based missile maintenance efforts include missile processing and handling associated with submarine offload and on-load; missile disassembly,
inspection, testing, and re-assembly; and missile/reentry body mating and de-mating operations. The Nuclear Weapons Security (NWS) program funding provides for the security
at the Navy's Strategic Weapons Facilities located in Kings Bay, GA (SWFLANT) and Bangor, WA (SWFPAC), to include the Transit Protection Program. These efforts ensure
the security and safety of nuclear weapons while in US Navy custody. Fleet training efforts provide for SWS and NWS operational training products and equipment to ensure
incontestable effectiveness and credibility of our sea-based strategic deterrent.

Treaty implementation and compliance support efforts includes both strategic and conventional arms control efforts. Conventional arms control treaty support includes all
implementation and compliance efforts for the Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC), Biological Weapons (BW) Convention, Compliance Assessment Program (CAP), Treaty
Support Program Office and other non-strategic arms control treaties.

Funding also provides for lifecycle support activities for the Ship, Submersible, Guided Missile Nuclear (SSGN) Attack Weapons System (AWS) program. Efforts include the
overall systems engineering process required to design, develop, test, evaluate, procure, operate, support, maintain, and inactivate the AWS.

Finally, funding provides for support activities for the Navy's Conventional Prompt Strike (CPS) sea-launched hypersonic capability program office.

The FY 2026 request for Nuclear-Armed Sea-Launched Cruise Missile program (SLCM-N) includes $44,249K of mandatory funding (reconciliation), which includes +91 FTEs
and program office support. The mandatory funds support the acceleration of the development, procurement, and integration of the SLCM-N.


II. Force Structure Summary:
In Fiscal Year 2026, this sub-activity group will support fourteen TRIDENT II (D-5) submarines, one Military Sealift Command (MSC) Consolidated Navigation/Flight Test ship
to support launch area exercises and navigation testing, four MSC Transit Protection System Off-Shore Vessels (Blocking Vessels), two 87-foot Reaction Vessels, six 78-foot MK
VI Patrol Boats, twelve 64-foot Screening Vessels, twelve 33-foot Screening Vessels and two Missile Processing facilities: Strategic Weapons Facility Atlantic (SWFLANT) in
Kings Bay, GA, and Strategic Weapons Facility Pacific (SWFPAC) in Bangor, WA. Additionally, life-cycle maintenance and operations support is provided for the Attack
Weapon System of four OHIO Class SSGNs

IV. Performance Criteria and Evaluation Summary:



 NUMBER OF UNITS FUNDED
 The Strategic Weapons Systems (SWS) Operations and Maintenance, Navy (OMN) funding (for Performance Evaluation, Reliability Maintenance, Surveillance,
 Targeting/Accuracy Evaluation and Accuracy Maintenance) is not directly relatable to the deployed number of SSBNs or the number of missiles. It is required for the
 establishment and continued operation of a closed-loop operational and engineering support system which: collects data from the fleet; measures weapon system performance;
 analyzes the data collected to identify performance deficiencies; investigates problems identified; develops solutions to resolve the deficiencies and problems; and implements
 corrective actions in the fleet. This closed-loop system operation is necessary in order to maintain the reliability of the strategic weapon system and is required whether there is
 one or fourteen SSBNs deployed with that strategic weapon system in support of the strategic deterrence mission.

I. Description of Operations Financed:
The Weapons Maintenance sub-activity group provides a wide-range of weapons system maintenance and support functions for Air and Sea, Surface, and Sub-Surface Launched
Missiles, Ordnance and Ammunition, Aircraft Armament Equipment (AAE), Aircraft and Ship Gun systems, Surface and Undersea Warfare systems, Anti-Submarine Warfare (ASW)
systems, Mine Warfare systems, Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS), Unmanned Surface Vehicles (USV), Unmanned Underwater Vehicles (UUV), and mission planning systems.
This includes operational maintenance support, intermediate maintenance, depot level maintenance and overhaul of systems and sub-system components, supplemental aircrew for
UAS launch and recovery and mission execution, site stand-up activities to make UAS squadrons operational, reliability testing, in-service engineering and technical support, systems
engineering, logistical, hardware and software support, maintenance plans and procedures, technical data updates (drawings, publications, provisioning information), peculiar weapons
support equipment sustainment, system software maintenance (to include threat library assessment necessary to meet inventory requirements of the Fleet Response Plan and offset the
effects of aging on systems), obsolescence, and component reliability.

Missile Maintenance: Supports maintenance performed at Naval Munition Command (NMC) activities, Fleet Readiness Centers (FRC), and other organic and commercial depots,
joint service maintenance facilities managed by the Air Force and Army, and other Defense and Government Agency activities. Systems supported include the Above Water Warfare
Advanced Data Link Pods associated with the Standoff Land Attack Missile-Expanded Response (SLAM-ER), Advanced Anti-Radiation Guided Missiles (AARGM), Advanced
Medium-Range Air-to-Air Missiles (AMRAAM), All Weapons Information Systems (AWIS), Common Munitions BIT/Reprograming Equipment (CMBRE), High-Speed Anti-
Radiation Missiles (HARM), Harpoon (Air and Surface), Joint Air-to-Ground Missiles (JAGM), Griffin, Long Range Anti-Ship Missiles (LRASM), Precision Guided Missiles
(Hellfire & Maverick), Sidewinder Missiles (AIM-9M, AIM-9X), and other air-launched guided weapons that fall under special access program control. Beginning in FY25 the
CMBRE program will begin to transition to the Standardized Tester of Reprogrammable Munitions (STORM) program as the Munition Application Program (MAP) software
update conduit for missile systems.

Ordnance Maintenance: Supports maintenance performed at Naval Munition Command (NMC) activities, Fleet Readiness Centers (FRC), organic and commercial depots, joint
service maintenance facilities managed by the Air Force and Army and other Defense and Government Agency activities. Systems supported include Airborne Armament
Equipment (AAE), Aircrew Escape Propulsion Systems (AEPS) associated with Cartridge/Propellant Activated Devices (CAD/PAD), Bomb Rack Unit (BRU-61), airborne
rockets, aircraft gun systems, aircraft gun ammunition, Cartridge Activated Devices (CAD), Common Automated Logistics Environment (ALE) associated with countermeasures,
Direct Attack Weapons (DAW) and components, Jet Assisted Take Off / Rocket Assisted Take Off (JATO/RATO), Joint Direct Attack Munitions (JDAM), Joint Standoff Weapons
(JSOW), Pyrotechnics, Small Diameter Bombs (SDB-II), and associated support equipment. This program also includes the transfer in of External Fuel Tank maintenance from the
BA 1 (OMN) Equipment Maintenance and Depot Operations Support line item (1C7C). These fuel tanks provide fleet aircraft the ability to carry more fuel during missions and are
exposed to salt-water environment. If corrosion is not repaired, it can lead to structural cracks and failures/mission aborts, reducing CVN based aerial refueling capability. These
types of corrosion repair cannot be done at the I-level.

Navy Mission Planning System (NavMPS): Provides software and hardware maintenance, user training, and repair and maintenance of Fleet Released software used in support of
Air-weapon systems. Work is performed primarily at Naval Air Warfare Center Weapons Division (NAWCWD), the PMA-281 System Logistics Support contractor facility, and at
shore-based and shipboard sites for Joint Mission Planning System (JMPS). Software maintenance is based on fielded Mission Planning Environments and includes framework and
component sustainment, correction of deficiencies, and Information Assurance activities. Fleet help desk, web portal, and on-site support includes restoring a degraded system to
full operational capability, providing mission planning SME support, and conducting on-the-job training. Sustained Mission Planning System program readiness requires extended
and enhanced sustainment support to affordably maintain system reliability, availability, maintainability until program end-of-life, with a focus on Fleet issues, high priority defects,
and Information Assurance.



                                                                                                                                                                   Exhibit OP-5 1D4D
           341                                                                                                                                                     (Page 1 of 33)
                                                                                Department of the Navy
                                                                        FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                           Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                           Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                            Activity Group: Weapons Support
                                                                   Detail by Subactivity Group: Weapons Maintenance

Geo-Spatial Intelligence (GEOINT) Unified Naval Streaming System (GUNSS): Provides the capability to receive and integrate near real-time organic and non- organic Beyond
Line of Sight (BLoS) Full Motion Video (FMV) and track data with associated Metadata from Navy and Air Force Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance (ISR) Sensors for
dissemination aboard CVN / LHDs and DDGs. The system provides situational awareness to Fleet commanders and the ability to process, exploit and disseminate information with
enough fidelity to be acted upon in a timely manner.

Weapons Engineering Technical Services (ETS): Provides direct fleet support and subject matter expertise for all PEO U/W program offices of on-site technical weapons and
aircraft interface troubleshooting; provides onsite training (formal and on the job) to aviation ordnance and maintenance personnel for organizational and intermediate levels of
maintenance for ashore and afloat activities, to include on-site depot repairs of training and tactical weapons, aircraft guns systems, weapons related test equipment, weapons
transport equipment and aircraft armament missile launchers and bomb racks. The purpose of the troubleshooting and training expertise is to elevate the technical knowledge and
skills of Navy and Marine aviation maintenance aviation ordnance technicians in the installation, maintenance, repair, and operation of all types of air-launched weapons,
components, and associated support equipment to support repair at the lowest level of maintenance and reduce operating and support cost.

INDOPACOM-Special Operation Command (SOCPAC) ISR Program in the Philippines for Regional Operations and support/integration services: Program for flight
hours, contract support, contract services, fuel increase cost, commercial travel, maintenance, and facility support supporting Intelligence Surveillance and Reconnaissance (ISR)
for the Special Operations Command Pacific (SOCPAC) within the Philippine Area of Responsibility (AOR) in support to Secretary of Defense USINDOPACOM integrated
deterrence operations and counter-terrorism operations in INDOPACOM AOR as authorized under Global Compete and Operation Pacific Eagle-Philippines (OPE-P) EXORDs. )
and Cyberspace Operations.

AFRICOM Reconnaissance Intelligence Exploitation Services (ARIES): Provides United States Africa Command (USAFRICOM), its service component commands, and its
strategic and operational partners with enterprise Intelligence, Service, and Reconnaissance (ISR) and ancillary services to achieve DoD outcomes. These services provide support
primarily in the USAFRICOM Area of Responsibility (AOR) and in a continuous manner. These services include the rapid deployment of capabilities and technology in the
domains of airborne, ground, maritime, cyber, and space based ISR to yield a competitive military advantage against adversaries and threat networks. These services provide the
entirety of Command, Control, Computers, Communications, Cyber, Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance (C5ISR) operations to include Mission planning services,
Sustainment and enhancement of ISR capabilities, Collection, processing, and dissemination of ISR data, Full-Motion Video (FMV), Processing, Exploitation, and Dissemination
(PED), Airborne Electronic Attack (AEA), Exploratory ISR research, prototyping, software engineering, proof of concepts, evaluation, integration, and sustainment of emerging
technologies, Evaluation of Commercial Off-the-Shelf (COTS) ISR technologies, Integration of DoD Information Assurance (IA), Cybersecurity standards, and other intelligence-
related capabilities. Weapons systems is not within scope. Use of weapons systems, other than as it relates to the analytical and technical services described is not within scope of
this requirement. Personal protection weapons is not prohibited; however, the Government does not anticipate that contractors will need to be authorized to carry personal
protection weapons under the USAFRICOM Reconnaissance Intelligence Exploitation Services (ARIES).

The Collaborative Mission Planning Continuum (CMPC): Provides software and hardware distribution, user training, repair, and maintenance of Fleet Released Naval Open
Mission Systems and CMPC mission planning products used in support of Air-weapon systems. Software maintenance is based on fielded CMPC and includes software / hardware
sustainment, correction of user / Fleet-identified deficiencies, cyber security, Information Assurance patching and updates, and mission planning web-portal, cloud, and distribution
system updates supporting CMPC. Ashore and afloat on-site support includes restoring a degraded system to full operational capability, providing mission planning SME support,
and providing on-the-job training. Sustained Mission Planning System program readiness requires extended and enhanced sustainment support to affordably maintain system
reliability, availability, maintainability, with a focus on Fleet issues, high priority defects and information assurance. Through the FYDP, the JMPS funding profile ramps down to an




          342                                                                                                                                                      Exhibit OP-5 1D4D
                                                                                                                                                                   (Page 2 of 33)
                                                                                 Department of the Navy
                                                                         FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                            Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                            Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                             Activity Group: Weapons Support
                                                                    Detail by Subactivity Group: Weapons Maintenance

eventual end of life in FY27. This funding will transition to CMPC for ongoing software / hardware sustainment, correction of user / Fleet-identified deficiencies, cyber security,
Information Assurance patching and updates, and mission planning web-portal, cloud, and distribution system updates supporting NGNMPS.

 Air Wing Ship Integration: Provides software and hardware systems support for the Aviation Ready Rooms and Carrier Intelligence Center to permit secure, collaborative
 briefing, with a temporary secure work area capability, and to support mission planning and de-brief operations. Includes system software/firmware cybersecurity support through
 information assurance vulnerability assessment testing and reporting for maintaining the Authority to Operate.

The Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) are supported by Naval Air Warfare Center Aircraft Division (NAWCAD) Patuxent River, Naval Surface Warfare Center (NSWC)
Indian Head, Fort Huachuca AZ, Redstone Arsenal AL, FRC Cherry Point NC, FRC Jacksonville, FL, FRC Havelock, NC, Warner-Robbins AFB (for MQ-9A Reaper Contractor
Logistics Support (CLS), and multiple commercial depots. UAS are classified into 5 DoD UAS Groupings. Group 1 have a weight of up to 20 pounds. Group 2 weigh 21 to 55
pounds. Group 3 weigh up to 1,320 pounds. Group 4 have a weight of over 1,320 pounds with an altitude of less than 18,000 feet. Group 5 weigh over 1,320 pounds and have an
altitude greater than 18,000 feet. This budget line supports the following 11 UAS programs:

         1.       The MQ-4C Triton Program: Provides a high altitude-long endurance Group 5 UAS that offers the Fleet and combatant commanders with persistent maritime
         ISR of nearly all the world's high-density sea-lanes, littorals, and areas of national interest. FY25 will deliver one (1) additional aircraft to continue increasing Fifth Fleet
         orbit inventory

         2.       The MQ-8C Fire Scout Program: The MQ-8C Fire Scout provides a vertical take-off and landing, runway independent, Group 4 UAS capable of day/night
         ship and shore- based operations. Fire Scout provides an airborne, unmanned, sea-based, persistent intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance and targeting (ISR-T) asset
         to the host Littoral Combat Ship (LCS) platform or suitably equipped air capable ships (SEACS) without the reliance on limited joint theater or national assets. The MQ-
         8C Fire Scout system of systems includes the Mission Control System (MCS), Unmanned Common Aircraft Recovery System (UCARS) (required for shipboard only),
         and Tactical Common Data Link (TCDL) for MQ-8C command & control and payload data transmission. MQ-8C payloads include electro-optical/infra-red/laser
         designator-range finder, Automated Information System (AIS), voice communications relay, Radar, and other specialty payloads. The quantities and schedules for MCS
         and UCARS components are based on host ship requirements, while quantities and schedules for air vehicle components are based on operational deployment plans. With
         Navy decision to fully divest MQ-8C, a phased drawdown of Operation and Maintenance, Navy to support sundown tasking as sites deactivate and equipment is shipped
         for disposal including contract closeouts and program/technical data archiving.

         3.       The MQ-9A Reaper Program: Provides medium altitude long endurance (MALE) Group 5 UAS within the larger Marine Corps and Joint Family of Unmanned
         Aircraft Systems (FoUAS) with its unique capabilities within the Command, Control, Communications, Computers, (C4) ISR network. Operational and employment
         concepts for the MQ- 9A will continue to evolve with the assimilation of future systems. The end state is a robust Intelligence, Surveillance, Reconnaissance, and
         Targeting (ISR-T) capability that compliments the strategic objectives of the USNORTHCOM, USCENTCOM and USINDOPACOM areas of responsibility.


         4.        MQ-25 Stingray Program: Provides Group 5 UAS critical persistent aerial refueling (primary) and Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance (secondary)
         capabilities to the carrier air wing as the world's first operational carrier-based unmanned aircraft. MQ-25 will increase available CVW Strike Fighter assets and preserve
         F/A-18E/F Fatigue Life Expenditure. The capability significantly extends carrier air wing strike radius, operational capability, and lethality, lays the foundation for all


                                                                                                                                                                        Exhibit OP-5, 1D4D
          341
          343                                                                                                                                                                 (Page 3 of 33)
                                                                      Department of the Navy

                                                                  Activity Group: Weapons Support
                                                         Detail by Subactivity Group: Weapons Maintenance

future carrier-based unmanned air system operations, and establishes the foundation for the Navy's Manned-Unmanned Teaming (MUM-T) and autonomous operations
from the CVN.

5.       Unmanned Carrier Aviation (UCA) Mission Control Station (UMCS) Program: Provides the system of systems required for unmanned aircraft system
(UAS) vehicle and payload control both afloat and ashore and will include multi-level security, Joint All-Domain Command and Control (JADC2) and other service
network interoperability, future capability for simultaneous control of multiple A/Vs and A/V types. UMCS consists of all ground and ship-based hardware, software, and
networks associated with the planning and execution of flight operations and tactical missions, including the control stations (displays, computers, and servers), line of
sight (LOS) communications, and Beyond LOS (BLOS) satellite communications. MQ-25 Stingray will be the first UAS to be controlled by UMCS. Ultimately, UMCS
will control all CVN-based UAS in the future carrier air wing (CVW), including Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCA).

6.       The Navy Family of Small UAS (FoSUAS) Program (formerly PUMA (RQ-20): Provides Group 1 and Group 2 commercial Nano, Micro, Short Range/Short
Endurance (SR/SE), and Medium Range/Medium Endurance (MR/ME) UAS fielded to Naval Special Warfare (NSW) and Navy Expeditionary Combat Force (NECF)
with scalable airborne surveillance and reconnaissance capabilities to aid in detecting, identifying, engaging, and/or avoiding enemy units.


7.       The Blackjack RQ-21 Program: Provides Small Tactical Group 3 UAS program management, engineering, logistics, and Field Service Representatives (FSR)
support to enable USN Naval Special Warfare units to defeat the enemy in a dynamic environment providing persistent Reconnaissance, Surveillance, and Target
Acquisition (RSTA) support for tactical maneuver decisions and unit-level force defense/force protection.

8.       The Scan Eagle-ISR Services OIR Program: Provides and maintains multiple model types of Group 2 and Group 3 UAS required to support Operation
Inherent Resolve (OIR) mission ISR activities through contractor owned and contractor operated equipment. This support is codified under a Joint Urgent Operational
Needs Statement (JUONS).

9.       The Scan Eagle-ISR Services Sea Program: Provides and maintains multiple model types of Group 2 and Group 3 UAS required to support Sea Based mission
ISR activities through contractor owned and contractor operated equipment. This support is codified under a CENTCOM Request for Forces (RFF).

10.      The Scan Eagle-ISR Services DDG Fleet Program (formerly Scan Eagle-ISR Services 7th Fleet: Provides and maintains multiple model types of Group 2 and
Group 3 UAS required to support Fourth, Fifth, Sixth, and Seventh Fleets with ISR capabilities through contractor owned and contractor operated equipment. ISR Services
provides Over- The-Horizon situational awareness for ships that do not have aviation capability embarked on them. This support is codified through Top Level
Requirements

11.       The ARchitecture and Capabilities for Autonomy in the Naval Enterprise (ARCANE) Program (previously known as Common Control System):
Provides autonomy, executive control, artificial intelligence, and machine learning for Unmanned Systems (UxS) systems. The program will continue to reduce barriers to
integration of autonomy on Naval Aviation systems. This software sets a progressive path for decreased human intervention so that Naval Aviation Platforms can better
perform within contested environments. The redirection to ARCANE has also necessitated logistical, training, and software maintenance requirements. The logistical,
training and software maintenance supports afloat technical support that will meet warfighter demand signal for specialized expertise in operating legacy aviation planning


                                                                                                                                                        Exhibit OP-5, 1D4D

        tools, gathering the data that will inform emerging solutions that boast reduced operator input. Starting in FY25, the ARCANE program has been terminated, with the
        funding reallocated to address higher priority requirements for the Navy.


The Software Sustainment Support Program (formerly Program Related Engineering): Provides sustainment and depot maintenance of Operation Flight Program (OFP)
mission software, including triage, receipt, and responding to Software Trouble Reports (STRs); maintain labs to simulate OFP software to build/test software updates; and provide
Capability Defect (or Deficiency) Packages (CDPs) for OPF software updates. This software maintenance performed at multiple government and prime vendor locations for Air
Launched Missiles (AMRAAM, AIM-9X, ARM, AARGM-ER, Harpoon, LRASM and SLAM-ER), Air Launched Ordnance (BRU-61, DAW, JDAM, JSOW and SDB-II) as well
as Mission Planning Systems. Support for the Fire Scout MQ-8 weapons system has been discontinued due to the decision to divest of the MQ-8 program.

 The Sustainment Logistics Support Program (formerly Program Related Logistics): Provides weapon system sustainment for all Engineering and Logistics Support (ELS) of
 hardware in support of Aircraft Intermediate Maintenance Depot and support for External Fuel Tank systems.

Tomahawk: Provides Type Commander (TYCOM) functions in support of the All-Up Round and Tactical Tomahawk Weapons Control System (TTWCS) by outfitting, certifying,
training and supporting 19 worldwide intermediate maintenance activities (IMAs), as well as weapons stations and Tomahawk loading and handling locations. In-Service TTWCS
requirements include operations and support activities for six software versions. The system includes All-Up-Round (AUR) missiles, Weapons Control System on ships and
submarines. The Tomahawk Weapons System is currently deployed on Ticonderoga Class Cruisers, Arleigh Burke Class Guided Missile Destroyers, Los Angeles Class
Submarines, Virginia Class attack submarines (SSN), and Ohio Class Guided missiles submarines (SSGNs) and Zumwalt Class Destroyers.
Tomahawk is planned for future Arleigh Burke Destroyers, Constellation Class Frigates, and Virginia Class (SSN) with Virginia payload modules, Large Unmanned Surface
Vessels (LUSV), and future ground-launched applications.

Theater Mission Planning Center (TMPC): Provides the fleet with the ability to use Tomahawk missiles. Provides software and hardware distribution, user training, repair, and
maintenance of TMPC software used in support of targeting, mission planning, and in-flight retargeting of Tomahawk Missiles. Software maintenance is based on fielded TMPC
Software and includes software / hardware sustainment, correction of user / Fleet-identified deficiencies, cyber security, Information Assurance patching and updates, and
distribution system updates supporting TMPC. Ashore and afloat on-site support includes restoring a degraded system to full operational capability, providing Firing Units SME
support, and providing on-the-job training. Sustained TMPC program readiness requires extended and enhanced sustainment support to affordably maintain system reliability,
availability, maintainability, with a focus on Fleet issues, high priority defects and information assurance.

Submarine Acoustics / In-Service Submarine Combat Systems

        Fleet Support: Provides for the resolution/corrections to Fleet Problem Reports, resolution of emergent software/hardware problems, and distance support for deployers,
        tech manual updates, and training for combat and sonar systems. Provides for the on loading and offloading of arrays, IMA (Intermediate Maintenance Activities) operations,
        water front storage of assets, driving services, and training.

        Repair/Refurbishment: Provides operational and maintenance support necessary for the safe, reliable, and mission capable operation of in-service submarine combat
        systems on SSN 688, SSN 688i, SSN 21, SSBN, SSGN, and VIRGINIA Class submarines. Provides in-service and systems engineering expertise, logistics and material

       Fleet Support: Provides resolution/corrections to Fleet Problem Reports, resolution of emergent software/hardware problems, and distance support for deployers, tech
