         USN Seats Supported CONUS & OCONUS: Variable seat distribution to the Budget Submitting Office (BSO) ITSM Seats for their selection of devices, applications, and
         seat services based on the organization's mission needs.

Oracle Enterprise Software: Is a commercial database management company that offers software and cloud products. The Strategic Sourcing Services portfolio in the Program
Executive Office for Digital and Enterprise Services (PEO DES) develops and manages the Department of the Navy Enterprise Software License (DON ESL) agreements that
improve visibility of software licensing requirements and costs; reduce stakeholder contract administration; and provide best value to the DON. The DON ESL agreement with
Oracle is an enterprise agreement for commercial software licenses and software maintenance that supports 365 systems and 832,000 Navy and Marine Corps stakeholders (ashore
and afloat). The Strategic Sourcing Services team awarded the latest DON ESL with Oracle (Oracle III, 3rd agreement) on 19 May 2023 with a period of performance from 31
May 2023 to 30 May 2028 (1 base year, 4 option years) and a total cost of $342.5M.

The agreement has two parts in order to provide stakeholders with comprehensive access to Oracle products; 1) a centrally POM funded Unlimited License Agreement (ULA) for
the highest use set of 11 core database and database related products and 2) a catalog of Oracle non-core products (i.e., over 5000 products including Oracle Cloud Infrastructure,
Java subscriptions, Oracle Linux support, etc.) with orders funded in a decentralized manner by Echelon II Budget Submitting Offices. Oracle III provides best-in-class terms and
conditions such as virtualization, and ease of access to key offerings. Maintenance costs are fixed in Oracle III, which results in longer term cost savings and price predictability.
The successful negotiation of Oracle III avoided the potential costs and resources of a software audit that may have been incurred if the ULA was not maintained and the extensive
inventory of licenses and corresponding support had to be repriced.

Navy Enterprise Service Desk (NESD) is a modernization effort to provide an efficient and consolidated IT service desk capability using artificial intelligence (AI)/machine
learning (ML) across all tiers of support and specifically provides live Tier 1 agents to serve the user community. This initiative supports DoD and DON priorities to achieve
savings in acquisition, sustainment, and manpower costs while enabling greater information management, IT efficiencies, and cyberspace security. NESD provides the Naval
enterprise a service that eliminates organizational barriers, redundant capabilities and processes, and provides a consistent and enhanced user experience through a single point of
entry. This enables secure mission-driven global access to synchronize and integrate information. NESD improves the availability and reliability of information and provides the
rapid delivery of new business and mission capabilities in a seamless manner. NESD support C4I, Business Systems, Personnel Systems, and Nautilus (Edge Devices, NVD, and
Azure Virtual Stacks).

Program Executive Office for Digital and Enterprise Services (PEO DES) is the Department of the Navy's (DON) information technology (IT) networks, enterprise software,
digital infrastructure and hardware services provider. PEO Digital is positioned for flexible delivery of IT services to a broad spectrum of users and is digitally transforming
systems to deliver modern capabilities and technologies to help the DON maintain a competitive edge while connecting Sailors and Marines around the globe. Next Generation


                                                                                                                                                                Exhibit OP-5, BSIT
         388                                                                                                                                                         (Page 7 of 26)
                                                                         Department of the Navy
                                                                  FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                    Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                    Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                      Activity Group: Base Support
                                                     Detail by Subactivity Group: Enterprise Information Technology
Enterprise Network Program consists of Navy/Marine Corps Internet (NMCI), Outside the Continental United States Navy Enterprise network (ONE-Net), two support offices and
eight Portfolios of Services.

The Portfolios consist of Platform Application, Infrastructure, End User, Public Safety Systems, Digital Workplace, Cybersecurity and Operations, Special Information
Technology, and Strategic Sourcing Services. The Navy Projects within these portfolios specifically include Hybrid Cloud Services, NMCI Enterprise Services Tool (NEST),
Navy Digital Marketplace (NDM), Naval Identity Services (NIS), Flank Speed (FS), Nautilus Virtual Desktop (NVD), Software Practice and Automation (SPA), Devices and
Images, Mobile Device Management (MDM), Print Services, Virtual Desktop, Core Enterprise Technology Agreements (CETA), Enterprise Software Initiatives (ESI), and
Enterprise Software Licenses (ESL).

This Program funds the PEO's operating expenses and personnel who perform acquisition oversight and program monitoring of the entire PEO DES portfolio, which ensures that
networks and enterprise capabilities vital to the warfighter's mission meet all acquisition, contracts, legal, budgetary, and technical compliance requirements and are delivered on
time and within cost and performance parameters.

Data Analytics and Management Decision Support Tools

Jupiter is the Department of the Navy's Business Intelligence/Data and Analytics environment and is a tenant within the larger Department of Defense (DoD) analytics
environment known as ADVANA. As a tenant of the Office of the Secretary of Defense (OSD) ADVANA platform, DON's Jupiter environment leverages (financially and
technically) the state-of-the-art capabilities and infrastructure investments made by OSD. Jupiter provides data at the speed of relevance to ensure decision-makers have the most
accurate information to drive decision-making. The Jupiter platform has training capabilities that are leveraged across the DON to upskill our workforce to ensure the successful
transition to a data driven department.

Force Level Integration (FLINT) is a digital decision support solution that enables mass exploration of Program Objective Memorandum (POM) alternatives, positioning the
Navy's POM programing process to evolve from a subjective human capital and document-centric process to a data driven, model-centric process that leverages automated
frameworks and computing to serve as the analytical underpinning for developing the POM.

FLINT completed the pilot initiative and transitioned to a funded program of record in 2022 undergoing acquisition as a Defense Business System in the Software Acquisition
Pathway (SWP). FLINT successfully established a NAVAIR Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud sandbox and environments and has reached step 4 of the Risk Management
Framework (RMF) Authorization to Operate (ATO) process for the FLINT pilot software, now known as FLINT v1.0. Operations funding supports management and sustainment
required to maintain the fielded pilot. In 2023 was expanded to host FLINT in the NAVAIR Secure Network and NAVAIR Amazon Web Services (AWS) cloud sandbox and
environment, while FLINT v1.0 continued to undergo the ATO process concurrently with FLINT v2.0 agile development.

FLINT v2.0 supports AWS hosting, Model based systems Engineering efforts, Application Management and Sustainment, Help Desk, Training, and ensures the FLINT v2.0
Minimal Viable Capability Release (MVCR) security posture is continuously monitored in accordance with the continuous monitoring strategy in order maintain the FLINT v2.0
ATO.

Force Level Integration (FLINT) program is being terminated at the end of FY25.

DoD IT Portfolio Repository (DITPR)-DoN provides automated reporting to Congress and Office of Management and Budget (OMB) on Mission Critical (MC)/Mission
Essential (ME) systems, Federal Information Security Management Act (FISMA) compliance and personal information protection. DITPR provides interoperability data to the
                                                                                                                                                   Exhibit OP-5, BSIT
         389                                                                                                                                              (Page 8 of 26)
                                                                           Department of the Navy
                                                                   FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                      Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                      Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                         Activity Group: Base Support
                                                      Detail by Subactivity Group: Enterprise Information Technology
Joint Staff; and supports DoD Investment Review Board (IRB) certification and Defense Business Systems Modernization Committee approval of DoN-IT development and
modernization investments.

DoN Application and Database Management System (DADMS) is the Authoritative Data Source (ADS) for the DoN IT system, application, database, network and server
inventories. DADMS includes the DoN variant of the Defense IT Portfolio Repository, or DITPR, entitled DITPR-DoN. DITPR-DoN serves as the ADS for DoN IT systems,
including National Security Systems.

Visibility & Management of Operation & Support Costs (VAMOSC): Provides the Office of the Assistant Secretary of the Navy, Financial Management & Comptroller
(OASN FM&C) Office of Budget, Naval Cost Policy Division with cost tools as well as the collection and reporting of historical operating & support (O&S) costs and non-cost
data for DON Major weapons systems. VAMOSC is the authoritative data source used by the DON cost estimating community when preparing O&S cost estimates.

Information Technology System (NAV-ITAS) provides capability to track IT-related acquisition procurement requests, ensure IT-related procurement acquisition requests meet
capabilities/mission requirements, and provides a capability for containing cost controls and compliance with the governance associated with DoD and DON organizational
mandates. NAV-ITAS provides a standardized and repeatable process to allow Navy decision makers to have enterprise visibility into the procurement acquisition of IT assets and
total cost.

Financial Management and Defense Business Systems

Navy Enterprise Resource Planning (Navy ERP) is an integrated business management system that modernizes and standardizes Navy's business processes. Navy ERP utilizes
best commercial practices to provide real-time information exchange, unprecedented financial and asset visibility, and improved reporting and decision-making capabilities across
key acquisition, financial, workforce management, and supply chain operations. The Department of Navy (DoN) has designated Navy ERP as the Navy's financial system of
record for all the major acquisition System Commands including General Fund (GF) and Navy Working Capital Fund (WCF) activities. Navy ERP is essential to the DoN in
meeting the 2020 National Defense Authorization Act's requirement to obtain an audit opinion. Further, Navy ERP is core to recording transactions across financial, acquisition
and logistics operations within the Navy General Ledger (GL). Activities will implement mandatory treasury and federal mandates for interoperability and cyber-security to
include decommissioning of legacy systems. The DoN remains committed to the strategy of reducing redundancy by migrating to two General Ledger systems: Navy ERP and
Defense Agencies Initiative (DAI) ERP. The final migration effort will be to bring Commander, Naval Installations Command (CNIC), Naval Facilities Engineering Systems
Command (NAVFAC), and the three Fleet commands into Navy ERP: Pacific Fleet (PACFLT), United States Fleet Forces Command (USFFC) and Reserve Forces (RESFOR).

ERP is the engine for all Navy business functions and serves as the foundational technological infrastructure that keeps DoN operating. The foundational ERP efforts that are
needed to support core DoN business include sustainment, modernization and migration.

Sustainment: Advances core services to Navy ERP end-users through improved ERP issue resolution, operational governance, and cost-effective automation tools. Additionally,
Navy ERP funds sustainment costs associated with the increase in user base resulting from completed WCF migrations post 2019 for Commander Fleet Readiness Command
(COMFRC), Naval Research Lab (NRL), Military Sealift Command (MSC), Expeditionary Warfare Command (EXWC), Depot Maintenance Activity Group (DMAG), Supply
Management Activity Group (SMAG), Department of Navy/Assistant for Administration (DoN/AA), and Bureau of Medicine and Surgery (BUMED), General Fund (GF)
migrations Naval Intelligence Agency (NIA), Field Support Activity (FSA), and Office of the Assistant Secretary of the Navy Financial Management & Comptroller (BSO12), and
Navy Personnel Command (BUPERS). Migrations resulted in a 23% increased user base (from 72,000 to 88,413 users) over the last five years.


                                                                                                                                                             Exhibit OP-5, BSIT
        390                                                                                                                                                       (Page 9 of 26)
                                                                                Department of the Navy

Modernization: Continues to transform Navy ERP platforms to a state-of the art business process platform with enterprise reporting strategy, re-engineered processes, and adoption
of Application Programming Interface (API), which defines the way software and external programs interact. Execute special audit 2028 projects focused on real-time data
integration and analytics needed for business intelligence, audit readiness, and cybersecurity.

Navy ERP recently transitioned to the SAP High-performance Analytic Appliance (HANA), an in-memory data storage database. HANA is the business intelligence data
warehouse, which will provide more robust data analytics capabilities to support data financial reporting. The program began the process of upgrading HANA from bare metal
servers to virtual servers. This upgrade will set the conditions for improved cloud services and cloud consumption monitoring.

Accomplishments to-date include: Completed migration of Bureau of Naval Personnel (BUPERS) and Continued software releases and mock data loads in support of
CNIC/NAVFAC and fleet migrations. Delivered several special projects in FY24: first four phases of G-Invoicing, electronic Procurement System (ePS) phase 2, Foriegn Military
Sales Sourcing, three phase of MILSTRIP Master Data, Repairables Portal Phase 2, Employee Self-Service/Management Self-Service (ESS/MSS) and Supply Order Termination.
Achieved significant milestones in system upgrades, cloud migration, and automation. Upgraded 480 Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) systems to RHEL 8.6 ahead of the
Q3FY24 end-of-life deadline. A comprehensive SAP Roadmap was developed to identify and upgrade critical systems, including Business Objects Data Services/Business Objects
Business Intelligence (BODS/BOBI), HANA Cockpit, HANA client, Content Server, and Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC). All upgrades were completed by Q2FY24,
with BODS/BOBI implemented in Q3FY24. The ThinPrint migration to the Azure Cloud remains on schedule for Q3FY24 completion, addressing limitations with NMCI and
AWS. Real-time system usage tracking was enhanced through AWS CloudWatch, improving resource allocation and health monitoring. Additionally, Ansible was integrated
across all environments to automate tasks, increasing system efficiency and operational support.

Migration: Consolidates Navy financial operations from a collection of disparate systems across multiple commands into Navy ERP achieving a single financial ledger for the
entire Navy. DoN ERP final migration effort will be to bring Commander, Naval Installations Command (CNIC), Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command (NAVFAC),
and the three Fleet commands into Navy ERP: Pacific Fleet (PACFLT), United States Fleet Forces Command (USFFC) and Reserve Forces (RESFOR).

Accomplishments to-date include: Expanded ERP to 20 Commands; moved WCF Commands to single GL and consolidated from 9 to 3 Navy accounting systems; drove
significant cost avoidance and improved auditability from retirement of legacy systems; and established a GF Business Office to provide efficient support to newly migrated
Commands.

Budget profile in FY26 funds the continuation of NAVY ERP migrations, sustainment needs and modernization to enhance system capabilities.

Accounting Systems (SABRS, CFMS)

Standard Accounting, Budgeting and Reporting System (SABRS) is the one remaining DON Legacy GL. Three Navy commands will undergo a functional decommissioning
documenting the removal of all Command general ledger functionality in SABRS beginning in FY 2026 after the migration to ERP the same year.

Command Financial Management System (CFMS) is the primary financial feeder system used by Fleet Forces Command (FFC) and Command Pacific Fleet (CPF). There is now
only one variant of CFMS: CFMS-Consolidated (CFMS-C). CFMS-C is maintained and operated by DON Office of Financial Management Systems (FMS). CFMS-C is the only
Budget Submitting Office (BSO) financial management system that feeds the official book of record (SABRS). CFMS-C will remain as the single feeder system for all non-ERP
BSOs through FY 2026.


                                                                                                                                                            Exhibit OP-5, BSIT

                                                                          Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                            Activity Group: Base Support
                                                          Detail by Subactivity Group: Enterprise Information Technology
Electronic Procurement System (ePS) & eBusiness: ePS is the DON End-to-End (E2E) Contract Writing System (CWS). ePS is the modern advancement to address existing
CWS challenges including outdated architecture, limited capabilities, scalability concerns, and existing disparate legacy CWSs throughout the Navy and Marine Corps. ePS will
utilize DoD standards and support auditability requirements. It will provide the Navy and Marine Corps contracting community with full contract writing lifecycle support with
integration to DON financial systems and facilitated integration with industry and federally mandated systems. To fully function as the DON CWS, ePS supports capabilities
required for DON-level engagement and Enterprise management. These requirements include Wide Area Work Flow (WAWF) – Application for invoices/receipts, inspection, etc.;
Deputy Assistant Secretary of the Navy (DASN) Workflow Hosting Facility (WHF) – Middleware between the CWS and Defense Finance and Accounting Service (DFAS) and
the Procure to Pay (P2P) workflow. The WHF, supports a suite of custom Java/JEE based applications with capabilities that provide Enterprise procurement data validation,
business intelligence, procurement workflow and reporting, and a robust interface prototyping capability.

ePS is subsuming Electronic Business (eBus) as part of the strategy to provide comprehensive an E2E CWS. These two programs complement the mission to contracts with the
necessary wrap-around capabilities necessary to support contracts delivery of material and services.

Standard Labor Data Collection and Distribution Application (SLDCADA) is an unclassified, web-based, customizable time and attendance (T&A) and labor reporting
system provides services to more than 70,000 users in the Navy and Executive Office of the President. The current number of civilians paid through the SLDCADA system are
approx. 65,000 users

SLDCADA is one of two core DON time and attendance systems serving civilians, military, and contractors. SLDCADA Program Management transitioned in OCT2015 from
NAVSEALOGGEN to obtain the following: Cost avoidance of increased hosting charges at DISA, Technical refresh of SLDCADA infrastructure, Additional cybersecurity
measures, Proper alignment of system function to DON IT FAM, and Consolidation of Program Management duties and resources"

Case Management Systems

Naval Court-Martial Reporting System (NCORS): Commercial off the Shelf (COTS) product will become a part of the DON Legal Community Enterprise Solution
Architecture to support the Case Management requirements of the United States Navy (USN) and the United States Marine Corps (USMC) with a focus on the Judicial Actions
community. The Case Management Solution (CMS) will implement the requirements of Article 140a, Uniform Code Military Justice (UMCJ) for case management, data
collection and transparency requirements for DON, USMC and USCG. The CMS must meet annual statutory change per the National Defense Authorization Act and Privacy Act
policies that apply to Case Management Systems required by Article 140a along with Military Justice Act (MJA) 2016.

Cornerstone is a Law Enforcement & Investigation, Case and Evidence Management System (LE/INV) that provides the DON LE/INV communities a case management solution
to improve its operational capabilities. Improvements include: expediting case handling by using electronic case files containing all case-associated documents and information
(from open to closed and retention status), replacing manual paper files, connecting with other relevant systems across the LE community and LE Information Domain, and
supporting future needs and requirements, whether driven by policy or technological, organizational, or environmental changes such as advances in biometric technology, artificial
intelligence, and geospatial information.

Information Technology Support

Cyber Engineering Team (CET): Previously called Network Systems Engineering Team (NSET) is a quick-response, globally-dispersed cyber maneuvering force providing
unbounded, operationally-focused technical expertise and systems engineering services across all Navy networks in support of Commander TENTH Fleet (C10F) operational
outcomes. The CET's primary mission is to rapidly respond to both tactical and operational-level Department of Defense Information Networks – Navy (DoDIN-N) requirements
                                                                                                                                                      Exhibit OP-5, BSIT
       392                                                                                                                                                (Page 11 of 26)
                                                                             Department of the Navy
                                                                     FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                          Activity Group: Base Support
                                                         Detail by Subactivity Group: Enterprise Information Technology
to ensure resilient network communications and assured command and control capability is available in support of global U.S. Navy operations. In the event of a cyber-attack or
major cyber incident, CET is authorized and positioned to provide Tier IV technical response, emergent network maneuver(s) and/or configuration changes(s) as required to
support C10F mission needs. In addition to identifying capability gaps and developing and delivering solutions to the operational community, CET collaborates with Type
Commanders, System Commanders, Fleet Commanders, and Combatant Commanders at the acquisition, engineering, and operational levels to identify, address, and resolve
enterprise-wide problems at scale. The CET, under Commander Task Force 1010, serves alongside Naval Network Warfare Command to operate and secure as well as direct and
maneuver the Navy's portion of the DoDIN ensuring Fleet platforms, Network Operations Centers, Naval Computer and Telecommunications Area Master Station (NCTAMS) and
other communication nodes and network services Navy-wide are available, resilient, and interoperable in support of Joint and Naval Operations.

CET personnel around the globe align with their respective Task Group Commander and fully integrate with their Task Force counterpart to respond, assess, and advise on
DODIN-N incidents, problems, and operations to support the ability to operate and maneuver the Navy's networks as a warfighting platform. A combination of Navy Working
Capital Fund government civilians, active duty and naval reserve military, and contractor personnel, CET members serve as dedicated, on-site, deployment-capable subject matter
experts in: enterprise architecture, cloud architecture, service architecture, cybersecurity, and network services to support end-to-end network operations and cyber defense.

Telephony provides maintenance and in service engineering efforts that sustain 107 base phone systems that provide classified and unclassified voice and video services to the
Fleet, Maritime Operations Centers (MOCs), and shore C2 users at approximately 300 locations worldwide, in support of DoD's Everything over Internet Protocol (EoIP) mandate.
Systems include Voice and Video over Internet Protocol (VVoIP), Voice and Video over Secure Internet Protocol (VVoSIP), 911/Enhanced 911, and Video Teleconferencing
(VTC) and Telephony Situational Awareness Management System (T-SAMS). Telephony systems support Public Safety Communications such as emergency/911 dialing, blast
dialing (e.g. Active Shooter Warnings), alarms, elevator phones, 911 call boxes, and Supervisory Control And Data Acquisition (SCADA) networks. In addition, Telephony
supports cable plant upgrades and repairs at OCONUS and CONUS fleet concentration bases and stations. In addition, funding provides for software renewals and all areas of
program management support.

Identity, Credential, and Access Management (ICAM) Implementation provides centralized funding to support the DoD's requirement that 100% of all systems relevant to the
audit of financial statements implement the ICAM solutions to comply with Federal Information Systems Control Audit Manual (FISCAM). ICAM provides Naval Identity
Services (NIS) solutions for DON Audit Relevant Systems across the Acquisition Financial Management, Logistics, Human Resources and Legal portfolios to comply with Federal
Information Technology (IT) management laws. ICAM is an important cybersecurity domain that allows the DON to securely access resources across existing systems and
emerging platforms. Individual modules of ICAM provide for the following: external data checking, access recertification, segregation of duties, account management/automated
account provisioning, custom reporting, admin password vaulting, admin auditing, admin account management, portal access, single sign on, multifactor authentication,
authentication, and density synchronization (supports Denied, Degraded, Intermittent, and Latent (DDIL) environments), attribute acquisition and global naval user store.

Department of Navy Chief Information Office (DON CIO) Enterprise Support is responsible for DON Information Management (IM), IT and Information Resource
Management (IRM). To support the DON IM/IT requirements and enable the Information Superiority Vision (ISV), DON CIO provides the policy and oversight to enable
effective and efficient, trusted and shared IM/IT/IRM, cybersecurity, and cyber/IT workforce enterprise capabilities. DON CIO leads all matters involving enterprise architecture,
electromagnetic spectrum, data strategy, cybersecurity, cyber readiness, records management, privacy, information disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), and
civil liberties.

DON CIO is responsible for modernizing IT infrastructure, innovating and deploying new capability, and defending naval information wherever it resides.
The Office of the Chief Information Officer (OCIO) oversees the IM, IT, and IRM functions within the DON, and works closely with civilian executive and staff assistants to unify
DON efforts to design, deliver, operate, and defend a fully integrated naval enterprise network that will drive competitive advantage and increase naval combat power.

II. Force Structure Summary:

Next Generation Enterprise Network (NGEN) supports two geographical service areas:
CONUS service areas encompass Alaska, Hawaii, Guantanamo Bay (Cuba), Puerto Rico, and Japan for nearly 700,000 Navy and Marine Corps uniform and civilian workforce
members and is operational on DON CONUS facilities, Hawaii and Japan leveraging 23 Classified and Unclassified Data Centers and 28 Micro-server farms to provide 24/7
enterprise level service to: 3 Network Operation Center, 3 Enterprise Service Desks, and approximately 2500 sites.
         OCONUS provides for operations and maintenance functions associated with the network infrastructure at U.S. Navy OCONUS fleet concentration bases and stations for
         nearly 43,000 users and supports multiple Global locations:
                   14 major ONE-Net locations
                   3 main regions (Middle East, Far East and Europe)
                   28 global pier side locations (20 CONUS/8 OCONUS)

Oracle Enterprise Software has consolidated all its Shore and Afloat Oracle contracts under a single contract under Program Executive Office for Digital and Enterprise Services
(PEO-DES). Centralized oversight and management of a single DON Enterprise Software Licensing (ESL) agreement with Oracle will increase visibility and control of software
licensing costs, while reducing Budget Submitting Office (BSO) contract administration workload and unit costs since BSOs will no longer need to independently acquire and
manage software licenses for their supporting commands and activities. The Navy Afloat and Ashore ELAs supported 832,000 Ashore-based users and 100,000 Afloat-based
users.

Navy Enterprise Service Desk (NESD) program is to coordinate all IT help desks, services, processes and personnel resources in a consolidation effort. NESD processes over
1.5M calls annually. The intention of the effort is to reduce operational costs while leveraging enterprise services. The Department of the Navy will spend approximately $860
million, on 157 Help Desks, over the next six years. Through the proposed consolidation of those Help Desks into one Service Desk at three locations, a proposed cumulative
savings of $150 million can be achieved while streamlining and standardizing Naval IT procedures. The standardization of processes alone will immeasurably positively affect our
warfighters.

Program Executive Office Digital Enterprise Services (PEO DES) supports two support offices and eight Portfolios of Services

        ONE-Net operations and maintenance support to 75 OCONUS bases and stations. NGEN/NGEN-R support to approximately 277 active commands across 98 separate
        sites within U.S. Fleet Forces Command

Data Analytics and Management Decision Support Tools
       Force Level Integration (FLINT) 100 users
       DoD IT Portfolio Repository (DITPR) supports 39 stakeholders across the Defense Enterprise
       DADMS 9,000 users

          NMCI Enterprise Services Tool (NEST), Integrated Technical Item Management and Procurement (ITIMP), and SeaPort; by
          30 June 2026. This initiative aligns with the Secretary of Defense's priority of achieving a clean audit by FY28, ensuring
          greater financial accountability and streamlined procurement operations. The acceleration is mandated by the Joint ASN
          (FM&C)/ASN (RD&A) memo Legacy Entitlement System Consolidation to Navy Enterprise Resource to Pay Office
          Department of Defense Activity Address Code Guidance. (Baseline: $22,280)
      vii)NGEN - Non-IT Infrastructure (NMNI): Program increase to replace Hazardous Condition (HAZCON), End of Life (EoL)                  8,355
          and End of Service (EoS) non-IT infrastructure at critical sites including: emergency power generators, Uninterruptible
          Power Supplies (UPSs), Heating/Ventilation/Cooling (HVAC), physical security and fire suppression systems. Replacing
          failed and aging non-IT infrastructure will greatly increase site resiliency and reduce the chance of network
          interruptions/outages causing Critical Incident Reports that negatively impact Fleet operations. (Baseline: $8,674)
      viii)Provides funding to support enhanced U.S. posture in the COCOM area of responsibility. This includes support for                  750
          personnel who are currently deployed or preparing for deployments to and within the European theater of operations.

                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                        Budget Activity: Operating Forces
                                                                          Activity Group: Base Support
                                                    Detail by Subactivity Group: Sustainment, Restoration and Modernization
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Facility Sustainment, Restoration, and Modernization (FSRM) includes funding for shore activities supporting ship, aviation, combat operations and operational support forces.
FSRM funding enables maintenance, repair and minor construction for all buildings, structures, utility systems and grounds required for assigned forces and tenants to perform
their missions. This program includes facility sustainment (ST), restoration and modernization (RM), consolidation and demolition (DE), new footprint (NF) construction, and
logistics activities. These programs accomplish work through a combination of recurring maintenance contracts, contracted special projects and government forces. Facility
sustainment includes regularly scheduled maintenance, emergency services and cyclical major repairs to preserve shore infrastructure in working order over its expected service
life. Restoration and modernization include repairs to restore facilities degraded by inadequate sustainment, excessive age or other damage. It also funds modifications required to
support currently assigned missions and implement new or higher facility standards. Demolition includes facility renovations to consolidate existing commands and missions
within existing facilities footprint and disposal of excess, decayed or obsolete facilities that are beyond economic repair. New footprint provides construction below the military
construction (threshold) and addresses facility quantities deficiencies. Logistics activities include administration, planning, design, contracting, and construction oversight.

II. Force Structure Summary:
Force Structure includes Naval Bases, Naval Stations, Naval Air Stations, Navy Support Activities, and technical support sites in continental United States and overseas consisting
of 62,960 facilities funded by the Facility Sustainment, Restoration, and Modernization (FSRM) line item. Commander, Navy Installations Command (CNIC) is assigned as the
Type Command (TYCOM) to oversee the installation-level maintenance for facilities. Commander, Naval Facilities Engineering Systems Command (NAVFAC) is assigned as
the Systems Command (SYSCOM) to serve as the materiel agency with technical and acquisition authority to provide lifecycle management of facility systems to include depot-
level maintenance.

The FY 2026 request for Sustainment, Restoration and Modernization includes $3,991,438 thousand of discretionary and $1,686,916 thousand of mandatory (reconciliation) for a
total of $5,678,354 thousand. The mandatory funds provide for Puget Sound Naval Shipyard (PSNS) Dry Dock 4 seismic repairs; facility sustainment to 85% of the modeled
requirement; poor or failing condition unaccompanied housing recapitalization; and supplements the Navy’s restoration and modernization backlog. Further information for this
reconciliation request is provided in Sections 20001 and 20002 of the Reconciliation Exhibit. FY 2026 funding for homeland territorial integrity and security is included in
Reconciliation Exhibit Section 20011: Homeland Border Security Initiatives.

                                                                            Activity Group: Base Support
                                                                 Detail by Subactivity Group: Base Operating Support
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Base Operating Support (BOS) for Navy installations enables sea, air, and land operations for the Navy's fleet forces. Support functions include port and airfield operations,
utilities management and energy security, supply logistics, fleet ground transportation, environmental planning and compliance, security, fire and emergency services, personnel
support, unaccompanied housing, temporary lodging, facilities planning and contract award/management, custodial services, grounds maintenance, child care, galley functions,
information technology, training, safety programs, and morale, welfare and recreation.

II. Force Structure Summary:
Force Structure includes 70 installations in 13 countries that is lead by a team of approximately 48,000 military, civilian, and contractor personnel to deliver installation services
for over 1,400,000 military (active, reserve, and retired), civilian, and family members.

                                                           Detail by Subactivity Group: Ship Prepositioning and Surge
I. Description of Operations Financed:
The Navy's Sealift Program provides the worldwide capability to deploy combat forces and/or supporting material that may be required to meet national contingency objectives.
This Line Item is divided into three functional areas: (1) Prepositioning (2) Sealift Program Support and (3) Exercise Support

Prepositioning
Afloat Prepositioning strategically places military equipment and supplies aboard ships located in key ocean areas to ensure rapid availability during a major theater war, a
humanitarian operation or other contingency. Prepositioning ships provide quick and efficient movement of military gear between operating areas without reliance on other
nations' transportation networks. Many prepositioning ships are able to discharge liquid, containerized or motorized cargo both pier side or while anchored offshore by using
floating hoses and shallow-draft watercraft, called lighterage, that are carried aboard. This allows cargo to be ferried to shore in areas where ports are non-existent or in poor
condition and gives the nation's military forces the ability to operate in both developed and undeveloped areas of the world. These ships operate within the Navy Working Capital
Fund (NWCF).

Sealift Program Support
The Sealift Support Program Office (SSPO) provides cradle to grave life cycle management for all Amphibious Sealift systems. This function includes development and design of
new or replacement equipment, acquisition of new or recapitalized equipment, maintenance and repair to deployed equipment, and disposal of obsolete equipment. The systems
include: Improved Navy Lighterage System (INLS), Navy Lighterage (NL), Maritime Prepositioning Force (MPF) Utility Boars, Lighter, Amphibious Resupply, Cargo, 5-Ton
(LARC-V), Landing Craft, Mechanized (LCM-8), Elevated Causeway, Modular, Amphibious Bulk Liquid Transfer System (ABLTS), Travel Lifts, Mobil Lighterage Transfer
System (MLTS), and Off Shore Petroleum Discharge System (OPDS).

Exercise Support
Provides funding outside of a working capital fund for exercises of the Aviation Maintenance ships, Prepositioning ships, and the Naval Beach Groups.

                                                            Activity Group: Ready Reserve And Prepositioning Forces
                                                                Detail by Subactivity Group: Ready Reserve Force
I. Description of Operations Financed:
As of FY 2026, the Surge Sealift program is under single management by the Department of Transportation's (DoT) Maritime Administration (MARAD) with Military Sealift
Command (MSC) transferring all remaining Surge Fleet vessels and five Army Prepositioning Stock vessels to the Ready Reserve Force (RRF) program. Vessels in the RRF
program are maintained in a Reduced Operating Status (ROS). ROS ships have a cadre crew assigned, are out-ported at a lay-berth, and undergo annual sea trials, periodic dock
trials, and required periodic regulatory dry-docking/inspections. The Surge Sealift program provides over ten million square feet of cargo carrying capacity to U.S. Transportation
Command (USTC) to transport Army, Marine Corps, Navy, Air Force and other unit's equipment, combat support equipment, and supplies when needed.

Vessel Recapitalization: MARAD is the contracting agency for the acquisition of used sealift vessels. Used sealift vessels are purchased with Ship Conversion, Navy (SCN),
modified, and outfitted to meet baseline readiness, American Bureau of Shipping and U.S. Coast Guard regulatory requirements with Operation and Maintenance, Navy (OMN).
These vessels are placed in a ROS status in the RRF.

Mobility Alterations (MOBALTS) provides funding for emergent statutory and regulatory modifications required for ships to maintain certificates of inspection.

The FY26 Ready Reserve Force budget accounts for:

         Modification and outfitting of up to two used sealift acquisitions.
         Retirement of last two remaining Fast Sealift Ships (FSS).
         Transferring remaining 5 Watson-class Large Medium Speed Roll-On/Roll-Off (LMSR) ships from the Army Afloat Pre-positioned Stock
         (APS) program into the RRF program.
         Maintaining current RRF vessels in a ROS-5 status.
         Purchasing real property located in Orange County, TX and Tacoma, WA to develop infrastructure needed to strategically locate and
        service assigned RRF ships in support of USTC sealift requirements.
         Emergent modifications required to maintain regulatory certificates.

                                                                          Budget Activity: Mobilization
                                                                     Activity Group: Activations/Inactivations
                                                              Detail by Subactivity Group: Activations/Inactivations
I. Description of Operations Financed:
1. Ship Inactivation
This subactivity supports Non-nuclear/Conventional Inactive Ship Maintenance Facility Support, Non-nuclear/Conventional Surface Ship Inactivation, Nuclear Surface Ship
Inactivation, and Nuclear Submarine Inactivation.

Non-nuclear/Conventional Inactive Ship Maintenance Facility Support
Provides for the operation of three Government-Owned-Contractor-Operated (GOCO) Inactive Ship On-Site Maintenance Offices (ISMO) at Bremerton, WA, Pearl Harbor, HI,
and Philadelphia, PA. Operations include inactivation, storage, maintenance, and preparation of ships for disposal.

Non-nuclear/Conventional Surface Ship Inactivation
Inactivation of conventionally-powered ships includes depot level inactivation work to layup a ship. The composition and disposition of inactive ships is reviewed annually to
determine the number of ships held for retention. Funding includes the domestic dismantling of ships, contract administration of dismantling contracts, environmental surveys,
vessel disposal preparations, and unmooring for disposal which may be accomplished over multiple years.

Ships typically begin the inactivation process at the commencement of the inactivation availability. Ships are decommissioned when removed from active status. Once
decommissioned, ships remain in an inactivate status for retention, material reutilization, or pending disposal.

Ship disposal is accomplished by several methods including:
-Dismantle
-Interagency Government Transfer
-Foreign Military Sales (FMS)
-Donation
-Fleet Training (Weapons Effects Tests and Sinking Exercise).

Nuclear-Powered Ship and Submarine Inactivation
Responsible for all technical, environmental, and international treaty requirements associated with the safe and responsible inactivation and disposal of U.S. Navy nuclear-powered
ships. Nuclear-powered ships and submarines typically take several years between inactivation and decommission. They are placed in an in commission, in reserve status as of the
date of the start of their inactivation availability and decommissioned upon release of the crew following fuel removal. Nuclear-powered ships and submarines take years to fully
dispose and recycle.

This program includes all aspects of nuclear-powered ship inactivation and disposal including:

-Advance Planning
Funds the development shipyard dry-dock schedules, availability work schedules and key events, engineering and design drawings, surveys, nuclear training of defueling team,
material procurement, and demilitarization methodology and identification.

-Inactivation without Reactor Compartment Disposal (RCD) and Hull Recycling
Includes inactivation, including reactor defueling and tow preparations. Upon completion, the hull is towed to Puget Sound Naval Shipyard Intermediate Maintenance Facility for
future reactor compartment disposal and hull recycling.
                                                                                                                                                          Exhibit OP-5, 2B2G
         451                                                                                                                                                    (Page 1 of 18)
                                                                                 Department of the Navy
                                                                        FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                           Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                             Budget Activity: Mobilization
                                                                        Activity Group: Activations/Inactivations
                                                                 Detail by Subactivity Group: Activations/Inactivations

-Reactor Compartment Disposal and Hull Recycling (RR)
Includes the disposal of defueled reactor compartment and hull recycling. Reactor compartment subsequently transported to Handford Land Burial Site for final disposition. Hull
recycling includes removal of components for reuse and the demilitarization/declassification of components and materials to be recycled.

-Decontamination
Includes the decontamination and declassification of tender nuclear facilities.

2. Aircraft Inactivation
This program provides long-term storage for inactive aircraft to include aircraft induction and preservation, re-preservation of war reserve aircraft, maintenance, safety/security
inspections of all stored aircraft, and disposal at the Aerospace Maintenance and Regeneration Center (AMARC) located at Davis-Monthan Air Force Base. Aircraft disposal
includes reutilization of parts, production/special tooling and support equipment, and disposal of airframes, engines and other equipment.

Inductions:
The aircraft induction process can last up to 60 days due to aircraft inventory, de-fueling, and preservation. After induction, aircraft are placed into one of the following categories
of storage:

-Inactive Reserve – Aircraft must be ready to fly in 30 days, no parts removal
-CNO Special Program - Stricken aircraft held in storage
-Foreign Military Sales (FMS)/Security Assistance Programs (SAP) – Stricken aircraft on hold awaiting disposition decision
-Museum – Stricken aircraft identified for donation to service museums
-Reclamation/Disposition – Stricken aircraft awaiting final reclamation/disposition
-Disposal – Aircraft transferred for disposal

Re-Preservation:
Every 4 years, aircraft that are stored in the CNO Special Program category undergo a complete re-preservation. The aircraft are stripped of all preservation material and washed
prior to re-preservation.

In-storage Maintenance:
Every 120 days, all Navy aircraft stored at AMARC receive an inspection to ensure that a high level of preservation is in place to maintain the integrity of the aircraft. Preservation
material is inspected for degradation, tires and struts are checked for proper inflation, tie downs are checked to ensure the aircraft are properly secured, and flight control battens are
inspected for proper installation to prevent damage to the aircraft in high winds. Any discrepancies are corrected at the time of the inspection. The inspections are staggered on a
monthly basis so that not all 1,554 aircraft are due at the same time. Also included is the inspection of engines and propellers that are stored at AMARC to ensure that the integrity
of the containers and the components are maintained. This process also includes the helicopter blades that are stored in the blade farm.

Cost of inductions, re-preservation, and in-storage maintenance vary with type and model of the aircraft, demilitarization efforts, and preservation requirements of the aircraft.
Aircraft storage requirements are reviewed at least yearly.

IV. Performance Criteria and Evaluation Summary:
 Ship Inactivations ($000)

The below supplemental information is provided in response to 10 U.S. Code § 8691 - Nuclear-powered aircraft carriers: dismantlement and disposal:

SUPPLEMENTAL INFORMATION WITH BUDGETS.—In the materials submitted to Congress by the Secretary of Defense in support of the budget of the President for a fiscal year
(as submitted to Congress under section 1105(a) of title 31), the Secretary of the Navy shall include information on each dismantlement and disposal of a nuclear-powered
aircraft carrier occurring or planned to occur during the period of the future-years defense program submitted to Congress with that budget. Such information shall include, by
ship concerned, the following:
          (1) A summary of activities and significant developments in connection with such dismantlement and disposal.
          (2) If applicable, a detailed description of cost and schedule performance against the baseline for such dismantlement and disposal established pursuant to subsection
(a), including a description of and explanation for any variance from such baseline.
          (3) A description of the amounts requested, or intended or estimated to be requested, for such dismantlement and disposal for each of the following:
                    (A) Each fiscal year covered by the future-years defense program.
                    (B) Any fiscal years before the fiscal years covered by the future-years defense program.
                    (C) Any fiscal years after the end of the period of the future-years defense program.

Ex-Enterprise (CVN-65)
          (1)          A summary of activities and significant developments in connection with such dismantlement and disposal.

             The Navy has made significant progress in the dismantlement and disposal (D&D) of ex-CVN 65, with a Record of Decision (ROD) issued in
             October 2023 following the Environmental Impact Statement (EIS). The selected alternative involves dismantling the ship through commercial
             nuclear decommissioning services and ship recycling contractors. In October 2024, the Navy published a Firm Fixed Price (FFP) commercial
             D&D contract Request for Proposal (RFP). Proposals were received in January 2025, and a contract award is anticipated by Q3FY25.


          (2)             Detailed description of cost and schedule performance against the baseline.

             The Navy completed a business case assessment with industry to evaluate cost and schedule. Contracting is underway, with proposals received
             detailing cost and schedule data in line with contract requirements. Execution and performance monitoring reports will be submitted throughout
             the period of performance. The D&D contract encompasses engineering, planning, and execution efforts, with reports provided to the on-site
             government representative and Contracting Officer's Representative (COR). Quarterly meetings will assess actual performance against the
             project schedule to ensure contract compliance.

USS Nimitz (CVN 68)
 A summary of activities and significant developments in connection with such dismantlement and disposal.
   The current plan for CVN 68 includes national Fleet tasking through May 2026, followed by the Ship’s Terminal Offload Plan (STOP),
   Inactivation and Defueling. Dismantlement and Disposal (D&D) is scheduled to begin in 2030. A review and update of the Environmental
   Impact Statement (EIS) and a government cost estimate are required for the D&D effort. Once completed, the ex-CVN 65 D&D and EIS will
   guide the Navy’s next steps.

 Detailed description of cost and schedule performance against the baseline.

   The Navy will establish an execution baseline using historical data from ex-CVN 65 Dismantlement and Disposal (D&D) and will monitor
   performance once the D&D process begins.

I. Description of Operations Financed:
The EXMEDS program provides for the management, transformation, modernization, and reconstitution of platforms that will deliver more agile capabilities across the spectrum
of Navy, Marine Corps, and Joint operations. Navy's EHSS funding provides equipment and supplies for EMFs, T-AHs, EMUs, ERSSs, ERCS's and FDPMUs, as well as program
staff at BUMED and Navy Expeditionary Medical Support Command (NEMSCOM).

Expeditionary Health Services Systems (EHSS) provide comprehensive deployable medical systems (DEPMEDS) to U.S. and allied forces in the event of combat or contingency
operations. DEPMEDS includes large, land-based Role 3 Expeditionary Medical Facilities (EMF), Role 3 Hospital Ships (T-AH), smaller land-based Role 2 Enhanced
Expeditionary Medical Units (EMU), very small land or sea-based Role 2 Light Maneuver (R2LM) Expeditionary Resuscitative Surgery Systems (ERSS), and land-based Forward
Deployable Preventive Medicine Units (FDPMU). EMFs and EMUs are pre-positioned ashore and afloat throughout the world and are designed to complement and expand the
organic medical capabilities of the Fleet and play a critical role in the Marine Corps' evolving war fighting strategies of forward deployed theater operations.

The T-AH Hospital Ships, USNS MERCY (T-AH 19) and USNS COMFORT (T-AH 20), afloat have the capability to conduct casualty medical treatment in 12 operation rooms
and 1,000 beds. A Medical Treatment Facility (MTF) is integrated into the hull of each T-AH. The MTFs provide scalable hospitalization for use by Department of Defense in
support of the full spectrum of medical operations from foreign humanitarian assistance to major combat operations worldwide. The T-AHs are maintained in a five-day Reduced
Operation Status (ROS-5). They have a cadre assigned, are out ported at layberth, and undergo annual sea trials, periodic dock trials, and have periodic regulatory dry dockings
and inspections.
EMFs and EMUs are composed of distinct capability based modules/packages which can be tailored to meet whatever mission is required. These medical and surgical facilities
provide the ability to stabilize, treat, and rehabilitate wounded troops. Funding supports the transformational modernization/upgrading of these units through the Service Life
Extension Program (SLEP) and periodic replacement of perishable or shelf-life limited medical supplies. The CDML is a diagnostic mobile lab capability that is able to support
pandemic and outbreak response testing. The ERCS is the core capability to patient movement ensuring degradation of patient movement does not occur during transport to the
next level of care. FDPMUs provide deployable Force Health Protection to the Combatant Commanders by rapidly assessing, preventing, and controlling health threats in any
theater of operation. ERSSs are the smallest standard force package of the EHSS family and may be employed afloat, on any vessel, or ashore to provide immediate life and/or
limb saving damage control resuscitation and surgery. It is designed to support disaggregated maritime operations and operate in austere environments.
FDPMUs provide deployable Force Health Protection to the Combatant Commanders by rapidly assessing, preventing, and controlling health threats in any theater of operation.

ERSSs are the smallest standard force package of the EHSS family and may be employed afloat, on any vessel, or ashore to provide immediate life and/or limb saving damage
control resuscitation and surgery. It is designed to support disaggregated maritime operations and operate in austere environments.

Beginning in FY26, Ship Charter Operations of the MERCY and COMFORT will transition to the 1B1B Line Item while Medical Facilities and support will transition to 1C1M,
Medical Readiness Line Item.


II. Force Structure Summary:

Hospital Ships

1.   USNS MERCY (T-AH 19)
        The Fleet Hospital Program consists of one Afloat Fleet Hospital Ship, USNS MERCY (T-AH 19), Medical Treatment Facility (MTF), and Naval Medical Logistics
        Command (NMLC). The USNS MERCY (T-AH 19) is maintained to support real world humanitarian missions or Combatant Commander requirements.
                                                                                                                                                  Exhibit OP-5, 2C1H
        469                                                                                                                                              (Page 1 of 9)
                                                                         Department of the Navy
                                                                 FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                    Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                      Budget Activity: Mobilization
                                                               Activity Group: Mobilization Preparedness
                                                   Detail by Subactivity Group: Expeditionary Health Services Systems

2.   USNS COMFORT (T-AH 20)
        The Fleet Hospital Program consists of Medical Treatment Facility and medical equipment support for USNS COMFORT (T-AH 20), and Expeditionary Medical
        Facilities in Camp Lemonier and Djibouti. The USNS COMFORT (T-AH 20) is maintained in a five-day Reduced Operating Status (ROS-5) unless called upon to
        support real world humanitarian missions or Combatant Commander Theater Security Cooperation (TSC) requirements.


Expeditionary Medical Facility (EMF)
1. (4) 150-bed units

Expeditionary Medical Unit (EMU)
2. (2) 10-bed units

Forward Deployed Preventive Medicine Unites (FDPMU)
3. (5) units

Expeditionary Resuscitative Surgery Systems (ERSS)
4. (24) units

En-Route Care Systems (ERCS)
5. (36) units

                                                                        Budget Activity: Mobilization
                                                                 Activity Group: Mobilization Preparedness
                                                              Detail by Subactivity Group: Coast Guard Support
I. Description of Operations Financed:

The Coast Guard Support program funds the operation and maintenance of Navy-Type Navy-Owned (NTNO) weapons, radars, decoys, optical systems, Electronic Warfare
Support Measures (ESM) systems, and avionics installed on Coast Guard ships and aircraft. Funding complies with an agreement between the Department of Homeland Security
and the Department of the Navy, which ensures necessary interoperability between Coast Guard and Navy forces both in peacetime and in the event of Coast Guard wartime
service.

II. Force Structure Summary:

This program funds material and maintenance support for a variety of communications and electronics equipment installed on Coast Guard ships and aircraft. The funding also
sustains operations and maintenance support infrastructure.

                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                     Budget Activity: Training and Recruiting
                                                                         Activity Group: Accession Training
                                                                  Detail by Subactivity Group: Officer Acquisition
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Naval Service Training Command (NSTC) manages the Officer Candidate School (OCS), Officer Development School (ODS), The Naval Science Institute (NSI), and Limited
Duty Officer/Chief Warrant Officer (LDO/CWO) through its Officer Training Command and Strategic Sealift Midshipman Program (SSMP) and The Seaman to Admiral 21
Program (STA-21) through its Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps (NROTC) commands. United States Naval Academy (USNA) manages the academic programs for
midshipmen development, and the Naval Academy Preparatory School (NAPS). Washington Support Staff (WSS) manages the Office of Talent Optimization (OTO).

Officer Acquisition Programs: These programs provide orientation and indoctrination for officer candidates, preparatory training for selection for an officer accession program,
and academic study at higher education institutions for baccalaureate degrees. Those programs include:

The Officer Candidate School (OCS): Provides naval orientation and officer indoctrination to college graduates for commissioning as Reserve Officers for duty with the active
forces. It is a short-range officer acquisition program responsive to current requirements. This program is located at Officer Training Command, Newport, RI.

Strategic Sealift Midshipman Program (SSMP): Under the Maritime Education and Training Act of 1980, Departments of Naval Science have been established at the United States
Merchant Marine Academy and several State Maritime Academies to provide eligible students a course of instruction in Naval Science leading to commissions in the Naval
Reserve. OPNAV N13's Officer Strength & Inventory Letter governs Navy officer production, including SSMP, varying year-by-year, based on projected student graduation.

The Seaman to Admiral 21 Program (STA-21): Designed to improve support for Sailors who wish to become officers. STA-21 Sailors remain on active duty, draw full pay and
benefits, and receive a $10,000 education voucher per year for tuition, books, and fees. Upon selection into the program, the Sailor will attend the Naval Science Institute (NSI)
and then report to a NROTC affiliated University. Upon degree completion, they will be commissioned as Ensigns.

The Naval Science Institute: Portion of the STA-21 Program designed to teach each officer candidate the fundamental core concepts of being a naval officer. This program
provides competency in such areas as navigation, engineering, weapons, military history, and justice.

Officer Indoctrination Programs: The Officer Indoctrination program transitions newly commissioned Limited Duty Officer (LDOs) and Chief Warrant Officers (CWOs) into
new roles and responsibilities that supports the war-fighting capability and readiness of Naval Forces through leadership, technical proficiency, and experience.

Officer Development School (ODS): Designed to prepare newly commissioned officers of various staff corps (Judge Advocate Generals Corp, Medical Corp, Dental Corp, Nurse
Corp) and selected nuclear power specialists to carry out the duties and responsibilities of a naval officer.

Limited Duty Officer/Chief Warrant Officer (LDO/CWO): Designed to assist newly commissioned LDOs and CWOs with the transition into the naval officer community by
developing leadership, military communication, and administrative skills required in becoming a naval officer.

Office of Talent Optimization: Sailor 2025, a transformational Navy training initiative designed to accelerate the learning of every Sailor for faster response to our rapidly
changing warfighting requirements and achieves higher performance by coupling the timing of training delivery with every Sailor's actual deck-plate need. The Office of Talent
Optimization is located within the Washington Support Office and reports to the Chief of Naval Personnel. This office provides surveys and analytic tools which provides critical
advice on labor market conditions and new proposals informed by the private sector and academia to keep personnel systems competitive. Talent Optimization better reveals
talents in our future leaders at Annapolis and aligns those developing talents to long-term warfighting requirements. This office helps inform improvements to our Service
assignment policies for Midshipmen entering the Naval Service and, where applicable, informs the assignment process for Sailors and Marines entering the Service from other
commissioning sources.
                                                                                                                                                                 Exhibit OP-5, 3A1J
          485                                                                                                                                                          (Page 1 of 11)
                                                                              Department of the Navy
                                                                     FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                        Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                                    Budget Activity: Training and Recruiting
                                                                        Activity Group: Accession Training
                                                                 Detail by Subactivity Group: Officer Acquisition

Conning Officer Virtual Environment (COVE): Conning Officer Virtual Environment (COVE) training devices are employed at Officer Training Command, NAVSTA Newport,
to train officer candidates in an immersive virtual reality maritime environment, providing a full continuum of navigation, seamanship, ship-handling, piloting, and tactical Anti-
Terrorism/Force Protection (AT/FP) procedures.
Officer Accession Programs: Officer Accession Programs include United States Naval Academy (USNA) and USNA Preparatory School (NAPS). Funding for the Naval
Academy pays for the academic program, including faculty and staff, berthing and messing of midshipmen, professional training, including the summer cruise, and physical
education and instructional resources. NAPS consist of a ten-month course of instruction for enlisted personnel and selected candidates who are judged to need additional academic
preparation so that they will be able to perform successfully as midshipmen at the U.S. Naval Academy. Naval Service Training Command (NSTC) Accession program is also
used to transitions newly commissioned officers of the Navy Medical Corps, Judge Advocate General Corps, Chaplain Corps, Nuclear Power Specialists and Cyber Warfare
Engineers.

U.S. Naval Academy (USNA): This supports the academic programs faculty and staff, berthing and messing of midshipmen, professional training, including the summer cruise and
physical education, instructional resources, and facilities.

Naval Academy Preparatory School (NAPS): NAPS consists of a 10-month course of instruction for enlisted personnel and selected candidates in need of additional academic
preparation so they will be able to perform successfully as midshipmen at the U.S. Naval Academy.


II. Force Structure Summary:
This sub-activity group supports the missions of the USNA, WSS, NAPS, OCS, ODS, LDO/CWO School, SSMP, STA-21, COVE, Department of Naval Sciences at the Merchant
Marine Academy and several state maritime academies, and Office of Talent Optimization, and associated USNA Preparatory School (NAPS), Officer Indoctrination School, and
Direct Commission Officer School.

I. Description of Operations Financed:
Recruit Training indoctrinates recruits in basic military principles and naval skills, and it provides a realistic understanding of fleet environment and shipboard life. The Recruit
Training Program accomplishes its purpose through integration of men and women into a structured environment that stresses order, discipline, teamwork, responsive behavior,
loyalty, and pride in service. Resources support costs associated with recruit in-processing; training devices; training device support and maintenance; indoor fitness/drill facilities;
classroom facilities, equipment and related supplies; administrative staff salaries, supplies, travel; and other training items. The Recruit Training syllabus is structured to provide
the required training to meet the program's objective in a minimum amount of time, which is 73 curriculum days.

In addition to new recruits entering active duty, Basic Military Training (BMT) is provided to Selected Reservists in the Navy Reserve Enlisted under the Full-Time Support (FTS)
and Navy Accession Training (NAT) programs. Orientation and training is also provided to Navy Veterans and Other Service Veterans (NAVET/OSVET) re-entering the Naval
Service.

The Future Sailor Preparatory Course (FSPC) is an innovative program to help young men and women who want to serve in the Navy but cannot pass physical or academic
requirements. This program consists of two tracks – fitness and academic. The fitness track is for potential recruits who do not meet the Navy's enlistment body fat composition
standards. The academic track is for potential recruits who cannot attain a passing score on the Armed Services Vocational Aptitude Battery test. The program provides three
weeks of individualized paid training to potential recruits to improve their shortcomings so they can join the Navy. Upon completion, successful recruits are able to attend regular
boot camp training.

Battle Stations 21 is the capstone-training event at Recruit Training Command (RTC) Great Lakes. This training event is used to test recruits in a simulated combat environment to
help build Sailor confidence under stress. Battle Stations 21 is housed in a single facility and brings multiple stand-alone scenarios into a single storyline composed of realistically
sequenced events experienced onboard a simulated ship. It serves as the "rite of passage" from recruit to Sailor.


II. Force Structure Summary:
This sub-activity group supports the Recruit Training Command located at Great Lakes, IL.

I. Description of Operations Financed:

The Naval Reserve Officers Training Corps (NROTC) program produces unrestricted line Navy and Marine Corps officers. It provides college instruction to highly qualified
baccalaureate degree students who, upon graduation, receive a commission in the Navy, Marine Corps, or associated reserve program. NROTC is comprised of Scholarship and
College programs. The Scholarship Program is a long-range career officer accession program for the Regular Navy and Marine Corps. The College Program provides Naval
Reserve and Marine Corps Reserve Officers for active duty. Selectees enter either a minimum of a two-year program or a maximum of a four-year program as a College Program
or Scholarship Program student and must attain a baccalaureate degree prior to commissioning. The funds requested represent the cost of educational subsidies for Scholarship
Program students and administrative expenses of the program.

Educational subsidies consist of payments for tuition, fees and books for college courses required for a baccalaureate degree. Administrative expenses include unit operating costs,
purchase of Naval Science course textbooks, course reference materials, training aids, the costs associated with operating new student indoctrination and the costs associated with
operating several summer training sites.


II. Force Structure Summary:

NROTC includes 78 units along with 82 cross-town affiliations across the country with approximately 800 Navy and 278 USMC officers commissioned each year.
