                                                          Detail by Subactivity Group: Civilian Education and Training


II. Force Structure Summary:
The DON Financial Management Career Program Office (FMCPO) incorporates development of new employees by providing appropriate training and work experience
including rotational work assignments at a variety of Department of the Navy activities and echelons. Formal training and travel for training are essential to the
development of a broad experience base and to ensure personnel are well trained and prepared to assume positions in the Department of the Navy civilian workforce.

The Naval Acquisition Development Program (NADP). The Civilian Education Programs funded by this sub-activity group include the Acquisition Workforce (AWF).
Mandated by the Defense Acquisition Workforce Improvement Act (DAWIA) of 1990 (USC 10, Chapter 87), the AWF is designed to improve professionalism and
enhance business and technical skills in the Acquisition, Technology and Logistics (AT&L) Workforce. Components of the AWF include the Naval Acquisition
Development Program (NADP). The NADP provides hiring and professional development of Department of the Navy (DON) acquisition entry-level employees through a
highly structured program of education requirements imposed by DAWIA.

Acquisition Workforce (AWF) funds the labor, travel, continuous learning activities, and career development costs of approximately 500 entry-level employees who
participate in the Assistant Secretary of the Navy Research, Development and Acquisition (ASN, RD&A) centrally managed NADP. NADP graduates provide the AT&L
community with high performing individuals, fully capable of backfilling vacant, critical workforce positions that require highly skilled acquisition managers and senior
analysts.

                                                             Activity Group: Recruiting & Other Training And Education
                                                                      Detail by Subactivity Group: Junior ROTC
I. Description of Operations Financed:

The Navy Junior Reserve Officers' Training Corps (NJROTC) program is a congressionally sponsored youth citizenship program mandated by Public Law 88-647. NJROTC is
intended to instill the value of citizenship, service to the United States (including an introduction to service opportunities in military, national, and public service), personal
responsibility, and a sense of accomplishment in 8th grade through high school students. Eighty-Two (82%) percent of the NJROTC operation and maintenance budget currently
supports the government's share of instructor salaries. Other expenses include cadet orientation (travel), training aids, curriculum texts and educational materials, drill rifles, and
unit operating expense funding. Administrative support costs include office operating costs, travel, and per diem for eleven (11) NJROTC area managers.

The Navy also maintains a Navy National Defense Cadet Corps (NNDCC) program in partnership with qualifying secondary educational institutions. The NNDCC will provide
secondary educational institutions that do not qualify for, who do not wish to host, or currently cannot be offered an NJROTC unit, an opportunity to provide a course for students
to learn the values of citizenship, service to the United States, personal responsibility and attain a sense of accomplishment. The NNDCC program is modeled on the NJROTC
program, except that it is primarily funded by host schools.


II. Force Structure Summary:

NJROTC is a highly visible program in the local community receiving high-level political interest. The program enhances the image of the military in the eyes of the community
by providing opportunities for our nation's youth. Currently the average enrollment for NJROTC units is 121 cadets per school. Approximately Sixty-Four (64%) percent of the
cadets are minorities. The program currently supports 583 NJROTC units and 75 NNDCC units. The Naval Service Training Command (NSTC) anticipates increased 8th grade
participation in future years.

                                                            Budget Activity: Administration and Servicewide Activities
                                                                      Activity Group: Servicewide Support
                                                                   Detail by Subactivity Group: Administration
I. Description of Operations Financed:
Funds requested in the Administration line item support the direct staff offices of the Secretary of the Navy and the Chief of Naval operations, as well as various functional
activities reporting to them. It also resources the headquarters staffs of Navy operational and systems commands. Finally, this budget funds centrally-managed programs on behalf
of the DON and Navy.

The Secretary of the Navy is responsible for, and has the authority under Title 10 of the United States Code, to conduct all the affairs of the Department of the Navy, including:
recruiting, organizing, supplying, equipping, training, mobilizing, and demobilizing. The Secretary also oversees the construction, outfitting, and repair of naval ships, equipment
and facilities. SECNAV is responsible for the formulation and implementation of policies and programs that are consistent with the national security policies and objectives
established by the President and the Secretary of Defense. The Department of the Navy consists of two uniformed Services: the United States Navy and the United States Marine
Corps. The SECNAV staff offices serves as the principal policy advisors and assistants in the administration of the affairs of the Department of the Navy (DON). This includes the
immediate offices of the Secretary and Undersecretary of the Navy, the Assistant Secretaries of the Navy for Energy, Installations, & the Environment (EI&E) and Financial
Management & Comptroller (FM&C), the Office of General Counsel (OGC), the Office of Legislative Affairs (OLA), the Chief of Information (CHINFO), the Naval Inspector
General (IG), the DON Chief Information Officer (DON CIO), the Naval Audit Service (NAS), the Performance Improvement Office (PIO), previously known as the Office of the
Chief Management Officer (OCMO ), the Office of Small Business Programs (OSBP), the DON Assistant for Administration (DON/AA), the Sexual Assault Response and
Prevention Office (SAPRO), and the Naval Education Division (NED) and the Naval Community College (NCC).

Assistant Secretary of the Navy for Energy, Installations and Environment (ASN EI&E): The Assistant Secretary of the Navy for Energy, Installations and Environment serves to
enhance combat capabilities for the warfighter through greater energy security; the acquisition and disposal of real property; construction and maintenance of installations;
protecting the safety and occupational health of the military and civilian personnel; environmental protection, planning and restoration ashore and afloat; and conservation of
natural and cultural resources.

Assistant Secretary of the Navy (Financial Management & Comptroller) (ASN (FM&C)): The ASN (FM&C) leads the Department of the Navy's (DON) efforts to produce
auditable financial statements, execute effective and transparent budgets, operate agile financial systems, and develop and comply with financial laws, regulations and policies,
enabling leadership decision-making and ensuring stewardship of taxpayer dollars. The DON's Financial Management Strategy and mission is executed by the Offices of the
Assistant Secretary of the Navy (FM&C), which include: Financial Operations (FMO), Financial Systems (FMS), and Budget (FMB).

Secretary of the Navy, Under Secretary of the Navy (SECNAV, UNSECNAV): The Secretary of the Navy and Under Secretary of the Navy Office supports the SECNAV mission
to conduct all the affairs of the Department of the Navy (DON) including recruiting, organizing, supplying, equipping, training, mobilizing and demobilizing. DON policies and
programs are formulated and implemented which are consistent with the national security policies and objectives. Another component of the SECNAV Office includes the
Department of the Navy Principal Cyber Advisor (DON PCA) who serves as the principal advisor on all cyber matters affecting the DON. Cybersecurity management and
operations involve the recruitment, resourcing and training of military cyberspace operations forces, assessment of these forces against standardized readiness metrics, and
maintenance of these forces at standardized readiness levels.

Office of General Counsel (OGC): The General Counsel of the Navy provides legal advice, counsel, and guidance to the Secretary, other Civilian Executive Assistants and
their staffs. Areas of legal advice include: Acquisition Integrity, Suspend and Debarment, Personnel and Labor, Business and Commercial, Real and Personal Property,
Intelligence and Intellectual Property Law, Environmental, Freedom of Information, Ethic/Standards of Conduct, Alternative Dispute Resolution, and Freedom of
Information and Privacy Act issues.


                                                                                                                                                              Exhibit OP-5, 4A1M
         588                                                                                                                                                        (Page 1 of 28)
                                                                               Department of the Navy
                                                                      FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                            Budget Activity: Administration and Servicewide Activities
                                                                        Activity Group: Servicewide Support
                                                                    Detail by Subactivity Group: Administration
Office of Legislative Affairs (OLA): The Office of Legislative Affairs plans, develops, and coordinates relationships between senior representatives of the DON and members of
the U.S Congress, their personal staffs, and professional committee staffs in order to facilitate engagement on legislative matters which are of concern to and affect the Department
of the Navy's mission. The Chief of Legislative affairs (CLA) has sole responsibility for legislative affairs within the secretariat. OLA also funds travel for informational visits to
naval commands and other areas of interest of Congressional members, their staffs, and accompanying military escorts.

Office of the Chief of Information (CHINFO): CHINFO speaks on the record on behalf of the U.S. Navy on its operations and activities and provides media training to Navy
senior leaders and active forces. The office conducts public opinion research, directs internal coverage of newsworthy activities, and develops public affairs plans to ensure
accurate and timely information is provided to the media and the public. CHINFO is responsible for collecting and maintaining Navy imagery and sharing it with and engaging the
public and the media through navy.mil and official U.S. Navy social media accounts. CHINFO is also responsible for Navy public affairs and visual information education,
training, and professional development. As the Public Affairs proponent, CHINFO serves as Community Flag Sponsor for Public Affairs Officers and Enlisted Mass
Communication Specialists and as Functional Community Manager for Navy public affairs civilians.

Office of the Naval Inspector General (NAVINSGEN): NAVINSGEN is assigned sole responsibility for the Inspector General (IG) function and prescribes the relationship to the
Chief of Naval Operations (CNO) and the Commandant of the Marine Corps (CMC) while ensuring necessary support to each. When directed, NAVINSGEN is to inquire into any
matter that affects the discipline or military efficiency of the DON. Additionally, NAVINSGEN periodically propose programs of inspections and additional investigations to
SECNAV. NAVINSGEN has broad responsibilities for inspections, investigations, and area visits along with other related assignments. NAVINSGEN is the DON Hotline
Program and Whistleblower Protection Act Manager, maintains oversight of DON intelligence and special activities for regulatory compliance, and acts as the primary liaison for
all DON activities with DoDIG.

Deputy Naval Inspector General for Marine Corps (DNIGMC): The DNIGMC facilitates Marine Corps efficiency, integrity and institutional readiness through objective and
independent assistance, assessment, inspections and investigations to enhance the Marine Corps' mission success and the welfare of its Marines, Sailors and their families.
DNIGMC independently and objectively inspects, investigates and inquiries into matters of importance to the DON and Marine Corps. This mission supports the warfighter by
providing the oversight that helps the DON and Marine Corps identify and manage risk.

Department of the Navy Principal Cyber Advisor (DON PCA): Department of the Navy Principal Cyber Advisor (DON PCA) advises the SECNAV, CNO, and CMC on all cyber
matters with emphasis on cyber security, cyber resiliency, and cyber warfighting. DON PCA ensures implementation of the DoD and DON Cyber Strategy, and oversees
execution of service's policies and programs related to military cyber operations forces; cyber capability acquisition for offensive and defensive military cyber operations;
cybersecurity management and operations; cyber tools and capabilities acquisition; evaluating, improving, and enforcing a culture of cybersecurity warfighting and accountability;
industrial base cybersecurity and supply chain risk; and cybersecurity of information technology services, and weapons systems, incorporating threat information into
development, testing, and mitigations.

Department of Navy Chief Information Officer (DON CIO): The Special Assistant for Information Management/DON CIO is the DON's senior official for, and senior advisor to
the SECNAV on, matters involving the DON information environment, enterprise architecture, IT governance, cybersecurity and cyber hygiene, the Risk Management Framework,
information systems, data strategy, electromagnetic spectrum, privacy, records management, information disclosure and the FOIA, and civil liberties. The DON CIO oversees the
Information Management (IM) function within the Office of the SECNAV, OPNAV, and HQMC. The DON CIO works closely with other Secretariat staff offices in support of
UNSECNAV-directed business operations. The DON CIO works closely with senior DON leadership to ensure that DON efforts to organize, train, equip, and deliver information
capabilities aligned with the needs of naval forces operating in the cyber domain.

         589                                                                                                                                                         (Page 2 of 28)
                                                                                Department of the Navy
                                                                      FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                             Budget Activity: Administration and Servicewide Activities
                                                                        Activity Group: Servicewide Support
                                                                    Detail by Subactivity Group: Administration
Deputy Under Secretary of the Navy (DUSN) Management (M): The DUSN (M) serves as the principal advisor to the UNSECNAV, in support of the SECNAV, on all matters
related to management and business operations spanning the DON's functions and organizations. The DUSN (M) provides the SECNAV and UNSECNAV with findings and
policy recommendations that will contribute to greater agility, accountability, efficiencies, and effectiveness for the broad operations of the DON to include, but not limited to,
DON administrative authorities, performance improvement measures and Secretariat IT support and delivery.

Secretariat Information Technology (ITD): The DON CIO also manages information technology support for the Secretariat offices, including operation of the Information
Technology Department and funding for NMCI services, including user hardware, software, network infrastructure, VIP support, and printing capabilities.

Naval Audit Service (NAVAUDSVC): As defined in SECNAVINST 7510.7G CH-1, DON Internal Audit, NAVAUDSVC is the DON's internal audit organization.
NAVAUDSVC is led by a civilian Auditor General of the Navy, who serves as the principal advisor to the SECNAV, UNSECNAV, CNO, and CMC on all audit-related
matters. The Auditor General has sole responsibility for auditing within the DON. NAVAUDSVC conducts independent and objective internal performance audits of Navy
and Marine Corps activities, systems, and functions to assist Navy leadership in assessing risk to improve efficiency, accountability, and program effectiveness. These
internal audits are designed to add value and improve the DON's operations, and help the DON accomplish its objectives by bringing a systematic, disciplined approach to
evaluate and improve the effectiveness of risk management, control, and governance processes. Internal audits also help management identify risks and arrive at solutions
to problems; devise better ways of doing business; and deter fraud, waste, and abuse.

Performance Improvement Office (PIO): The Performance Improvement Office, formerly the Office of the Chief Management Officer (OCMO), provides expertise and innovation
to catalyze change and drive enhanced performance of business operations in support of the warfighter. PIO works collaboratively with the Assistant Secretaries of the Navy, and
all senior leaders across the Navy Secretariat and the Navy and Marine Corps staffs to bring agility to DON business operations to increase warfighter lethality and readiness
across all mission areas.

Office of Small Business Programs (OSBP): The DON Office of Small Business Programs (OSBP) fosters acquisition opportunities where small businesses can best support
warfighters and their families through policy, advocacy, counseling and training. OSBP promotes a DON-wide culture that leverages small businesses as a strategic advantage.

Department of Navy Office of Strategic Assessment (OSA): Among the SECNAV/UNSECNAV staff is Office of Strategic Assessment (OSA). The DON OSA is responsible to
provide timely, independent, and data-informed assessments and recommendations to assist the SECNAV in ensuring that Navy and Marine Corps plans, programs, processes, and
policies are integrated and aligned to SECNAV priorities and overarching DoD guidance, as well as inform future priorities and guidance. OSA is planned to be phased out by FY
2026.

Science and Technology Board (S&T Board): Among the Assistant Secretary of the Navy (Research, Development, and Acquisition) (ASN(RD&A)) staff is the Department of the
Navy Science and Technology Board (DON S&T Board). The DON S&T Board is a federal advisory board operating under the auspices of the Federal Advisory Committee Act
(FACA) to provide independent advice and recommendations on matters concerning science, technology, manufacturing, acquisition process, and other topics of special interest to
the DON. The DON S&T Board is composed of uncompensated senior level experts from industry, academia, and government appointed by the Secretary of Defense and are
tasked by the ASN(RD&A) who serves as the Board sponsor. Secretariat personnel support daily operational activities of the DON S&T Board to include selection, onboarding
and renewal of Board member appointments, identification of expert consultants, and study support. Secretariat personnel further assure compliance with FACA and ethics
statutes and serve as Designated Federal Officials (DFO) for Board activities. The funds also provide for contracted professional, analytical, and administrative services that are
critical to assuring effective DON S&T Board operations. Contracted services support the DON S&T Board and DON S&T Board staff with technical, business, and administrative
planning; organizing, managing, coordinating, and tracking (e.g., cost, schedule, and deliverables); performance management, and data management, as well as refining initial and
final reports of the various study groups.
                                                                                                                                                               Exhibit OP-5, 4A1M

Department of the Navy Assistant for Administration (DONAA): The Department of the Navy Assistant for Administration provides responsive and customer focused
management of administrative communications, documentation and correspondences, publications and reproduction programs and operational services directly to Navy Secretariat
per the DoD Headquarters Activities instructions. Key services include six mission critical areas handling the strategic, tactical, and operational roles and responsibilities: facilities,
space management and parking services; programs and integration; directives and records management; special security/command officer services; contract management, and the
Executive Dining Facility. Additionally, DONAA provides Automatic Declassification, Mandatory Declassification and Systematic Review support for the entire DON, both Navy
and Marine Corps. The Directives and Records Management program is funded in the 4C1P budget line item.

Sexual Assault Prevention and Response (SAPR) /DON Office of Force Resiliency (OFR): OFR is committed to promoting warfighting readiness by preventing harmful behaviors
and fostering healthy climates where Sailors, Marines, and civilian employees thrive. OFR is tasked with oversight to prevent and respond effectively to suicide, sexual assault,
sexual harassment, and other harmful behaviors. On behalf of the Secretary, OFR conducts Department-level initiatives, develops policy, and maintains visibility and oversight of
Sexual Assault, Sexual Harassment, Suicide Prevention and Response and Integrated Prevention activities throughout the Navy and Marine Corps. OFR is committed to
identifying effective efforts to prevent harmful behaviors, provide victims coordinated support and resources, improve access to mental health resources and reduce stigma for
seeking help. Preventing and effectively addressing harmful behavior requires systematic changes to climate and culture change to foster connectedness, trust, and accountability at
every level.

Naval Education Division (NED): Naval Education Division (NED) supports the Assistant Secretary of the Navy (Manpower and Reserve Affairs) in overseeing DON naval
education strategy, policy, governance, processes, curricula, and resources; provides unified intent for the Naval Education Enterprise and Naval University System and identifies
opportunities to improve naval education for competitive advantage. The NED portfolio includes naval education oversight, learning modernization, credentialing, common
military training, fellowships, scholarships, and tours with industry. Major components of the program: SECNAV strategic initiatives and advisory boards internal and external to
the DON, such as the Education for Sea power Advisory Board (E4SAB), Naval Education Board (NEB), and Naval Education Steering Committee (NESC) and the Low-
Residence Graduate Education Program (LGEP) Pilot.

United States Naval Community College (USNCC): The United States Naval Community College (USNCC) was established by the Secretary of the Navy in 2019 to increase
naval educational opportunities for over 450,000 active duty Sailors and Marines. USNCC is establishing academic programs in technical and non-technical fields, which are
relevant to the current and future needs of the Navy and Marine Corps, including in designated fields of national and economic importance such as cybersecurity, artificial
intelligence, machine learning, data science, and software engineering. Programs would also incorporate critical 21st Century skills through instruction in the fields of the liberal
arts and sciences. USNCC is addressing student success and learning outcomes by developing remedial, developmental, or continuing education programs and support and
advisement services for individuals pursuing such programs and continuous monitoring of the progress of USNCC students. USNCC is also pursuing academic accreditation for its
degree programs. Resources predominantly support the expansion of the number of enrolled students as well as an increase in course offerings as USNCC progresses towards full
operational capability. As an accredited education institute, USNCC will be responsible for all academic related support and execution infrastructure. USNCC is operating through
a consortium model with the top academic programs in the country, which specialize in professional concentration and general education programs that meet the needs of the naval
services. The USNCC-led consortium will expand to up to twenty partner institutions by FY28. The USNCC is responsible for designing world-class programs based on the
feedback provided by the naval operating forces. The NCC transferred to line item 3B3K Professional Development Education in FY26.

The Office of the Chief of Naval Operations staff (OPNAV) advises and assists the Chief of Naval Operations (CNO) in the discharge of his responsibilities as the principal
naval advisor and naval executive to the Secretary of the Navy on the conduct of the activities of the DON, and as the Navy member of the Joint Chiefs of Staff. The office
performs administrative and support activities and includes the following offices: the Office of the Judge Advocate General (OJAG) and the Navy Legal Service Command
(NSLC); the Naval History and Heritage Command (NHHC) and U.S.S. Constitution; the Naval Safety Center (NSC); and the U.S. Navy Band and Fleet Band.
                                                                                                                                                            Exhibit OP-5, 4A1M

Administrative, Personnel & Support manages the provision of funds and manpower, evaluate the utilization of such resources, and initiate or recommend appropriate corrective
action. These include programs such as support for the Armed Services Board of Contract Appeals (ASBCA) and personnel support to the White House Management Office and
funding for the Periodic Review Secretariat (PRS), which administers the periodic review process for eligible Law of War detainees held at U.S. Naval Station, Guantanamo Bay
Cuba (GTMO). In addition, the Secretariat Comptroller Office provides the support for civilian total force staffing. Starting in FY26, programs such as Pentagon Rent, Raven
Rock Complex, and Mass Transit has been realigned out of administrative personnel and support into DUSN M.

Naval Legal Service Command (NLSC) provides legal services and counsel to fleet and shore commands world-wide concerning command legal matters including, but not limited
to, military justice, conducting investigations, adjudicating claims, providing legal assistance, Sexual Assault Prevention and Response, Victims Legal Counsel, and other functions
or task as may be directed by the Chief of Naval Operations.

Office of the Judge Advocate General's (OJAG) primary mission is to support the Judge Advocate General (JAG) in providing legal and policy advice to both the Secretary of the
Navy and the Chief of Naval Operations in formulating and implementing policies and initiatives pertaining to the provision of legal services within the Navy. OJAG also supports
JAG in executing leadership and management of a 2,300-member worldwide JAG Corps community; carrying out responsibilities granted by statue to oversee a fair and robust
military justice system.

Naval History and Heritage Command (NHHC) manages the collection, preservation, exhibition and distribution of objects and information of Navy historical interest. The
Historical Ships Program provides resources to maintain and display the USS CONSTITUTION. Currently working towards the relocation of the National Museum of the US
Navy outside the constraints of the WNY, as well as the Overhaul and avail of the USS NAUTILUS, located at Submarine Force Museum, RI.

NHHC is a consolidation of the Naval Historical Center and a number of independent SECNAV-designated field museums at ten (10) locations around the country. The NHHC
mission is to:
      (1) Collect, preserve, protect and make available artifacts, documents and art that best embody our naval history and heritage for present and future generations;
      (2) Advance the knowledge of naval history and heritage through professional research, analysis, interpretation, products and services;
      (3) Make naval history and heritage "come alive" for our Sailors and Marines to enhance readiness and esprit de corps; and
      (4) Remind America and Americans of its need to maintain a strong Navy and Marine Corps to protect its citizens, their freedoms, and the nation's maritime commerce.

NHHC is organized into:
    (1) Headquarters activities at the Washington Navy Yard (previously, the Naval Historical Center (NHC)), which include:
            a. The Collections Management Division manages, collects, preserves, protects, and controls Navy historic properties, art, and artifacts, and sunken military craft,
            including developing and implementing management policy and procedures for such property that is not in NHHC custody. Operating units include curation, art
            collection and gallery; underwater archeology (enforcement of the Sunken Military Craft Act of 2005, including oversight and management of all sunken military
            ships and aircraft worldwide; issuance of dive permits, and civil citation and penalty determination).
            b. The History and Archives Division, which serves as chief historical advisor to CNO, OPNAV Staff, fleet and other operational Navy commanders. Operating
            units include archives, histories, the Navy library, and NHHC web site support.
            c. The Communication and Outreach Division represents the NHHC, providing support to DoN leaders, Navy service members, their families and the public. These
            services include publications, and organizing and coordinating Navy designated commemorations and public events.
            d. The Naval Museums Division manages, collects, preserves, protects and controls Navy historic properties, art and artifacts at 9 field museums in 10 geographic
            locations.

       (3) Maintenance Detachment Boston, the maintenance for the USS CONSTITUTION, the U.S. national Ship-of-State, homeported in Boston, Massachusetts, which is the
       oldest commissioned warship in the U.S. Navy.

The Naval Safety Command (NSC) promotes and monitors safety to reduce accidents afloat and ashore. NSC assesses, trains (environmental compliance and safety), and
investigates safety related items via travel, contracts, training, and data analytics. Manages and oversees the Naval Aviation Safety Management System and provides aviation
safety support to the Navy and Marine Corps. Provides technical guidance and assistance for explosives and weapons system safety. Provides Shore Safety support for the Navy
and Marine Corps for Safety and Occupational Health (SOH), Traffic Safety, and Recreational Off-Duty Safety (RODS). Responsible for establishing the policies, doctrine and
guidance as well as implements, coordinates, maintains, and promotes programs, policies, and procedures for Safety and Occupational Health, Traffic Safety, and RODS; Develops
mishap investigation and reporting procedures; Serves as a liaison with OSHA, DoD, and other entities for SOH compliance and reporting.

The U.S. Navy Band provides musical support for the President of the United States, Department of the Navy and other senior military and government officials. Through
ceremonies, national tours, public concerts, and recordings, the U.S. Navy Band inspires patriotism, elevates esprit de corps, enhances Navy awareness and public relations,
supports recruiting and retention efforts, preserves our nation's musical heritage and projects a positive Navy image at home and abroad.

Fleet Band provides resources required for musical support to the Department of the Navy, directly supporting USSOUTHCOM, COCOM, AFRICOM, JFC Naples and Norfolk,
USPACFLT, USFF, COMSEVENTHFLT, NRNW, NRSW, RTC, NRSE, Naval War College, CHINFO, Naval Station Great Lakes, other senior military and government
officials, and active duty communities around the world through support of six CONUS bands and three OCONUS bands. Through ceremonies, broadcasts and telecasts, Fleet
Weeks and Navy Weeks, special events and public concerts, Fleet Bands inspire patriotism, elevate esprit de corps, enhance Navy awareness and public relations, support
recruiting and retention, and project a positive Navy image at home and abroad. Funding supports mission travel in support of DoN directed outreach and ceremonies, musical
equipment, supplies, shipping (TAC), vehicles and support services to include hall rentals, audition space rentals, lighting sound and video support, commissions, guest artists,
advertisements. .


Centrally-managed programs on behalf of the DON and Navy include: Pentagon Rent, the Financial Improvement and Audit Readiness (FIAR) program, support from the
Defense Finance and Accounting Service (DFAS), the Mass Transit Benefit Program (MTBP), the Navy Travel Service Fee, and payments to the National Archives and Records
Administration (NARA):

Pentagon Rent/Raven Rock Mountain Complex (RRMC): The Pentagon Reservation Maintenance Revolving Fund (PRMRF) provides space, building services, and force
protection for the Department of Defense (DoD) components, military departments, and other activities within the Pentagon Reservation. Established by the 1991 National Defense
Authorization Act and codified in Title 10, U.S. Code, Section 2674, the PRMRF covers both operating and capital investment costs, with standard rates set annually. Operating
costs include inflationary increases for supplies, equipment service contracts, and personnel, while capital costs encompass security improvements and facility sustainment,
restoration, and modernization projects. Each year, through the Real Property Operations (RPO) center, Washington Headquarters Service (WHS) ensures the safe and efficient
operation, maintenance, and repair of the Pentagon Reservation. The PRMRF also finances the Pentagon Force Protection Agency (PFPA), which is responsible for law
enforcement, force protection, security, counterintelligence, antiterrorism, and chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear protection for the people, facilities, infrastructure, and
other resources at the Pentagon Reservation. The PFPA coordinates with DoD components, executive departments, agencies, and state and local authorities on matters of force
protection, security, and law enforcement for the Pentagon Reservation. The Raven Rock Mountain Complex (RRMC) in Pennsylvania is a highly secure underground military
facility that supports the U.S. government in times of crisis. It's also known as Site R. The RRMC supports the COOP, which is a backup plan for the Pentagon and other federal
agencies.
The RRMC has emergency operations centers for the military branches and other federal agencies. The RRMC conducts full-scale COOP exercises to test procedures for
relocating personnel and integrating functions. The RRMC is a key part of the U.S. national defense strategy, enabling government operations to continue after a nuclear attack or
natural disaster. Pentagon Rent and Raven Rock Complex has been realigned from administrative personnel and support to DUSN starting in FY26.

The Financial Improvement and Audit Remediation (FIAR) program provides the DON the resources to meet FY 2024 National Defense Authorization Act (Sec. 1005)
requirement which mandates all Department of Defense (DoD) components obtain an unmodified financial statement audit opinion by FY 2028. Audits are rigorous examinations
of Navy's business environment. Corrective actions lead to improved transparency of resource usage and improved asset accountability. To date, the Navy has made substantial
progress on its audit roadmap, which highlights interdependencies between business processes and needed system improvements and documents the path to achieving an
unmodified audit opinion by FY 2028.
Continued audit success requires documentation, testing and standardization of business processes related to the recording and reporting of financial transactions. Auditability,
once achieved, must be sustained into the future to include building the appropriate infrastructure in Navy-Marine Corps organizations to monitor and test financial and cyber
security internal controls regularly. The requested FIAR resources will enable the DON to establish the capability to simultaneously respond to current audits, remediate
deficiencies, and sustain an audit ready business and operations environment, thus keeping the DON on the trajectory to obtain an unmodified audit opinion in FY 2028 or sooner -
ultimately heightening warfighter readiness and lethality.

Defense Finance and Accounting Service (DFAS) provides the DON finance and accounting services, including delivering accurate, timely, and reliable financial reports and
information while monitoring, managing, and continuously providing appropriate quality control over data. Specific services provided to the DON include; Civilian Pay
Operations, Military Pay Operations, Contract Payments, Travel Payments, Commercial Payments, Debt Services, Garnishments, Support to Others, Accounting Services, and
Financial Management Systems operations and maintenance. DFAS also provides the DON professional audit readiness, response, and sustainment services that are in accordance
with Service Provider responsibilities outlined in the Office of the Under Secretary of Defense (Comptroller) (OUSD(C)) FIAR Guidance, Office of Management & Budget
(OMB) Circular A-123 and associated federal statutes that govern internal control compliance.

The Mass Transit Benefit Program (MTBP), administered under DODI 1000.27, is available to eligible civilian employees and military service members as permitted by law and
regulation. Its goals are to reduce pollution and traffic congestion, preserve the environment, and expand transportation options. Participants in the National Capital Region (NCR)
receive subsidies covering their personal commuting costs, up to the monthly limit set by Congress. Mass Transit has been realigned from administrative personnel and support to
DUSN M starting in FY26

The Navy Travel Service Fee is a centralized management fee paid to the contracted vendor for official commercial travel services provided to all Navy personnel.

The National Archives and Records Administration (NARA) fee is for the U.S. Navy and the USMC document storage and services bill. The NARA bill provides decorations for
veterans since the Cold War era; and pays for Navy and USMC veterans' records retrieval, review, and replacement.


Other programs financed in this sub-activity group include Medical Headquarters, Overseas Banking, Navy Medical Travel, International Cooperative Administrative Support
(ICASS), American Forces Radio and Television Service (AFRTS) (Site TV) Ship, Equipment Repair, Personnel and Other Support, Environmental Conservation and
Compliance, Alcohol Abuse Prevention, and Reports, Alcohol and Drug Management Information System (ADMITS), Exceptional Family Member Program, Foreign Military
Sales (FMS), Suicide Prevention, Operational Stress Control, and the Commander Fleet Forces Command Security Cooperation.


II. Force Structure Summary:
The force structure supported in this sub-activity group includes staff offices for the Secretary of the Navy, Chief of Naval Operations Support activities, various Centrally
Managed Navy-wide programs and other programs.

      i)Navy Medical Travel: Decrease in funding due to fluctuations in transportation cost and in travel requirements associated the        -7
         different type of medical appointments. (Baseline: $267)
      ii)CHINFO: Decrease in supplies and materials utilized in support of External Community Relations and Public Affairs events           -21
         that showcase America's Navy, specifically in cities where there is no Navy footprint, via Sailor, unit and command
         participation in community-related events. (Baseline: $17,118)
      iii)Foreign Currency Fluctuation: Decrease due to change in Foreign Currency Fluctuation Rates. (Baseline: $48)                       -54
      iv)SAPR: Decrease in support funding reflects a reduction in Navy Contractor Support Services (CSS) for the Victim's Legal            -59
         Counsel and Naval Justice School training courses. Additionally, operational funding for management and professional
         support services is reduced due to the attainment of full operational capability for defense counsel requirements. (Baseline:
         $24,288)
      v)U.S. Navy Band/Fleet Band: Decrease in funding for printing, maintenance, commercial transportation, telecom, IT                    -67
         implementation, postal service, equipment, advertising, and other professional management services. (Baseline: $6,448)
      vi)NSC: Decrease in funding due to the DON continuing to implement more cost-effective management of its training courses.            -99
         (Baseline: $22,798)
      vii)SAPR: Decrease in support funding for professional development, travel, utilities, IT infrastructure communications,             -135
         printing, equipment, management and professional support services. (Baseline: $24,288)
      viii)FMS: Resources decreased due to reduction in case closures and assistance in bill payment. (Baseline: $2,422)                   -161
      ix)Suicide Prevention: Resources decreased for the Management Support contract due to the implementation of the Suicide              -164
         Prevention Lethal Means Safety (LMS) gun-lock procurement and distribution system. This contract was established as part
         of the suicide prevention strategies for Navy Personnel that aims to reduce suicide, suicide attempts, and stress related
         injuries. (Baseline: $11,918)
      x)NHHC: Decrease in funding that support services for the National Museum of the U.S. Navy (NMUSN) relocation, which has             -180
         been delayed. Services include design, fabrications and installation of exhibits, artifact baseline reset, fieldwork and
         acquisition of new artifacts. (Baseline: $64,816)
      xi)OPNAV: Decrease in civilian personnel funding for the Civilian Harm Mitigation Response (CHMR) program. CHMR was                  -201
         mandated by DoDI 3000.17, is responsible for a range of critical activities, including the identification of a lead general

         Updates, and the Quickeries Software Updates. (Baseline: $2,941)

         FY26 funding includes recurring costs for user licenses and platform maintenance. (Baseline: $18,534)

      xvii)NLSC: Decrease in funding due to efficiencies in travel, document services, commercial transportation, communications,          -398
         supplies, printing, equipment maintenance, facility sustainment, management and professional support services, intra-
         government purchases, IT contract support other services. (Baseline: $66,824)

         systems on DDG platforms. Repairs maintain the capability of the ship to distribute Navy Motion Picture Service movies
         throughout the ship. (Baseline: $561)

         within all IT operational functions such as equipment, software and information security needs, IT and Legacy infrastructure,
         and telecommunications needs. (Baseline: $64,816)

         establishment of classified/unclassified email accounts, desk-side support), IT research and advisory support, in addition to
         reduced GSA and mobile communications costs resulting from contract negotiations. (Baseline: $28,040)
      xxiii)NARA: Decrease in support funding due to anticipated transition of records being stored electronically. (Baseline:             -987
         $15,882)

         legacy IT system into ERP. (Baseline: $82,898; -3 civilian FTE)
      xxv)OPNAV: Decrease in support funding for certain climate initiatives, eliminates support for non-tactical zero-emission           -1,000
         vehicles within the Navy, and discontinues targeted investments. (Baseline: $202,431)

      xxvi)    Administrative, Personnel, & Support: Funding decrease due to the realignment of the National Capitol Region Mass         -1,101
        Transit, Admiral Gooding Center and the British Uruguay Cemetery to DUSN (M). (Baseline: $38,277)
      xxvii) Fleet Band: Decrease funding for supplies, equipment and operational travel. (Baseline: $3,038)                             -1,358
      xxviii) SAPR: Reduces civilian full-time equivalents and funding to rephase the Independent Review Commission on Sexual            -1,417
        Assault in the Military workforce due to the year-long continuing resolution, civilian hiring freeze, and federal workforce
        optimization. (Baseline: $24,288; -7 civilian FTE)
      xxix)    SAPR: Decrease in support funding resulting from Independent Review Commission (IRC) Tier 3 Recommendation                -1,460
        3.3.b funding ending in FY25. IRC Tier 3 Recommendation 3.3.b funding is for educating leaders on cyber harassment and
        technology-facilitated-sexual harassment and assault. (Baseline: $24,288)
      xxx) Overseas Operations Costs: Decrease in funding to support the Civilian Expeditionary Workforce (CEW) requirements.            -1,615
        (Baseline: $4,194)
      xxxi)    NHHC: Program reflects decrease in facilities contractual requirements to support mission critical areas such as          -2,263
        exhibit de-installation, studies to remove servers, moves of exhibit displays, furniture and critical artifacts, IT and
        communication services, and Artifact Baseline Reset (ABR), which addresses the conservation efforts, scientific analysis, and
        customized treatment of cultural assets. (Baseline: $64,816)
      xxxii) DON/AA and PIO: Decrease in civilian personnel funding due to the realignment of funding from DON/AA and PIO                -3,129
        to DUSN(M). (Baseline: $30,138; -13 civilian FTE)
      xxxiii) Efficiency – Travel: Reduces discretionary travel funding to align with Executive Order 14222, Implementing the            -3,314
        President's Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative. (Baseline: $23,074)
      xxxiv) DFAS: Decrease in funding resulting from the DFAS bill anticipated savings due to transformational efforts that are         -3,908
        projected to decrease workload and costs. These efforts include decommissioning DFAS-owned legacy systems as the DON
        migrates commands to ERP and DAI, Disbursing Station Symbol Number (DSSN) consolidation efforts, enhanced use of E-
        Commerce through system changes, and implementation of more efficient processes through Robotic Process Automation.
        (Baseline: $279,840)
      xxxv) ASN(FM&C): Decrease in support funding due to prior investments in data analytics and bots that reduced manual               -4,245
        tasks, eliminated data discrepancies and enhanced audit remediation efforts. (Baseline: $82,898)
      xxxvi) ASN(FM&C): Decrease in funding due to the reduction of contract support for FM&C legacy financial management                -5,616
        systems infrastructure (ex., licenses, help desk support, and other sustainment costs) due to the decreasing user community
        in Command Financial Management System (CFMS) and Standard Accounting, Budgeting and Reporting System (SABRS)
        resulting from the migration of Navy commands to Navy ERP and DAI. (Baseline: $82,898)
      xxxvii) Pentagon Rent and Raven Rock Complex: Decrease in support funding due to the identification of the correct square         -14,959
        footage for billing. Reduction also reflects updated pricing from White House Services as new Presidential Orders that
        promote workforce efficiencies are implemented. (Baseline: $83,928)

      xxxviii) Decrease in Contractor Support Services (CSS) to curtail non-Advisory and Assistance Services across the Navy,           -15,637
        eliminating redundant or lower-value contracted functions. This reduction directly supports the Navy's imperative to build a
        more lethal, agile, and self-reliant force by prioritizing mission-essential capabilities. (Baseline: $1,408,462)

        and advance the policies of the Administration in alignment with Executive Order 14222, Implementing the President's
        Department of Government Efficiency Cost Efficiency Initiative. (Baseline: $1,408,462)

        the workforce in compliance with Executive Order 14210, Implementing the President's Department of Government

                                                                       Activity Group: Servicewide Support
                                                        Detail by Subactivity Group: Civilian Manpower and Personnel Mgt
I. Description of Operations Financed:

This sub-activity group supports the Assistant Secretary of the Navy (Manpower and Reserve Affairs) (ASN (M&RA)), Office of Civilian Human Resources (OCHR), the Board
for Correction of Naval Records (BCNR), the Secretary of the Navy Council of Review Boards (CORB), The Human Resource Delivery Services for SPECWARCOM, the
Unemployment Compensation Fund, and the Transportation Incentive Program for Outside the National Capital Region (OCNR).

The Assistant Secretary of the Navy (Manpower and Reserve Affairs) (ASN (M&RA)): ASN (M&RA) is responsible for the overall supervision and oversight of manpower
and reserve component affairs of DON. These responsibilities include the development of programs and policy related t o personnel readiness, quality of life, health care, and other
issues affecting DON military personnel, their family members, and the civilian workforce, plus tracking of the contractor wo rkforce and oversight of Human Resources (HR)
systems. ASN (M&RA) acts as liaison with the Under Secretary of Defense (Personnel and Readiness), the Assistant Secretaries of Defense for Reserve Affairs, Health Affairs,
and other Under and Assistant Secretaries of Defense, as appropriate and relevant, in matters of mutual c oncern. In addition, ASN (M&RA) assists in managing all DON civilian
career senior executive programs, including, but not limited to, Senior Executive Service (SES), scientific, highly qualified experts, intelligence and national security senior
executives, and the human resources support for all administratively determined, Schedule C, and Presidential Appointee in a Position Requiring Senate Confirmation (PAS)
offices. The ASN (M&RA) oversight and execution portfolio includes Deputy Assistant Secretary of the Navy Civilian Personnel (DASN CP), DASN Military Manpower &
Personnel (MM&P), Navy Secretariat Human Resources Office, Navy Secretariat Equal Employment Office (EEO), DON Office of Equa l Employment Opportunity (OEEO),
Executive Management Program Office (EMPO), and Manpower, Analytics, & HR Systems (MAHRS). In addition, M&RA oversees the Office of Civilian Human Resources
(OCHR), via DASN CP, while executing direct oversight of the Board for Correction of Naval Records and the Council of Review Boards (CORB), which are detailed below.
ASN (M&RA) also administers the Office of Force Resiliency (OFR), and Naval Education Division (NED); however, their operations are funded in the 4A1M line item.

The Office of Civilian Human Resources (OCHR): OCHR is responsible for DON HR operations, Legal Counsel, the Civilian Employee Assistance Program and the HR
Benefits Call Center. This includes the HR execution for the entire Department of the Navy civilian workforce of more than 195,000 employees in over 500 different occupations,
to include 300+ bargaining units.

The Board for Correction of Naval Records (BCNR): Under Title 10 U.S. Code § 1552, the Board for Correction of Naval Records (BCNR) is the Secretary of the Navy's
highest Board to correct errors or injustices in military records for Sailors, Marines, and Veterans. The BCNR's authority is directly delegated by Congress to the Secretary of the
Navy. Congress mandates strict case review timelines under Title 10 U.S. Code § 1557 to ensure all petition ers receive timely decisions. The Board receives over 11,000
applications annually, and must adjudicate 90 percent of its applications within 10 months and 100 percent of its application s within 18 months. Moreover, the Secretary of the
Navy must submit a report to Congress annually when these timelines are not met. There is no equivalent organization elsewhere in th e Department of the Navy.

The Secretary of the Navy Council of Review Boards (CORB): CORB is comprised of the Physical Evaluation Board (PEB), the Naval Discharge Review Board (NDRB), the
DON Combat Related Special Compensation Board (CRSC), the Naval Clemency and Parole Board (NC&PB), the Navy Department Board of Decorations and Medals (NDBDM,
the Personnel Security Appeals Board (PSAB), the Traumatic Service member's Life Insurance Appeals (TSGLI) Board, and the Naval Complaints Review Board (NCRB).

The Human Resource Delivery Services for SPECWARCOM aligns HR functions, accounts transparent common processes, policies, tools, systems and adapts to changing
mission requirements. Field Support Activity provides an annual reimbursable order to SPECWARCOM.

The Transportation Incentive Program (TIP) for Outside the National Capital Region (OCNR): supports voucher administration, program administrative fees, as well as
developing and maintaining the electronic vouchering system owned and operated through Dept. of Transportation.

                                                                                                                                                              Exhibit OP-5, 4A3M

                                              Detail by Subactivity Group: Civilian Manpower and Personnel Mgt
The Unemployment Compensation Fund provides resources to reimburse states for unemployment compensation paid to eligible former civilian employees of the Department of
Navy.

II. Force Structure Summary:

Funding is provided for the Assistant Secretary of the Navy Manpower and Reserve Affairs (ASN(M&RA), the Office of Civilian Human Resources (OCHR) Operation Centers
overseas hiring activities, the Board for Correction of Naval Records (BCNR), the Secretary of the Navy Council of Review Boards (CORB) comprised of six component boards
(PEB, NDRB, CRSC, NC&PB, NDBDM, and PSAB), SPECWARCOM Human Resource Delivery Service, Unemployment Compensation Fund, and Transportation Incentive
Program (TIP) for Outside the National Capital Region (OCNR).

                                                        Detail by Subactivity Group: Military Manpower and Personnel Mgt
I. Description of Operations Financed:

Funds requested in the Administration line item support the direct staff offices of the Secretary of the Navy and the Chief of Naval operations, as well as various functional
activities reporting to them. It also resources the headquarters staffs of Navy operational and systems commands. Finally, this budget funds centrally managed programs on behalf
of the DON and Navy.

The Bureau of Naval Personnel (BUPERS) supports the Deputy Chief of Naval Operations (Manpower, Personnel, Training and Education) (DCNO-MPTE) in providing Human
Resource support for the US Navy, to include officer and enlisted personnel manpower determinations, accessions, training, personnel assignments, career development, personnel
administration, and other functions. Operations include Enterprise Support; Force Development; and Force Management. The programs financed include:

Navy Manpower Analysis Center (NAVMAC): The NAVMAC program develops and documents manpower requirements for all Fleet activities within the Navy; provides
direct support to Chief of Naval Operations (CNO (N1)) in managing the Navy manpower requirements programs; provides manpower requirements determination support for the
Navy's acquisition programs and initiatives; administers the officer and enlisted occupational classification structure; provides manpower technical consulting services in all areas
to managers, claimants, and OPNAV sponsors; provides functional management support for assigned manpower Automated Information Systems (AIS); provides direct support to
CNO central authority to enforce policy or provide additional technical guidance needed to achieve objectives of total force manpower management; and performs other manpower
analyses as directed by higher authority

N17 Family Support: The N17 Family Support office is known as the Navy's 21st Century Sailor Office. Through a comprehensive, adaptive lens focused on the changing needs
of Sailors, families, and the Fleet, the office provides our Sailors and families with the support network, programs, resources, training, and skills needed to overcome adversity and
thrive. This office includes Prevention and Response Programs, Sailor and Family Programs, the Navy Alcohol and Drug Abuse Program, and the Physical Readiness Program.

N10 Salaries and Support: The staff of the Washington Support Services is responsible for the integrity and administration of financial resources and provides a full range of
financial support to program managers. They analyze information received for clarity, completeness and consistency with approved budget procedures and guidelines. They
administer financial management policies, procedures and funding controls to all programs; prepare budget submissions, exhibits and justifications; and analyze the budget
requirements to ensure proper financial support and allocation of appropriated funds.

Labor Support Cost: The Labor Support Cost program provides funding for all non-IT Civilian labor for the Bureau of Personnel (BUPERS), Navy Personnel Command (NPC).
These staff provide support to all non-IT BUPERS programs.

Data Center Cloud Migration: The Data Center Cloud Migration Program provides funding for migration and maintenance of Navy systems to a secure enterprise cloud
environment in conjunction with Transformation per CIO mandate to migrate to the cloud.

Chief Information Officer (CIO) Office, Deputy Chief of Naval Operations (Manpower, Personnel, Training, and Education) (DCNO-MPTE): The CIO Office serves as
the Information Management / Information Technology (IT/IM) Division for the DCNO-MPTE. As such, the CIO Office designs, selects and provides affordable next generation
technologies that deliver knowledge and information for the total force in a joint net-centric environment. The effective use of these technologies is promoted through sound
planning and investments, integrated architectures and standards, effective systems development and production support, and business process and IT solution support for business
processes. The division ensures IT resources are aligned with the strategic business objectives of Manpower, Personnel, Training and Education (MPTE) and the Department of the
Navy (DON) and facilitates the development and reporting of metrics for all IT products and services. The CIO Office also serves as the single DCNO-MPTE headquarters point
of contact for IT resources. Responsible to plan, implement and oversee Transformation of MyNavy HR IT systems.

                                                                                                                                                               Exhibit OP-5, 4A4M

                                                                         Operation and Maintenance, Navy

                                                                       Activity Group: Servicewide Support
                                                        Detail by Subactivity Group: Military Manpower and Personnel Mgt

Chief Information Officer (CIO) Support Office: NPC CIO Support and CIO Support functions across the Enterprise have been reorganized and consolidated. CIO Support
consolidates subordinate command level CIO support and oversight functions for MyNavy HR and Legacy IT under the DCNO MPTE CIO Office. The consolidated CIO Support
line funds CIO support services and labor across the enterprise for command level oversight, planning, and reporting up to Headquarters CIO office.

Electronic Military Personnel Record System (EMPRS) (Legacy IT System): EMPRS is the Navy's personnel records management system and the repository for Official
Military Personnel File (OMPF) images. It provides records storage and management for active-duty, reserve, and separated personnel, and provides decision support for the
Selection Board process, Mobilization, Casualty Assistance, Distribution, and Entitlements. It is a stand-alone hybrid system of electro-mechanical, photographic, manual, and
automated processes.

BUPERS Online (Legacy IT System): BUPERS Online is an IT application which hosts 26 different applications which provides Sailors and Commands with self-service access
to official personnel records, performance appraisals, fitness reports and official orders. The system allows both Commands and Sailors to electronically submit personnel actions
such as promotion packets, name changes, and assignment transfer requests.

Service Unique Desk Applications (Legacy IT System): The Service Unique Desk Applications funding provides hardware and software maintenance contracts for the core
system hosting critical personnel management systems, including the Electronic Military Personnel Record System (EMPRS), BUPERS Online (BOL), and other
systems/applications. This program also provides helpdesk support for the legacy application systems and network support for most of Millington IT functions

PERSTEMPO (Legacy System): PERSTEMPO is a program that tracks the deployment days of individual service members and supports eventual payment to members whose
deployment days exceed mandated thresholds.

Career Waypoints (CWAY) (Legacy IT System): CWAY is a critical Navy staffing IT tool used to screen, match, and assign Sailors to positions throughout the Navy during
initial enlistment, reenlistments, and throughout their military careers. CWAY is a rating quality and eligibility screening mechanism used for all enlisted Sailors entering the Navy
or desiring to reenlist or convert before their 14th year of service. It has become the Navy's primary force shaping tool used to control reenlistments and separate Sailors who do
not meet performance standards relative to their peers. It also provides conversion processing capabilities to level rating manning from overmanned to undermanned ratings for
both rated and non-rated Sailors.

Navy/Marine Corps Mobilization Processing System (NMCMPC) (Legacy IT System): NMCMPC provides end-to-end command visibility and control of integrated
augmentation processes and automated workflow, ranging from requesting, approving, sourcing and writing orders for requirements, to tracking, accounting, collecting data, and
coordinating during activation/deactivation. This includes the mobilization and demobilization of Reserve Component personnel and the temporary reassignment of Active
Component personnel for emergent manpower requirements. It allows administrative, operational, and ad-hoc Task Force based Chain-of-Command duty stations direct access via
the web to monitor manpower requests and personnel augmenting their commands.

BRIGS: Brigs Charleston, Miramar and their respective detachments (Chesapeake and Pearl Harbor) provide state-of-the-art correctional services to all branches of the military.
The Brigs also provide military escort services for adjudged prisoners and deserters. Funding supports facility infrastructure maintenance and repair, security system maintenance
contracts, and specialized locking and security sensitive equipment. Funding also supports inmate treatment programs for Alcohol and Drug abuse, violent and sexual offenders,
Post Traumatic Stress Disorder, medical, and mental health counseling. To prepare prisoners for successfully returning to a productive civilian life, the Brigs provide useful skill-
set and transitional training for inmates.

                                                                                                                                                              Exhibit OP-5, 4A4M
         628                                                                                                                                                        (Page 2 of 16)
                                                                               Department of the Navy
                                                                       FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                          Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                             Budget Activity: Administration and Servicewide Activities
                                                                        Activity Group: Servicewide Support
                                                         Detail by Subactivity Group: Military Manpower and Personnel Mgt
MyNavy Career Center and Pay and Personnel Administrative Support (MNCC/PASS) (Legacy IT System): MNCC provides operational and civilian personnel support for
all program activities that support military pay and personnel, passenger transportation, and identification card services for eligible personnel. This includes headquarters program
management activities, the operation of the Navy Pay and Personnel Support Center (NPPSC), Personnel Support Detachments (PSDs), and Customer Service Detachments
(CSDs).

Selection Boards: The Selection Boards funding supports Selection and Promotion Board travel, lodging, per diem expenses and local transportation costs for both statutory and
administrative promotion/selection boards. This also includes the cost of the shuttle from the airport to NSA Millington, the use of vehicles from the General Services
Administration/Naval Facilities fleet, and in-and-around travel by board members and recorders.

Next-of-Kin (Funeral) Program: The Next-of-Kin program provides non-discretionary travel funds for family members of deceased Navy personnel to attend military funerals,
memorial services, and dignified transfer of remains (when applicable) in accordance with 37 USC 481f, DoD Instruction 1300.18, Military Personnel Casualty Matters, Policies,
and Procedures and Joint Travel Regulation §7260.

Forgotten Widows: The Forgotten Widows program pays monthly annuities to surviving spouses of retired service members, required by Public Law 105-85 Section 644, Public
Law 106-65 Section 656, Public Law 107-314, Section 634.

Transitional Compensation for Abused Family Members (TCAFM): TCAFM is a Congressionally-authorized program, managed by N170C, which provides a minimum of
twelve months (1 year) to a maximum of thirty-six months (3 years) of monetary benefits (entitlement payments), medical and dental care, and commissary privileges to family
members (spouse and/or dependents) of service members separated from active duty due to a domestic abuse offense. The program is authorized by Title 10 USC 1059.

Dependent (Bedside) Travel Program: The Bedside Program provides non-discretionary travel funds for family members of very seriously ill or injured Navy personnel in
accordance with Title 37 USC 481h, DoD Instruction 1300.18, Military Personnel Casualty Matters, Policies, and Procedures, and Joint Travel Regulation, § 7315.

Medals and Awards: The Medals and Awards program provides service records support for medals and decorations for United States Navy, United States Marines, and United
States Coast Guard. PERS-312B provides and verifies record information; issues replacement awards/decorations, including adjudication and/or replacement of Purple Heart
medals and certificates for Navy service members separated prior to 1996; responds to congressional requests for information/medals replacement on behalf of constituents; and
provides funding to the National Personnel Records Center (NPRC) for medals/awards research and entitlement determinations for records stored at NPRC (paper/microfiche 1995
and prior).

Navy Retention and Monitoring System (NRMS) (Legacy IT System): NRMS is a web-enabled business intelligence tool within NMCI for staff and Fleet users to compute a
wide array of tasks related to Active/Reserve standardized reenlistment, attrition, Perform to Serve statistics, and reserve affiliation statistics. NRMS provides standardized
retention reports for every unit identification code (UIC) in the Navy to facilitate the quarterly Honor Roll and annual Fleet Retention Excellence Award programs. NRMS
provides ad hoc capability to respond to Congressional / OSD / SECNAV / CNP / OPNAV /ECM queries for reenlistment and attrition analysis, as well as, assisting in determining
the military skills most needing Selective Reenlistment Bonus funding.

Temporary Disability Retired List: This program enables Sailors with disabilities, as determined by the Secretary of Navy's Physical Evaluation Board (PEB), to receive
medical coverage for family members and self, until medical conditions stabilize, up to a five (5) year period. Title 10 USC 1210 requires physical exams be given every 18
months, (up to 3 exams per member at 18th, 36th and 54th month intervals), and provides travel/transportation allowances.

                                                                                                                                                             Exhibit OP-5, 4A4M
         629                                                                                                                                                       (Page 3 of 16)
                                                                                 Department of the Navy
                                                                        FY 2026 President's Budget Submission
                                                                           Operation and Maintenance, Navy
                                                              Budget Activity: Administration and Servicewide Activities
                                                                          Activity Group: Servicewide Support
                                                         Detail by Subactivity Group: Military Manpower and Personnel Mgt
Models: The Models program provides contract support funding to maintain, update, and provide training on analytical tools and models used by N1, Naval Personnel Command
(NPC), and other MyNavy HR organizations. The primary tools funded include the Navy Enlisted Manning Model (NEMMo), the sole long-term manning projection tool within
MyNavy HR; the Digital Dashboard, which provides monthly snapshots of enlisted manning metrics; the Billet Analysis Tool, which provides manpower impacts resulting from
proposed Force Structure changes; and the Officer Personnel Information System, which provides metrics and trends for officer inventory. The program also provides funding for
contractor analysts both on and off site. The objective is to ensure that MPTE leadership is provided timely and accurate information upon which to base decisions.

Mortuary Affairs: The Mortuary Affairs program covers all mortuary expenses for the Nation's fallen Sailors and Marines as directed by 10 United States Code. These services
include the preparation, transportation and subsequent burial of remains, cost of additional supplies and services related to the funeral, and remains escort travel. Services are
provided by contracted mortuaries or Navy morticians directly. This support is in compliance with Title 10 USC, 1481-1488, 1490, 1491; DoDD 1300.22; DoDI 1300.15 and
DoDI 1300.18.

Equal Opportunity: The Navy Equal Opportunity program supports the US Navy's commitment to diversity and equality of opportunity and treatment. The Equal Opportunity
office provides policy/guidance and oversight in support of mission readiness/operational effectiveness. The Navy's Diversity office seeks accessions of well qualified candidates
from under-represented markets and undertakes efforts to retain its diverse talent.

Financial Audit Sustainment and Financial Improvement Plan: The Financial Audit Sustainment program works to improve the Navy's financial processes, controls, and
information to attain a favorable financial audit. The Financial Improvement and Audit Readiness (FIAR) Plan outlines the strategy, priorities and methodology for achieving
these objectives.

International Cooperative Administrative Support Services (ICASS): ICASS is an Interagency Program Administered by the U. S. Department of State. ICASS and the
Personnel Exchange Program (PEP) supports the CNO'S Maritime Strategy by building partnerships through personnel exchanges with our closest allies. The personnel exchanges
allow the USN to influence future leaders of partner navies and help model USN officers ready to meet the global challenges of tomorrow.

IT Resources Management: The IT Resources Management program provides funding for all Civilian IT labor for the Bureau of Personnel (BUPERS), Navy Personnel
Command (NPC). The staff supports all BUPERS IT software programs used internally for business processes and externally by Sailors accessing MyNavy HR applications.

Sexual Assault Prevention and Response (SAPR): The Office of Force Resiliency implements and manages the Navy-wide SAPR prevention and response efforts through
integrated programs, policies, leadership development, and oversight activities. The office has shifted from focusing on survivor support to prioritizing the prevention of
destructive behaviors committed by or against Navy service members, their families, and civilians Navy-wide.

Military Manpower and Personnel and Support (Legacy Systems): This category includes funding for forty-one minor programs of Military Manpower Personnel and Support
including, but not limited to, several Information Technology databases for financial management, the MPN Appropriation Justification Book Input and Compilation (MAJIC)
system, Retired Affairs, Military Intelligence billets, the Office of Navy Personnel Surveys, the database of awards, the Brigs' Corrections Management Information Technology
Systems, maintenance of the Fleet Engagement website, Prisoner of War/Missing in Action identification, Defense Casualty Information Technology System funding, assistance
payments to families of severely injured or deceased Sailors, communication expenses, and travel of Headquarters personnel.

Authoritative Data Environment (ADE): The Authoritative Data Environment (ADE) is an enterprise information management system that will migrate the existing MyNavy
HR legacy data warehouses into a central data repository that is composed of a data warehouse, data lake, data management tools and an Application Program Interface (API)
Layer. ADE will provide an authoritative data-sharing framework, leveraging scalable and interoperable technologies as well as business intelligence and data analytic capabilities.
                                                                                                                                                           Exhibit OP-5, 4A4M
        630                                                                                                                                                       (Page 4 of 16)
                                                                            Department of the Navy
